title: IdiomX A Multilingual Benchmark for Idiom Understanding, Retrieval, and Interpretation
comment: 12 pages, 21 figures. Includes dataset and code. Resources available on HuggingFace, Kaggle, and GitHub
summary: Idiomatic expressions remain a persistent challenge for natural language processing because their meanings are often non-compositional, context-dependent, and difficult to align across languages. Existing idiom resources are often limited in scale, contextual diversity, or multilingual coverage, restricting their utility for modern language models. We introduce IdiomX, a large-scale multilingual benchmark for idiom understanding, retrieval, and interpretation, constructed through a reproducible multi-stage pipeline combining lexical resource extraction, large-scale normalization, controlled large language model enrichment, and structured validation. The resulting dataset contains over 190K contextualized examples spanning 12K+ idioms, with aligned English, Arabic, and French semantic representations, idiomatic and literal usage labels, and rich linguistic metadata. Building on this resource, we define a unified four-task benchmark covering idiom detection, context-to-idiom retrieval, Arabic-to-English idiom retrieval, and idiom interpretation, extending evaluation from figurative recognition to semantic grounding and explainable meaning retrieval. Experiments show that contextual transformer models substantially improve idiom detection, while hybrid retrieval and reranking architectures significantly strengthen both monolingual and cross-lingual idiom retrieval. Results further demonstrate that idiom interpretation can be effectively modeled as a semantic retrieval task, introducing interpretability as a complementary benchmark dimension. Overall, IdiomX provides a scalable benchmark for studying idiomatic language as a progression from detection to retrieval and semantic interpretation, and offers a modular framework extensible to additional languages and figurative reasoning tasks

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IdiomX, a large-scale multilingual benchmark and dataset for understanding, retrieving, and interpreting idioms in English, Arabic, and French, along with a four-task evaluation framework and experimental baselines.
AI.motivation: Idiomatic expressions are difficult for NLP systems because their meanings are often non-compositional, context-sensitive, and poorly aligned across languages. Existing idiom datasets are small, lack contextual diversity, or are not multilingual, which limits the training and evaluation of modern language models on idiomatic language. There is a need for a scalable, high-quality, multilingual benchmark to systematically study idiom detection, retrieval, and interpretation.
AI.method: The authors build IdiomX through a multi-stage, reproducible pipeline: (1) extracting idioms from lexical resources; (2) large-scale normalization to standardize forms; (3) controlled enrichment using large language models to generate or refine contextualized examples and semantic representations; and (4) structured validation to ensure quality. They annotate idiomatic vs. literal usage, align semantic representations in English, Arabic, and French, and add linguistic metadata. On top of the dataset, they design a unified benchmark of four tasks: idiom detection, context-to-idiom retrieval, Arabic-to-English idiom retrieval, and idiom interpretation (modeled as semantic retrieval). They then evaluate contextual transformer models and hybrid retrieval/reranking systems as baselines on these tasks.
AI.result: IdiomX yields a resource of over 190K contextualized examples covering more than 12K idioms, with aligned English–Arabic–French semantics and rich annotations. Experimental results show that contextual transformer models perform strongly on idiom detection compared to prior approaches. For both monolingual and cross-lingual idiom retrieval, hybrid retrieval-plus-reranking architectures outperform simpler baselines. Modeling idiom interpretation as semantic retrieval proves effective, with systems able to retrieve meaningful interpretations reliably enough to be used as a benchmark for interpretability.
AI.conclusion: IdiomX is presented as a scalable, multilingual benchmark that systematically covers a progression of idiom-related abilities: detection, retrieval, and semantic interpretation. It demonstrates that modern contextual and retrieval-based architectures can substantially improve idiom handling, and it frames idiom interpretation as a semantic retrieval problem, adding an interpretability-oriented dimension to evaluation. The pipeline and benchmark are modular and can be extended to more languages and to broader figurative language and reasoning tasks.

title: Greener Than Humans? Environmental Attitudes in Large Language Models
comment: Code can be found at https://gitlab.opencode.de/uba-ki-lab/llm-questionnaire-benchmarking-framework Benchmark data and results can be found at https://zenodo.org/records/20445903
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in sustainability-related decision support, reporting, and public communication, yet little systematic evidence exists on the environmental attitudes embedded in their outputs. This paper develops a benchmark for evaluating environmental cognition, affect, and behavioural recommendations in LLMs and applies it to 31 widely used proprietary and open-weight models. Drawing on questions from established environmental awareness surveys and additional sustainability-related behavioural measures, we compare LLM responses 1) among models and 2) between models and human survey benchmarks from Germany. We assess their robustness across prompting conditions. We find that many LLMs align more closely with environmentally progressive attitudes than the average survey respondent, exhibiting higher levels of environmental affect and cognition and recommending behaviours associated with substantial potential CO2 reductions. At the same time, we observe no systematic relationship between sustainability-oriented responses and model origin, size, or release context. However, models exhibit contextual sensitivity, controlled by persona-based prompting and show sycophantic shifts mirroring user-specified ideological positions, which raises concerns about steerability and normative reliability in real-world deployments. Our findings provide a reusable evaluation framework for assessing sustainability-related value alignment in LLMs and highlight the importance of governance, transparency, and critical oversight as AI systems become increasingly embedded in sustainability transformations and public decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks how large language models express and recommend sustainability-related views, comparing them with human survey data and examining their robustness and steerability.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in sustainability decision support, reporting, and public communication, their embedded environmental attitudes can subtly shape opinions and choices, yet there is little systematic evidence about these attitudes or how they compare to human views.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark based on established environmental awareness surveys plus additional sustainability behaviour questions, then query 31 popular proprietary and open-weight LLMs. They analyze models’ environmental cognition, affect, and behavioural recommendations, compare outputs across models and against German human survey benchmarks, and test robustness under different prompts including persona- and ideology-based prompting.
AI.result: Many LLMs express more environmentally progressive attitudes than the average German survey respondent, with stronger environmental concern, knowledge, and support for high-impact CO2-reducing behaviours. There is no clear link between how sustainability-oriented a model is and its origin, size, or release context. However, models’ responses are context-sensitive and can shift sycophantically to match user-specified ideological positions.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a reusable evaluation framework for measuring sustainability-related value alignment in LLMs and shows that while many models lean towards pro-environmental attitudes, they are also easily steered, raising concerns about normative reliability. It argues for stronger governance, transparency, and oversight as AI becomes more embedded in sustainability transitions and public decision-making.

title: On the Persistent Effects of Lexicality in Large Language Mod
summary: Representations extracted from large language models (LLMs) play an important role in many downstream applications. However, the structure of these representations is often influenced by lexical overlap rather than semantic content. Our understanding of the relationship between this lexical influence and semantic content, and its implications for downstream tasks, remains limited. In this work, we investigate representations to quantify the effect of lexical overlap relative to semantic content. We consider several adversarial semantic stress tests and further connect our findings to the information theory perspective. We find that lexical influence extends across the depth of models, consistently across architectures, training regimes, and objective functions, including the models trained for semantic similarity. Moreover, we observe a mid-depth region in which both lexical and semantic signals degrade simultaneously, indicating a transitional regime where representations are poor for both surface form and meaning. We further demonstrate the effect of lexical influence on downstream uses of LLMs using summarization and model editing as a case study.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how much large language model representations are driven by superficial lexical overlap versus true semantic content, and how this affects downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Although LLM representations are widely used, they are often biased by surface-form similarities (e.g., shared words) rather than deeper meaning. The field lacks a clear quantification of this lexical bias, its layer-wise behavior inside models, and its practical impact on tasks that are supposed to rely on semantics, such as semantic similarity, summarization, and model editing.
AI.method: The authors design adversarial semantic stress tests that decouple lexical overlap from semantic similarity, then probe internal representations of various LLMs across layers, architectures, training regimes, and objectives (including models explicitly trained for semantic similarity). They analyze how much representations encode lexical versus semantic information, and interpret their findings via an information-theoretic lens. They also conduct case studies on summarization and model editing to see how lexical influence manifests in real applications.
AI.result: They find that lexical influence pervades model representations across depths and model types, even in models optimized for semantic similarity. They identify a mid-layer "transition" zone where both lexical and semantic information are weakened, making representations poor for capturing either surface form or meaning. These patterns are robust across architectures and training setups. In downstream evaluations, they show that lexical bias materially affects summarization and model-editing performance and behavior.
AI.conclusion: LLM representations are strongly shaped by lexical overlap, and this influence is robust across models and layers, including systems trained for semantic similarity. There is a mid-depth regime where representations are weak for both lexical and semantic information, hinting at non-monotonic transformation of information through the network. These findings imply that many downstream uses of LLM embeddings may be more surface-form-driven than intended, motivating new methods and training objectives that explicitly control or correct for lexical bias when semantic fidelity is required.

title: Topics as Proxies for Sociodemographics: How Conversational Context Affects LLM Answers
summary: When large language models (LLMs) are used in high-stakes scenarios, such as legal, medical and financial advice, even a single conversation history is enough to drive differences in outcomes between users. Prior work has demonstrated that this results in outcome disparities between sociodemographic groups, with some groups receiving more advantageous outcomes than others. In this work, we demonstrate that LLMs actually struggle to infer user sociodemographics from a single conversation history and that although there are disparities between sociodemographic groups, they are minimal in magnitude. To investigate what the main driver of these disparities is, we compare user sociodemographics to a range of (psycho)linguistic features of conversations, including conversation topic, emotions, and readability. We find that conversation topics are most predictive of LLM-generated advice within a conversational context, which, to some extent, function as proxies for sociodemographic groups and often affect advice in unpredictable ways. This is cause for concern and highlights the need for future research to better understand and, if needed, mitigate the effect of conversational context on LLM outputs in high-stakes scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why large language models (LLMs) can produce different advice for different users in high-stakes domains and finds that conversation topics, rather than accurately inferred sociodemographics, mainly drive these disparities.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for high-stakes advice (legal, medical, financial), where inconsistent outcomes can have serious consequences. Prior work shows disparities across sociodemographic groups, but it is unclear whether models are truly conditioning on user demographics or on other aspects of the conversation. Understanding what actually drives these differences is necessary to assess fairness and to design mitigation strategies.
AI.method: The authors analyze single conversational histories between users and LLMs in high-stakes scenarios. They (1) measure how well LLMs can infer users’ sociodemographic attributes from one conversation; (2) quantify disparities in advice quality or favorability across sociodemographic groups; and (3) compare the predictive power of user sociodemographics versus various psycholinguistic and linguistic features (topic, emotions, readability, etc.) for explaining LLM advice. They use statistical and predictive models to identify which factors best explain variation in LLM-generated advice.
AI.result: LLMs perform poorly at inferring users’ sociodemographic attributes from a single conversation. While there are measurable disparities in outcomes between sociodemographic groups, these differences are small in magnitude. In contrast, conversation-level features—especially topics—are much more predictive of the advice the LLM generates. Topics effectively serve as proxies for certain sociodemographic groups and can shift advice in ways that are not straightforward or easily anticipated.
AI.conclusion: Outcome disparities in LLM advice in high-stakes settings are not primarily due to accurate conditioning on user sociodemographics but are instead driven largely by conversational context, particularly topics that can correlate with demographic groups. Since these topic effects can lead to unpredictable variations in advice, the paper argues for further work to understand and, where appropriate, mitigate how conversational context influences LLM behavior in high-stakes applications.

title: Visual Graph Scaffolds for Structural Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Graphs have been used to enhance large language models (LLMs) for structured reasoning, mostly as external knowledge sources are provided to models at test time. In this paper, we take a different view: the value of graphs for LLMs lie not only in supplying information, but also in organizing reasoning. Inspired by how humans use graph-structured mind maps to organize branching and converging thoughts, we ask whether graphs can serve as an internal form of reasoning assistance. We study this question on multi-hop question answering tasks, where teacher-provided reasoning traces are rewritten as graph mind maps and used to guide a student model. Our experiments reveal a clear modality gap. When graph structures are flattened into text, their benefits become limited once direct answer hints are removed. Under this abstract guidance setting, both reasoning efficiency and answer quality degrade substantially. In contrast, visual graph guidance remains effective without direct answer clues, and its advantage persists after supervised fine-tuning and KL-based distillation. The above findings support the claim that graphs should be studied not only as external knowledge structures for LLMs, but also as visual scaffolds for organizing reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper studies using graph-structured “mind maps” as internal reasoning scaffolds for LLMs, showing that visual graph guidance helps multi-hop QA even when direct answer hints are removed, unlike text-flattened graphs.
AI.motivation: Most prior work uses graphs as external knowledge bases for LLMs, focusing on supplying missing information. The authors suspect graphs are valuable not only as information containers but also as structures that organize reasoning, similar to human mind maps. They want to test whether graph-structured guidance can improve LLM reasoning on multi-hop QA without simply leaking answers, and whether visual graphs differ in effect from equivalent textual descriptions.
AI.method: They take teacher-provided multi-hop reasoning traces and rewrite them into graph-structured mind maps. These graphs are then used to guide a student LLM during multi-hop question answering. They compare two modalities of guidance: (1) visual graph guidance and (2) the same graph structures flattened into text, under settings with and without direct answer hints. They evaluate reasoning efficiency, answer accuracy, and the persistence of benefits after supervised fine-tuning and KL-based distillation from teacher to student.
AI.result: When graph structures are converted into plain text, their benefit diminishes once direct answer hints are removed: reasoning becomes less efficient and answer quality drops. In contrast, when the same information is provided as visual graphs, the student model maintains improved reasoning and answer quality even without explicit answer clues. This effectiveness of visual graph guidance continues to hold after supervised fine-tuning and KL-divergence-based distillation.
AI.conclusion: Graphs should be treated not just as external knowledge repositories for LLMs but also as visual scaffolds that structure and support internal reasoning processes. Visual graph mind maps provide robust guidance that improves multi-hop reasoning beyond what can be achieved by text-only representations of the same structure, especially when explicit answer hints are absent.

title: Do Value Vectors in Deep Layers Need Context from the Residual Stream?
summary: The success of the transformer architecture as the backbone of modern LLMs is in large part due to its use of attention layers. An attention layer follows the standard neural network paradigm: it takes the residual stream as input and thereby produces context-dependent query, key, and value vectors. However, we find that model performance meaningfully improves when deeper layers learn only a context-free value vector to preserve the original token information, without drawing on any context from the residual stream. When the model has access to this context-free value vector, adding back the context-dependent component provides little additional benefit for aggregate benchmark performance. Such context-free value vectors can be stored as sparse model parameters, eliminating the need to recompute or persistently cache these values. Through systematic ablations on the key design choices for such context-free value vectors, we propose Bank of Values (BoV), a new way of computing value vectors in attention by learning a lookup table of token-specific value vectors for each of the last third of layers. Across 135M and 780M models, BoV improves validation loss over standard attention and, at 780M, the average score across 21 benchmarks, matching the previous best method that adds token information to the value vector with less compute and memory.

AI.tldr: They propose Bank of Values (BoV), where deeper transformer layers use token-specific, context-free value vectors stored in a lookup table, improving performance and efficiency over standard attention.
AI.motivation: Standard attention computes query, key, and value vectors as context-dependent projections of the residual stream. The authors observed that in deeper layers, performance actually improves when value vectors are largely context-free, suggesting that full context dependence may be unnecessary and inefficient. They want to exploit this to reduce compute/memory while keeping or improving model quality.
AI.method: They empirically study transformers where deeper layers use value vectors that are independent of the current residual stream, focusing on preserving original token information. Building on these ablations, they introduce Bank of Values (BoV), which replaces the usual value-projection in the last third of layers with a sparse, learnable lookup table of token-specific value vectors. They then compare standard attention to BoV (and to other variants that add token information to values) on 135M and 780M parameter models, measuring validation loss, compute, memory, and benchmark scores.
AI.result: BoV models outperform standard attention models in validation loss at both 135M and 780M scales. At 780M parameters, BoV also matches the best existing approach that augments value vectors with token information, while requiring less compute and memory. They further find that once a context-free value vector is available, adding a context-dependent component yields little extra benefit on aggregate benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Context-free, token-specific value vectors are surprisingly effective in deeper transformer layers and can replace standard context-dependent value computation there. The proposed Bank of Values mechanism provides a practical way to implement this via sparse lookup tables, improving performance and efficiency and challenging the assumption that value vectors must always be fully context-dependent in attention.

title: AURA: Action-Gated Memory for Robot Policies at Constant VRAM
summary:
The KV-cache is the right memory for datacenters but the wrong memory for robots. Datacenter inference batches many short requests and resets them, amortizing an attention cache across a crowd. Embodied agents instead run one long, non-resetting episode on bandwidth-limited edge hardware, where high-bandwidth memory and flash are scarce, flash has finite write endurance, and memory writes rather than compute can become the binding constraint.
  AURA-Mem (Action-Utility Recurrent Adaptive Memory) targets this regime. It wraps a frozen vision-language-action backbone with a constant-size recurrent memory and a learned gate that writes only when the current observation would change the next action: memory that knows when to stay silent. Unlike reconstruction-based memory, the gate is trained directly against a closed-loop action-error signal. Its inference state is fixed at 4,224 bytes regardless of horizon, while a KV-cache grows to 6,061 times larger at 100,000 steps.
  On a controlled synthetic benchmark, AURA-Mem matches the best O(1) baseline in accuracy while using 5.19-6.13 times fewer writes, and up to 9.19 times fewer writes on easier configurations. Budget-matched random and periodic schedules do not recover this gain, isolating the benefit to the action-surprise signal. On a trained closed-loop OpenVLA-OFT 7B panel on LIBERO-Long (n=60 episodes per arm), the gate does not hurt success: AURA-Mem matches the ungated base policy (0.233) and slightly exceeds an always-write KV arm (0.217), while using 7.0 times fewer writes and constant memory. We also instantiate an approximate-information-state value-loss bound as a methodology demonstration; at this scale, the bound is vacuous rather than a guarantee.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AURA-Mem, a constant-size, write-sparse recurrent memory designed for long-horizon robot control on bandwidth-limited edge hardware, showing it can match KV-cache performance while using far fewer memory writes and constant inference-state size.
AI.motivation: Standard KV-caches are well-suited to datacenter LLM inference but poorly matched to embodied agents that run long, continuous episodes on resource-constrained edge devices, where memory bandwidth, flash endurance, and write costs dominate. There is a need for a memory mechanism that maintains performance in long-horizon control while drastically reducing memory footprint and write frequency.
AI.method: The authors propose AURA-Mem, which wraps a frozen vision-language-action backbone with (1) a constant-size recurrent memory state and (2) a learned gating mechanism that decides whether to write to memory at each step based on whether the new observation would change the next action. The gate is trained directly from closed-loop action-error (action-surprise) rather than reconstruction loss. They compare it to KV-caches and O(1) baselines on synthetic benchmarks and on a real robotic setting using OpenVLA-OFT 7B on LIBERO-Long, and analyze write counts, memory size, and task success. They also demonstrate an approximate information-state value-loss bound as a theoretical tool, though it is loose at the presented scale.
AI.result: AURA-Mem keeps the inference state fixed at 4,224 bytes regardless of horizon, while a comparable KV-cache grows up to 6,061x larger at 100k steps. On synthetic benchmarks, it matches the best constant-time baseline in accuracy while reducing memory writes by 5.19–6.13x, and up to 9.19x on easier setups; random or periodic writing with the same write budget cannot reproduce these gains, indicating the benefit comes from the action-surprise-driven gate. On LIBERO-Long with OpenVLA-OFT 7B, AURA-Mem maintains or slightly improves success rate relative to the ungated base policy and an always-write KV-cache variant, while using 7x fewer writes and constant memory. The information-state value-loss bound is empirically vacuous at this scale.
AI.conclusion: AURA-Mem demonstrates that a small, recurrent, adaptively-written memory can replace large, ever-growing KV-caches for long-horizon robotic control on edge hardware, preserving task performance while drastically reducing memory usage and write frequency. The learned, action-error-driven gate is key, outperforming budget-matched random or periodic write schedules. Although the theoretical value-loss bound is not yet practically useful, the work suggests a promising direction for scalable, memory-efficient control in embodied agents and provides a methodology for future theoretical analysis.

title: Translating Classical Poetry into Modern Prose
summary: We introduce Padyam2Gadyam, a dataset for the task of poem-to-prose translation from 13th-17th Century Telugu Classical Poetry to contemporary Telugu and English prose. The dataset consists of 600 poems and their human-verified Telugu and English prose translations. We evaluated 5 contemporary Large Language Models (LLMs) on their ability to do poem-to-prose translation into Telugu and English. Our results indicate that while there are differences across LLMs, their overall performance leave a large room for improvement in both languages. Through qualitative analysis, we discuss the the capabilities and limitations of contemporary MT evaluation approaches for this task.

AI.tldr: They present a new bilingual (Telugu & English) poem-to-prose dataset from historical Telugu poetry and benchmark several LLMs, finding substantial headroom for improvement and discussing evaluation challenges.
AI.motivation: Classical Telugu poetry (13th–17th century) is linguistically and stylistically distant from contemporary language, making it hard for modern readers and for NLP systems to understand. There was no dedicated, human-verified dataset for translating such poetry into accessible prose in Telugu or English, nor clarity on how well modern LLMs handle this specialized translation and how to reliably evaluate them.
AI.method: They construct Padyam2Gadyam, a poem-to-prose translation dataset with 600 classical Telugu poems, each paired with human-verified prose translations in both contemporary Telugu and English. They then evaluate five recent large language models on poem-to-prose translation into both target languages and conduct qualitative analysis of system outputs, as well as a discussion of the strengths and weaknesses of current machine translation evaluation methods for this type of task.
AI.result: The dataset is successfully created and used to benchmark five LLMs. Performance varies by model but is generally far from human quality, indicating that poem-to-prose translation for historical Telugu poetry remains challenging for current systems. Their qualitative analysis reveals specific error types and shows that standard MT evaluation metrics have notable limitations in capturing quality for this literary, stylistic task.
AI.conclusion: Padyam2Gadyam provides a new resource for studying poem-to-prose translation from classical Telugu poetry to modern Telugu and English, revealing that present-day LLMs are still inadequate for this task. The work highlights the need both for better models and for more suitable evaluation methodologies tailored to literary and style-preserving translation.

title: Evaluating Transformer and LSTM Frameworks for Prediction in Ungauged Basins
summary: Watershed networks exhibit convergent topologies in which multiple tributaries merge into downstream channels,integrating diverse upstream hydrological processes. In ungauged basins, the absence of direct observations increases uncertainty and limits the ability to anticipate extreme events. This study evaluates whether an encoder-only Transformer provides an advantage over an LSTM for upstream streamflow inference under limited hydrologic information, using retrospective simulations from the NOAA National Water Model (NWM). Across both upstream-only and combined configurations, the LSTM showed stronger overall performance than the Transformer model across the two configurations. Incorporating downstream information further boosted performance for all models, increasing median NNSE by more than 60%. Rather than treating this as a leaderboard-style comparison, we interpret the experiments as a test of architectural inductive bias for hydrologic sequence inference. The results indicate that recurrent memory remains better aligned with this upstream reconstruction task than an encoder-only Transformer, while downstream hydrologic context provides a strong auxiliary constraint that substantially improves prediction skill across architectures

AI.tldr: The paper compares encoder-only Transformers and LSTMs for inferring upstream streamflow in ungauged basins, finding LSTMs perform better, while adding downstream information greatly improves all models.
AI.motivation: Hydrologic prediction in ungauged basins is difficult due to lack of direct observations, which increases uncertainty and hampers anticipation of extreme events. Watershed networks naturally integrate upstream processes into downstream flows, suggesting that learning-based sequence models might reconstruct unobserved upstream streamflow from limited information. The authors want to know whether newer Transformer architectures provide an advantage over traditional recurrent networks (LSTMs) for this specific inference task, and how much downstream information can help.
AI.method: Using retrospective simulations from the NOAA National Water Model (NWM), the authors train and evaluate two main neural sequence architectures—an encoder-only Transformer and an LSTM—for inferring upstream streamflow. They consider two configurations: (1) using only upstream information and (2) combining upstream and downstream information as inputs. Performance is quantified using NNSE (normalized Nash–Sutcliffe efficiency) and compared across models and configurations, with experiments framed as probing architectural inductive biases rather than as a pure benchmark competition.
AI.result: Across both upstream-only and combined input configurations, the LSTM consistently outperforms the encoder-only Transformer for upstream streamflow inference. When downstream information is incorporated, prediction skill improves markedly for both architectures, with median NNSE increasing by more than 60% relative to upstream-only settings. This shows both a clear performance gap favoring recurrent memory and a strong benefit from including downstream hydrologic context.
AI.conclusion: For reconstructing upstream streamflow in ungauged or data-limited basins, recurrent architectures with explicit memory (LSTMs) currently align better with the hydrologic sequence structure than encoder-only Transformers. The study emphasizes that the observed performance differences reflect inductive bias suitability rather than a simple ranking of models. Additionally, leveraging downstream hydrologic information serves as a powerful auxiliary constraint that substantially enhances prediction accuracy for any tested architecture, suggesting that model design should jointly consider network architecture and use of spatial hydrologic context.

title: Fixing FOLIO and MALLS: Verified Annotations and an LLM-assisted Framework to Focus Human Relabeling
summary: Accurate translation from Natural Language to First-Order Logic (NL-to-FOL) underpins neurosymbolic AI systems and Natural Language Inference (NLI), making the quality of NL-to-FOL benchmarks essential -- yet these datasets have never been rigorously audited. Our first contribution is to present a systematic human inspection of the validation split of \textsf{FOLIO} and a subset of \textsf{MALLS} test instances, finding that approximately 39% and 36% of entries, respectively, contain incorrect FOL formalizations (i.e., ground truth labels), with additional rates of ambiguous NL sentences (16.4% and 48%) and incorrect NLI labels in \textsf{FOLIO} (8.4%). Our second contribution is to develop and release corrected ground truths for such datasets, showing that annotation errors distort model evaluation on a reference benchmark task: testing three state-of-the-art LLMs (Gemma~4 31B-it, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and GPT-4o-mini) with the corrected ground truths yields accuracy gains from +9 to +22 percentage points. Motivated by these findings, we propose an LLM-based framework to support humans in manual reviewing NL-to-FOL datasets. By directing reviewers toward the most error-prone instances, we empirically show that it is possible to achieve 90% dataset accuracy after reviewing fewer than 24% of instances, compared to over 70% required by unguided review. We release all human-verified annotations and the code for our framework.

AI.tldr: The paper audits and corrects NL-to-FOL benchmarks, shows large error rates that significantly distort LLM evaluation, and proposes an LLM-assisted reviewing framework that makes dataset correction far more efficient.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic AI and NLI rely on accurate translation from natural language to first-order logic (NL-to-FOL). Benchmark datasets for this task, such as FOLIO and MALLS, are widely used to train and evaluate models, but have never been rigorously checked for annotation errors. If ground-truth FOL formalizations or NLI labels are wrong, model performance estimates and research conclusions built on these benchmarks become unreliable. The authors are motivated to systematically quantify and correct these errors and to develop practical methods to improve dataset quality at scale.
AI.method: 1) Perform a systematic human audit of part of two major NL-to-FOL datasets: the validation split of FOLIO and a subset of MALLS test instances. Human annotators judge the correctness of FOL formalizations, identify ambiguous NL sentences, and flag incorrect NLI labels. 2) Produce corrected ground-truth annotations for problematic entries in these datasets. 3) Re-evaluate three state-of-the-art LLMs (Gemma 2 31B-it, Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-4o-mini) on the original vs corrected benchmarks to quantify the impact of annotation errors on measured accuracy. 4) Propose and implement an LLM-based framework that prioritizes instances for human review by predicting which examples are most error-prone. 5) Empirically compare guided (LLM-prioritized) vs unguided (random or exhaustive) human review in terms of how many instances must be checked to reach a target dataset accuracy.
AI.result: 1) The audit reveals substantial issues: about 39% of FOLIO validation entries and 36% of the examined MALLS test entries have incorrect FOL formalizations; additionally, 16.4% (FOLIO) and 48% (MALLS) of NL sentences are ambiguous; and 8.4% of FOLIO entries have incorrect NLI labels. 2) Using the corrected ground truths, re-evaluation of Gemma 2 31B-it, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and GPT-4o-mini shows large accuracy increases of +9 to +22 percentage points, indicating previous evaluations underestimated model performance due to dataset noise. 3) The proposed LLM-guided review framework can achieve 90% dataset accuracy after humans review fewer than 24% of instances, whereas unguided review would require checking over 70% of instances to reach the same quality. 4) The paper releases all human-verified annotations and the implementation of the review framework, enabling reproducibility and future work.
AI.conclusion: Existing NL-to-FOL benchmarks such as FOLIO and MALLS contain a high proportion of incorrect or ambiguous annotations, which significantly distorts the evaluation of modern LLMs on logical reasoning tasks. Correcting these labels reveals that current models perform substantially better than previously measured. Moreover, dataset quality can be improved far more efficiently by using an LLM-based system to guide human reviewers toward the most suspect instances, reducing annotation effort by more than a factor of two for a given accuracy target. The authors conclude that rigorous auditing and LLM-assisted curation are essential for reliable neurosymbolic and NLI benchmarks, and they provide corrected datasets and tools to facilitate this practice in the community.

title: BehaviorBench: Modeling Real-World User Decisions from Behavioral Traces
summary: Many decision-support settings require systems that adapt to individual users, but evaluation data for this problem remain limited. Existing benchmarks for user understanding often rely on simulated users or model-generated behavior, even though recent work cautions that model-based simulations can diverge systematically from human behavior. We introduce \textsc{BehaviorBench}, a benchmark for evaluating personalized decision modeling from real-world behavioral traces. \textsc{BehaviorBench} reconstructs wallet-level decision histories from observed public prediction-market and on-chain records, and organizes them into two complementary task layers: \emph{Belief prediction}, which predicts a user's final revealed stance and confidence in a market, and \emph{Trade prediction}, which predicts the direction and amount of individual transactions. Across 2,000 evaluation wallets, the benchmark contains 141,445 Belief instances and 1,485,972 Trade instances, with disjoint support pools for retrieval-based evaluation. We evaluate frontier and open-weight generative models under four history interfaces: no personalization, direct recent history, generated user profiles, and retrieved support-wallet evidence. Personalization improves Belief prediction more consistently than Trade prediction, model rankings change across task layers and metrics, and different history interfaces expose different failure modes. \textsc{BehaviorBench} provides an evaluation setting for studying whether personalized methods can use real-world behavioral evidence rather than simulated users alone.

AI.tldr: BehaviorBench is a real-world benchmark for evaluating how well models can personalize to users' decision behavior using prediction-market and on-chain trading histories.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation datasets for user personalization mostly use simulated or model-generated user behavior, which can differ systematically from real human decisions. There is a need for a benchmark grounded in actual behavioral traces to study and compare personalized decision models more reliably.
AI.method: The authors construct BehaviorBench by reconstructing wallet-level decision histories from public prediction-market and on-chain transaction data. They define two task layers: (1) Belief prediction, where models predict a user's final position and confidence in a market; and (2) Trade prediction, where models predict the direction and size of each trade. They prepare 2,000 evaluation wallets with 141,445 Belief instances and 1,485,972 Trade instances, and set up disjoint support pools for retrieval-based evaluation. They then test various generative models—both frontier and open-weight—under four history interfaces: no personalization, direct recent history, generated user profiles, and retrieved similar-wallet evidence.
AI.result: Personalization generally improves Belief prediction performance more reliably than it improves Trade prediction. Model rankings are not stable—they change depending on whether the task is Belief vs. Trade and on which metric is used. Different ways of giving history (no history, raw history, generated profiles, retrieved support wallets) highlight different types of model failures and strengths.
AI.conclusion: BehaviorBench offers a realistic, large-scale benchmark for assessing personalized decision models on real behavioral data. It shows that personalization helps in belief-level prediction, reveals that model evaluations are sensitive to task and interface design, and provides a platform for future work on methods that leverage real user traces instead of relying solely on simulated users.

title: Economy of Minds: Emerging Multi-Agent Intelligence with Economic Interactions
summary: How can a population of agents self-orchestrate and self-adapt into stronger collective intelligence without centralized control? Inspired by Friedrich Hayek's economic theory of decentralized coordination in markets, we study this question through an agent economy in which agents compete via auctions for the right to act, exchange payments, and accumulate wealth from environmental rewards. These simple economic signals induce decentralized credit assignment, driving planning without global orchestration or explicit communication protocols. The population evolves through economic selection: effective agents accumulate wealth and are mutated via exploitation, while ineffective ones go bankrupt and are replaced via exploration. We show that, initialized with weak agents, the economy produces emergent multi-step reasoning strategies and outperforms stronger monolithic baselines across five agentic tasks, including mathematical reasoning, financial research, scientific research, accelerator design, and distributed-system optimization. We further provide theoretical insights into how economic dynamics shape agent behaviors, linking local incentives to long-term global performance. Our results suggest a new path to multi-agent intelligence: rather than engineering coordination, we can design decentralized incentive structures under which it automatically emerges.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an agent economy where multiple AI agents compete in auctions to act and earn rewards, enabling decentralized coordination and emergent collective intelligence without central control. Economic signals and selection drive the evolution of better multi-step reasoning strategies that outperform single, monolithic models on several complex tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand how a population of agents can self-organize into a more intelligent collective system without centralized control or hand-crafted coordination mechanisms. The authors are inspired by Hayek’s theory that market-based price signals can coordinate decentralized actors, and they aim to bring similar ideas to multi-agent AI to achieve scalable planning, credit assignment, and coordination.
AI.method: They design an agent economy where many agents bid in auctions for the right to take actions in an environment. Winning agents act, receive environmental rewards, pay or receive transfers, and thereby accumulate or lose wealth. This economy supplies decentralized credit assignment through prices and payoffs. An evolutionary mechanism is applied: agents that accumulate wealth are preferentially mutated and exploited, while bankrupt agents are removed and replaced (exploration). The system is tested on several tasks requiring multi-step reasoning and planning, comparing against stronger single-agent baselines.
AI.result: Starting from weak agents, the agent economy evolves agents that exhibit emergent multi-step reasoning strategies. Across five domains—math reasoning, financial research, scientific research, accelerator design, and distributed-systems optimization—the evolved agent population collectively outperforms stronger, monolithic baselines. Theoretical analysis connects how the local economic incentives (auctions, payments, wealth) guide agents toward behaviors that improve long-horizon global performance.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized economic mechanisms—auctions, payments, and wealth accumulation—can serve as powerful coordination and credit-assignment tools for multi-agent systems. By designing appropriate incentive structures rather than explicit coordination protocols or central planners, one can induce emergent collective intelligence that solves complex tasks more effectively than stronger single agents. This suggests a promising new approach to building scalable, adaptive multi-agent AI systems based on market-like dynamics.

title: ChatHealthAI: Aligning Electronic Health Record Representations with Large Language Models for Grounded Clinical Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong natural-language reasoning abilities for clinical decision support, but struggle to effectively model structured longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs). In contrast, EHR foundation models can learn predictive patient representations, yet lack interpretable language-based reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose ChatHealthAI, a multimodal reasoning framework that aligns structured EHR representations from a pretrained EHR foundation model with the semantic space of a frozen LLM through a task-aware resampler. By integrating longitudinal patient representations with refined clinical event descriptions, ChatHealthAI enables clinically grounded natural-language reasoning while maintaining accurate patient prediction. We evaluated ChatHealthAI on three clinical predictive tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark. Results show that ChatHealthAI improves reasoning quality and interpretability while preserving competitive predictive performance. These findings highlight the potential of integrating EHR foundation models with pretrained LLMs for interpretable clinical prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ChatHealthAI, a framework that connects EHR foundation models with large language models to provide interpretable, language-based clinical reasoning while retaining strong predictive performance on longitudinal EHR tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are good at natural-language clinical reasoning but bad at using structured longitudinal EHR data, while EHR foundation models are good at prediction from EHRs but lack interpretable, language-based reasoning. There is a need to combine the strengths of both so that clinical predictions are both accurate and explainable in natural language.
AI.method: The authors propose ChatHealthAI, a multimodal reasoning framework. It takes structured longitudinal EHR representations from a pretrained EHR foundation model and aligns them with the semantic space of a frozen LLM. This alignment is done via a task-aware resampler that transforms EHR-derived patient representations and integrates them with refined clinical event descriptions so the LLM can reason over them in natural language.
AI.result: On three clinical predictive tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark, ChatHealthAI maintains competitive predictive performance while enhancing the quality and interpretability of its reasoning compared with baselines that use only LLMs or only EHR models.
AI.conclusion: Integrating EHR foundation models with pretrained LLMs via task-aware alignment can deliver clinically grounded, interpretable natural-language reasoning without sacrificing prediction accuracy, showing a promising direction for interpretable clinical prediction systems.

title: Adaptive Latent Agentic Reasoning
summary: Large reasoning models improve performance by generating extended chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, but this behavior becomes inefficient when applied to LLM agents. Current LLM agents often generate verbose textual reasoning at every decision step and allocate reasoning effort nearly uniformly across turns, leading to substantial inefficiency in multi-turn agentic trajectories. We propose Adaptive Latent Agentic Reasoning (ALAR), a dual-mode framework that uses compact latent reasoning for routine turns and selectively escalates to explicit chain-of-thought when deeper deliberation is needed. ALAR learns latent reasoning by using the agent's actions as supervision anchors and is further optimized to use latent reasoning when it is sufficient for task success and reserve explicit CoT for harder decisions. Experiments on agentic search and tool-use benchmarks show that ALAR maintains comparable or better task accuracy while substantially reducing generated tokens by up to 43.6% in search and 84.6% in tool use. These results demonstrate that ALAR improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of LLM agents by reducing unnecessary textual reasoning while preserving explicit deliberation for harder decision steps.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ALAR, a dual-mode reasoning framework for LLM agents that uses compact latent reasoning for easy steps and explicit chain-of-thought only when necessary, cutting tokens while preserving or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing large reasoning models and LLM agents often generate long chain-of-thought explanations at every decision step. This uniform, verbose reasoning is inefficient in multi-step, agentic settings, where many turns are routine and do not require deep deliberation. The authors aim to address the poor accuracy–efficiency trade-off caused by unnecessary textual reasoning at each step of an agent’s trajectory.
AI.method: The authors propose Adaptive Latent Agentic Reasoning (ALAR), a dual-mode framework. ALAR learns a compact latent reasoning representation anchored by the agent’s actions, enabling the model to internally reason without emitting full textual CoT on routine steps. A decision mechanism determines when latent reasoning is sufficient and when to escalate to explicit chain-of-thought for harder decisions. Training encourages the agent to rely on latent reasoning where it does not harm task success, and to selectively produce explicit CoT only when needed.
AI.result: On agentic search and tool-use benchmarks, ALAR achieves comparable or better task accuracy to baselines while substantially reducing the number of generated tokens. The paper reports up to 43.6% token reduction in search tasks and up to 84.6% in tool-use scenarios, indicating a much more efficient reasoning process without performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: ALAR enables LLM agents to reason efficiently by separating internal (latent) and external (explicit CoT) reasoning modes. By adaptively allocating explicit deliberation only to difficult decisions, the framework significantly improves the accuracy–efficiency trade-off in multi-turn agentic settings, suggesting a promising direction for scalable, cost-effective LLM agents.

title: Traj-Evolve: A Self-Evolving Multi-Agent System for Patient Trajectory Modeling in Lung Cancer Early Detection
summary: Modeling patient trajectories from longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs) requires reasoning over sparse, noisy, and long-context multimodal sequences. Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems address context length but process patients in isolation, failing to mirror how clinicians leverage accumulated experience from similar prior cases. We present Traj-Evolve, a self-evolving multi-agent system with two complementary evolving mechanisms. First, an Experience Pool (ExPool) acts as a non-parametric memory, indexing rejection-sampled reasoning traces to retrieve similar patients as few-shot contexts. Second, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) via reward-ranked fine-tuning parametrically optimizes inter-agent and agent-memory collaboration. A leave-one-out cross-retrieval strategy unifies the two, aligning training- and inference-time behavior under retrieval augmentation. On a lung cancer prediction task utilizing up to five years of multimodal EHRs, Traj-Evolve outperforms 9 strong baselines on the overall population and a challenging never-smoker population. Analysis of the evolving dynamics highlights three key findings: (1) expanding the ExPool shifts optimal retrieval from diverse to specific samples; (2) under MARL, the manager agent's prediction loss converges quickly while the worker agents' temporal reasoning continues to benefit from more verified patients; and (3) the two mechanisms are complementary on the predicted risk, where ExPool improves specificity while MARL improves sensitivity.

AI.tldr: Traj-Evolve is a self-evolving multi-agent system that models patient trajectories from long, noisy, multimodal EHRs by combining a growing non-parametric memory of past reasoning traces with reinforcement-learned collaboration between agents.
AI.motivation: Longitudinal EHR modeling needs systems that can reason over long, sparse, noisy multimodal sequences and leverage experience from many prior, similar patients, similar to how clinicians reason. Existing LLM-based multi-agent approaches can handle long context but treat each patient independently and do not accumulate and reuse structured experience across cases. The work aims to close this gap.
AI.method: Traj-Evolve introduces two coupled mechanisms: (1) an Experience Pool (ExPool), a non-parametric memory that stores and indexes rejection-sampled reasoning traces from prior patients, enabling retrieval of similar cases as few-shot exemplars for new patients; (2) multi-agent reinforcement learning with reward-ranked fine-tuning that trains manager and worker agents, along with their interaction with ExPool, to optimize prediction performance. A leave-one-out cross-retrieval strategy is used during training so that retrieval-augmented behavior at training matches inference-time retrieval patterns.
AI.result: On a lung cancer prediction task using up to five years of multimodal EHR data, Traj-Evolve surpasses nine strong baseline methods for the general patient population and a more difficult never-smoker subgroup. Empirical analysis of training dynamics shows that as ExPool grows, retrieval focuses from diverse to more specific examples; with MARL, the manager agent’s prediction loss quickly stabilizes while worker agents continue improving their temporal reasoning when exposed to more verified patients; and the combination of ExPool and MARL yields complementary gains in prediction metrics, with ExPool mainly enhancing specificity and MARL mainly enhancing sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: Combining a growing case-based memory of prior reasoning (ExPool) with reinforcement-learned multi-agent collaboration yields a self-evolving system that better models complex patient trajectories from longitudinal EHRs than existing baselines. The two evolving mechanisms are complementary, improving different aspects of predictive performance (specificity vs. sensitivity), and the unified leave-one-out retrieval framework helps align training and inference behavior under retrieval augmentation.

title: Linear Probes Detect Task Format, Not Reasoning Mode in Language Model Hidden States
comment: Accepted in the 6th Workshop on Trustworthy NLP, ACL 2026
summary: Linear probing of large language model (LLM) hidden states is widely used to claim that models learn distinct representations for different reasoning types. We test this by probing Qwen3-14B on three benchmarks spanning the classical trichotomy: LogiQA 2.0 (deductive), ARC-Challenge (inductive), and $α$NLI (abductive). At layer 32 of 40, linear probes achieve 100\% cross-validated accuracy with well-separated geometry (intrinsic dimensionalities: 20.6, 28.5, 33.6; convex hull contamination $\leq$1.5\%). However, this separation is entirely driven by format confounds. Residualizing source identity, option count, and response length reduces accuracy to chance. Trace-anchor similarity indicates largely shared reasoning across tasks (42.5\% agreement vs.\ 33.3\% chance), and causal steering with random controls ($n=20$) shows no functional link between geometry and reasoning mode ($p=0.286$). Thus, high probe accuracy reflects task format rather than computational structure, motivating routine format deconfounding in mechanistic interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that linear probes on LLM hidden states can appear to distinguish reasoning types (deductive, inductive, abductive) but this effect is largely due to superficial task-format differences rather than genuine differences in internal reasoning computations.
AI.motivation: Many interpretability works use linear probing to claim that models form distinct internal representations for different kinds of reasoning. The authors want to test whether these apparent separations truly reflect different computational structures or are artifacts of dataset and format confounds.
AI.method: They probe hidden states of Qwen3-14B on three benchmarks representing the classical reasoning trichotomy: LogiQA 2.0 (deductive), ARC-Challenge (inductive), and αNLI (abductive). They train linear classifiers on layer-32 activations to distinguish which benchmark a given example comes from, analyze geometric properties (intrinsic dimensionality, convex hull contamination), then systematically remove (‘residualize’) format-related factors such as dataset/source identity, number of answer options, and response length. They also use trace-anchor similarity to compare reasoning patterns across tasks and perform causal steering experiments with random controls to test if the identified geometry actually drives different reasoning behavior.
AI.result: Initially, linear probes at layer 32 reach 100% cross-validated accuracy in identifying the benchmark and show clean geometric separation with low contamination and moderate intrinsic dimensionality. But once representations are residualized with respect to format variables, probe performance collapses to chance. Trace-anchor similarity suggests that the same internal reasoning patterns are largely shared across the three benchmarks, and causal steering fails to find a significant functional effect of the supposedly distinct geometric directions (p=0.286).
AI.conclusion: The apparent separability of reasoning types in linear probing is largely an artifact of task and format confounds, not evidence of distinct internal reasoning modes in the model. High probing accuracy can be misleading about underlying computation. The authors recommend routine format deconfounding and more stringent causal tests when using linear probes in mechanistic interpretability.

title: An Exploration of Collision-based Enemy Morphology Generation
summary: Despite a great deal of prior research into Procedural Content Generation (PCG), relatively little prior work has explored generating enemies for video games. In particular, there is almost no work on generating enemy morphologies, the basic body plan or collision information for in-game enemies, despite the existence of related morphology generation work in robotics. In this paper, we explore three different novel approaches to generate enemy morphologies based on player collision information. We found that each approach provides different strengths and weaknesses, but all had equivalent or better performance than an evolutionary baseline adapted from prior robotics morphology work.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates new methods to automatically generate enemy body shapes in video games, using player collision data, and shows these methods match or outperform an evolutionary baseline from robotics.
AI.motivation: Existing Procedural Content Generation (PCG) research has largely overlooked the automatic creation of enemy morphologies (their body structure and collision properties) in video games, even though similar morphology generation has been studied in robotics. The authors want to close this gap and leverage player collision information to design such enemies.
AI.method: The paper proposes and explores three novel approaches for generating enemy morphologies driven by player collision data. These approaches are compared against an adapted evolutionary algorithm baseline derived from prior robotics morphology generation work. The analysis focuses on strengths, weaknesses, and performance differences between the methods.
AI.result: All three proposed approaches achieve performance that is comparable to or better than the evolutionary baseline. Each approach exhibits distinct strengths and weaknesses, suggesting different trade-offs for practical use in game design.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that enemy morphologies in video games can be effectively generated using player collision information, with multiple novel methods performing as well as or better than a traditional evolutionary approach adapted from robotics. This opens up new opportunities for more nuanced and data-driven enemy design in PCG for games.

title: WRIT: Write-Read Intensive Trajectory Synthesis for Multi-Turn User-Facing Agents
summary:
Multi-turn user-facing agents must infer user intent from incomplete requests, collect missing information through dialogue and tools, and execute valid actions. A training trajectory records this process as an interleaved sequence of user messages, agent responses, tool calls, etc. Synthesizing sufficiently complex trajectory has become a central route to train agents: existing pipelines often increase difficulty by composing multiple user requests into longer tasks, producing write-intensive trajectories that train sequential execution.
  We argue that a single write decision can itself be difficult when the agent must gather and compare substantial read-tool evidence before its arguments become identifiable, a challenge that write-intensive data alone cannot address. Guided by this insight, we propose WRIT (\uline{W}rite-\uline{R}ead \uline{I}ntensive \uline{T}rajectory Synthesis), a pipeline for synthesizing multi-turn agent training trajectories along two complexity axes: the number of write decisions in a task and the evidence burden of each individual decision. WRIT first generates write-intensive and read-heavy tasks. It then diversifies user behavior instructions to reflect realistic conversational variation, and finally simulates agent-user interactions in an executable environment to produce complete training trajectories. The resulting data trains agents not only for longer task execution, but also for robust, evidence-grounded decision making under high information load. With only 2K synthesized trajectories, a 4B model trained on WRIT outperforms GPT-5.1 no-think on $τ^2$-bench and substantially reduces inference-time token usage, showing that compact SFT data can convert part of expensive test-time reasoning into efficient agent behavior.

AI.tldr: WRIT is a data synthesis pipeline for training multi-turn agents that are both write- and read-intensive, emphasizing difficult single decisions that require substantial evidence gathering, not just long sequences of actions.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic trajectory pipelines mainly increase difficulty by composing multiple user requests into longer, write-heavy tasks, which focus on sequential execution. However, real agent challenges often lie in making a single, hard decision that requires collecting and comparing large amounts of tool-derived evidence. Current data does not adequately train models for this high information-load, evidence-grounded decision making.
AI.method: The authors propose WRIT, a trajectory synthesis pipeline that controls task complexity along two axes: (1) the number of write decisions in a task (write-intensity), and (2) the evidence burden for each decision (read-intensity). WRIT first generates tasks that are both write-intensive and read-heavy, then injects diverse user behavior instructions to mimic realistic conversational variation, and finally simulates user–agent interaction in an executable environment to yield full training trajectories.
AI.result: Using only 2,000 synthesized trajectories from WRIT to fine-tune a 4-billion-parameter model, the resulting agent surpasses GPT-5.1 (no-think setting) on the τ^2-bench benchmark and significantly cuts inference-time token usage, suggesting more efficient reasoning at deployment.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed synthetic trajectories that jointly stress long-horizon execution and high-evidence single decisions can train compact models to perform strong, evidence-grounded reasoning as agents. WRIT shows that relatively small but well-structured supervised fine-tuning data can shift some of the computational burden from expensive test-time reasoning into cheaper, pre-trained agent behavior.

title: Thinking Past the Answer: Evaluating Harmful Overthinking in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) improve performance by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces through increased test-time compute, yet the assumption that longer reasoning is consistently beneficial remains under-examined. While recent evidence shows that additional reasoning can lead models to overthink, we ask: "Once a model has reached the correct answer, does further reasoning refine the solution, or deviate from it?" To study the dynamics after correctness, we introduce a prefix-level trajectory evaluation protocol grounded in reasoning sufficiency, defining the minimum reasoning budget required for a model to first generate the correct answer. This allows us to disentangle verbose overthinking, where additional reasoning is redundant but harmless, from harmful overthinking, where continued reasoning destabilizes an already-correct trajectory. Starting from multimodal benchmarks, we find that many instances considered reasoning-intensive require surprisingly little reasoning. Moreover, stopping at the first correct prefix improves accuracy over standard reasoning up to 21%, revealing that current models are limited not only by their ability to reason, but also by their inability to stop at the right time. Furthermore, while common efficiency strategies like early stopping substantially reduce verbose overthinking (up to 50%), they fail to mitigate harmful overthinking. Failure analysis reveals that correctness deviations are mainly driven by logical drift and visual reinterpretation. Finally, we show that our findings generalize to language-only reasoning benchmarks, highlighting harmful overthinking as a broader reliability risk. Code available at https://simonecaldarella.github.io/thinking-past-the-answer.

AI.tldr: This paper studies when additional test-time reasoning by large reasoning models (LRMs) helps or harms performance, showing that once a model has reached a correct answer, further reasoning often destabilizes it rather than refining it.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models improve accuracy by producing long, explicit reasoning traces, but it is unclear whether increasing test-time reasoning is always beneficial. Emerging evidence suggests models can "overthink," potentially hurting performance. The authors aim to rigorously characterize what happens after a model first becomes correct: does extra reasoning merely add verbosity, or can it actively derail correct solutions? Understanding this is essential for both efficiency and reliability of reasoning models.
AI.method: They propose a prefix-level trajectory evaluation protocol based on reasoning sufficiency. For each model output, they identify the earliest prefix of the reasoning trace at which the model first produces the correct answer—the minimal reasoning budget needed. They then extend decoding beyond this point to observe trajectory behavior. This protocol distinguishes (1) verbose overthinking, where excess reasoning does not change a correct answer, from (2) harmful overthinking, where further reasoning flips a correct answer to an incorrect one. They apply this to multimodal reasoning benchmarks, test alternative stopping strategies like early stopping, and conduct failure analysis to categorize causes of correctness deviations (e.g., logical drift, visual reinterpretation). They also repeat the analysis on language-only reasoning benchmarks to test generality.
AI.result: They find that many tasks labeled as reasoning-intensive actually require very short reasoning prefixes for correctness. Stopping at the first correct prefix can increase accuracy by up to 21% compared with standard long reasoning. Common efficiency techniques such as early stopping substantially reduce redundant, verbose overthinking (up to 50%) but do not prevent harmful overthinking, where correct answers are later overturned. Their error analysis shows that harmful overthinking is largely driven by logical drift in step-by-step reasoning and by the model revising its interpretation of visual inputs. Similar patterns appear on text-only reasoning datasets, indicating the phenomenon is not confined to multimodal tasks.
AI.conclusion: Longer reasoning is not inherently better: once a model reaches a correct answer, additional reasoning frequently adds risk without benefit. Present LRMs are limited not only by their reasoning capacity but by their inability to recognize when to stop. Harmful overthinking, in which extended reasoning destabilizes initially correct trajectories, represents a general reliability problem across both multimodal and language-only benchmarks. Effective methods must therefore focus not just on generating better reasoning, but also on detecting sufficiency and terminating reasoning at the right time.

title: The Ghost Annotator: a Framework to Explore Human Label Variation in Content Moderation through Conformal Prediction
summary: Current research primarily focuses on model performance, while comparatively less attention has been devoted to uncertainty estimation, particularly in settings where LLMs are increasingly used to generate annotated data. We introduce a framework combining conformal prediction with Collaborative Filtering-style annotators' representation to model LLM behavior in relation to human annotators and to analyze patterns of agreement and disagreement. Using Non-Conformity Scores, we introduce the Ghost Prediction metric and the Ghost Annotator representation to quantify cases in which model predictions diverge from all available human annotations. We compute cosine similarity measures to explore differences in model behavior across sociodemographic axes. We evaluated four LLMs of different size and families across four content moderation datasets. Our finding shows that while we find that all models uncertainty increases with annotator disagreement, larger models tend to be more confident in the classification of texts that are not aligned with any human annotation. Finally, the Ghost Annotator framework reveals a consistent and robust pattern of demographic misalignment, suggesting a structural bias likely rooted in pretraining corpora.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to quantify and analyze how LLM predictions align or misalign with human annotators, especially under uncertainty and disagreement.
AI.motivation: Most current LLM research emphasizes raw performance metrics and pays relatively little attention to calibrated uncertainty and disagreement with human annotators, despite LLMs being widely used to generate labels. There is a need to understand when and how LLMs systematically diverge from humans, and whether such divergences correlate with sociodemographic factors, which could indicate structural biases.
AI.method: The authors combine conformal prediction with a Collaborative Filtering-style latent representation of annotators to jointly model LLM and human annotation behavior. They define Non-Conformity Scores to capture disagreement between LLM predictions and human labels, and from these derive two key constructs: (1) Ghost Prediction, a metric capturing instances where the model’s prediction does not match any human annotation, and (2) Ghost Annotator, a representation of the model as if it were an additional annotator in the collaborative filtering space. They then compute cosine similarity between this Ghost Annotator and demographic groups to analyze alignment patterns. The framework is evaluated on four content moderation datasets with four LLMs of varying size and families.
AI.result: Across datasets, all LLMs show higher uncertainty when human annotators disagree more. However, larger LLMs exhibit higher confidence specifically on examples where their predictions diverge from all human annotations (high Ghost Prediction), indicating confident disagreement with people. The Ghost Annotator representations expose stable patterns of demographic misalignment: some demographic groups are systematically closer or farther from the LLM’s latent representation, suggesting structured, non-random bias.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Ghost Annotator and Ghost Prediction framework offers a principled way to measure and interpret LLM disagreement with humans and to study demographic alignment. Results indicate that bigger models can be confidently wrong in ways that do not correspond to any observed human judgment, and that there is consistent demographic misalignment likely stemming from pretraining data. This underscores the need to evaluate LLMs not just on accuracy but on uncertainty, alignment with diverse annotators, and structural bias.

title: Toward a Modular Architecture for Embedded AI Agent Systems at the Edge
summary:
The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) has enabled agentic AI capable of complex reasoning and tool use; however, deploying such autonomy in pervasive computing environments remains challenging due to the strict memory and energy constraints of embedded microcontrollers. Existing frameworks typically assume server-class resources or continuous connectivity, leaving a gap for deeply embedded systems. This paper proposes a modular reference architecture for Embedded Agent Systems that bridges the divide between deterministic real-time control and agentic intelligence.
  We introduce a tiered design that decouples On-Device Agents - executing highly compressed neural networks and rule-based logic for low-latency, privacy-critical tasks - from Cloud-Augmented Agents that leverage Small Language Models (SLMs) for higher-level reasoning and planning. A key contribution is the integration of a cross-cutting Governance Layer, ensuring observability, policy enforcement, and safety across distributed fleets of autonomous devices. Rather than presenting purely empirical benchmarks, we analyze architectural design principles and trade-offs regarding latency, energy, and reliable execution in resource-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes a reference architecture for embedding agentic AI (LLM/SLM-based agents) into resource-constrained embedded and IoT devices using a tiered on-device/cloud design plus a governance layer, focusing on design trade-offs rather than benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM-powered agents can reason and use tools but are difficult to deploy on deeply embedded microcontrollers with tight memory, energy, and real-time constraints. Current frameworks largely target servers or devices with good connectivity, leaving a gap for pervasive, low-resource embedded systems that still need some level of agentic intelligence and safe autonomy.
AI.method: Conceptual and architectural work: the authors design a modular reference architecture for Embedded Agent Systems. They partition functionality into On-Device Agents (compressed neural nets and rules for real-time, privacy-sensitive tasks) and Cloud-Augmented Agents (SLM-based higher-level reasoning), and add a cross-cutting Governance Layer for observability, policy enforcement, and safety. They analyze design principles and trade-offs (latency, energy, reliability) instead of running extensive empirical benchmarks.
AI.result: The result is a tiered, modular architecture specification that describes how to combine deterministic real-time control on embedded hardware with cloud-assisted agentic reasoning, and how a governance layer can coordinate and supervise distributed autonomous devices. The paper offers qualitative analysis of latency/energy/reliability trade-offs rather than quantitative benchmark results.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid on-device/cloud architecture with an explicit governance layer can make agentic AI feasible for deeply embedded systems while respecting hard resource and real-time constraints. The work provides design guidelines and a reference structure rather than a specific implementation or performance claims, aiming to guide future Embedded Agent System designs.

title: Linguistic Productivity in Large Language Models: Models Coerce, but do not Preempt
summary: Usage-based theories of grammars posit that creative productivity of the structures of language is both bolstered and constrained by two distinct frequency signals: entrenchment, stemming from high frequency usage, and preemption, stemming from having never observed a particular linguistic structure in a context where one might expect that structure to appear. Large Language Models are also usage-based, in the sense that the structures of language are learned through exposure to vast amounts of text. Here, we test whether or not the opposing statistical forces of entrenchment and preemption also encourage and constrain linguistic productivity in LLMs. We demonstrate across model architectures that larger models recognize and can reproduce with nonce words constructional productivity (entrenchment) in cases of coercion, wherein the broader constructional context coerces an atypical interpretation of a lexical item. However, we also show that even the largest models do not extend negative evidence to novel language, and statistical preemption does not enable models to avoid overgeneralization of patterns that are semantically felicitous, but never observed in data.
