AI.tldr: The paper tests whether large language models (LLMs) show usage-based grammatical behavior, specifically entrenchment and preemption, in their linguistic productivity.
AI.motivation: Usage-based theories argue that grammar is shaped by frequency: frequent patterns become entrenched and productive, while the absence of certain patterns in contexts where they are expected (preemption) constrains overgeneralization. Since LLMs also learn language from large-scale usage data, the authors want to know whether these same frequency-driven mechanisms—entrenchment and preemption—emerge in LLMs’ behavior.
AI.method: The authors probe multiple LLM architectures of varying sizes with tasks involving nonce (novel) words embedded in constructions that require coercion—contexts where a word must take on an atypical interpretation to fit the construction. They analyze whether models can generalize constructional patterns to these nonce items (evidence of entrenchment) and whether they avoid overgeneralizing patterns that, while semantically plausible, never actually occur in training data (testing for preemption).
AI.result: They find that larger LLMs can detect and reproduce constructional productivity with nonce words in coercion contexts, indicating that they capture entrenchment-like effects. However, even the largest models fail to exhibit negative evidence effects: they do not use the absence of certain patterns in the data (statistical preemption) to refrain from generating overgeneralized yet semantically acceptable but unattested constructions.
AI.conclusion: LLMs show usage-based learning in terms of positive frequency effects (entrenchment) that support productive use of constructions with novel items, but they lack the complementary constraining force of preemption. As a result, LLMs overgeneralize patterns beyond what human-like usage-based theories would predict, suggesting a key difference between human grammatical learning and current LLM training dynamics.

title: Don't Gamble, GAMBLe: An Analytical Framework for AI-Driven Research Systems
comment: Preprint. 21 pages (10 main, 11 appendix). 6 figures (2 in main, 4 in appendix)
summary: AI-Driven Research Systems (ADRS) -- systems coupling LLMs with automated evaluation to discover algorithms, proofs, and designs -- are being optimized and adopted across domains, but the tools to analyze them have not kept pace. ADRS performance depends on component interactions that are poorly understood, expensive to explore, and (as we show) not well captured by standard convergence guarantees. These guarantees rely on structural assumptions that do not hold under the ADRS process we formalize. We introduce GAMBLe, a framework that decomposes ADRS behavior into four parameters (generator $G$, assessor $\mathcal{A}$, discovery mechanism $\mathcal{M}$, budget $B$) and one compositional object, the effective landscape $L_{\text{eff}} = \mathcal{A} \circ G$, which reveals that distinct generator-assessor pairs induce structurally different per-problem optimization landscapes. We exercise the framework on 760+ replicated runs (>46,000 iterations) spanning generators from single LLMs to dynamically-adaptive ensembles, mechanisms from greedy selection to co-evolutionary meta-search, and three NP-hard problems whose assessors range from continuous scoring to cliff functions. The experiments reveal no total ordering of generators or mechanisms: frontier models can underperform open-source alternatives and the simplest mechanism sometimes outperforms state-of-the-art meta-search. Results show that even under limited budgets (60 iterations per run), the right component choices can improve performance by 13-67% and search efficiency by 6-39x.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GAMBLe, a framework to analyze AI-Driven Research Systems (ADRS) that combine LLM generators, assessors, and search mechanisms, showing that component interactions shape effective optimization landscapes and that simple, well-chosen components can outperform more advanced ones.
AI.motivation: AI-Driven Research Systems that use LLMs plus automated evaluation are rapidly being deployed to discover algorithms, proofs, and designs, but we lack good tools to understand and predict their behavior. Existing convergence guarantees assume structural properties that do not actually hold for real ADRS workflows, and evaluating all component combinations is expensive. The authors aim to build a principled framework that explains how different generators, assessors, and discovery mechanisms interact and when one setup will outperform another.
AI.method: The authors formalize the ADRS process and show why standard convergence assumptions break down in this setting. They introduce GAMBLe, which decomposes an ADRS into four explicit parameters: the generator G (e.g., an LLM or ensemble), the assessor A (the evaluation/scoring function), the discovery/search mechanism M (e.g., greedy, evolutionary, or meta-search), and the budget B (number of iterations). They define the effective landscape L_eff = A ∘ G as the composition of generator and assessor, which determines how candidate solutions are distributed over scores. Using this framework, they run over 760 replicated experiments (>46,000 iterations) across multiple generators (single LLMs, adaptive ensembles), mechanisms (greedy selection, co-evolutionary meta-search), and three NP-hard problems with different assessor structures (smooth vs. cliff-like). They then compare performance and search efficiency across these configurations.
AI.result: The experiments show that ADRS behavior cannot be captured by a simple ranking of generators or mechanisms: there is no universal best model or search strategy. Frontier LLMs are sometimes worse than smaller or open-source models, and sophisticated meta-search can be outperformed by simple greedy mechanisms depending on the effective landscape. Across the three NP-hard tasks and under tight iteration budgets (about 60 iterations per run), appropriate choices of G, A, and M lead to substantial gains: 13–67% better performance and 6–39x higher search efficiency compared to naive or mismatched configurations.
AI.conclusion: ADRS performance is governed by the interaction between generators, assessors, and discovery mechanisms, which together define a task-specific effective landscape. Standard convergence guarantees are misleading because they ignore these structural details. The GAMBLe framework provides a way to reason about and empirically probe these interactions, revealing that there is no one-size-fits-all choice of LLM or search algorithm. Careful, problem-specific component design—even using simple mechanisms and non-frontier models—can significantly improve both solution quality and efficiency under realistic compute budgets.

title: Fast-dLLM++: Fréchet Profile Decoding for Faster Diffusion LLM Inference
comment: Initial version accepted at Workshop on Structured Probabilistic Inference & Generative Modeling, ICML 2026
summary: Diffusion large language models promise parallel token generation, yet inference remains bottlenecked by deciding which masked tokens can be safely committed together. Fast-dLLM addressed this with KV caching and confidence-guided parallel decoding, but its decoding theory uses a homogeneous high-confidence assumption that effectively reduces each candidate set to its weakest selected token. We argue that this leaves speed on the table because real decoding steps exhibit heterogeneous confidence profiles. We propose \textbf{Fast-dLLM++}, a training-free extension that introduces \emph{Fréchet profile decoding}: selecting parallel commit sets from the full sorted confidence profile rather than a single worst-case confidence. The resulting rule is a heterogeneous-confidence generalization of Fast-dLLM's factor selector and it recovers the previous rule exactly in the equal-confidence case and adds a provable \emph{heterogeneity bonus} when the selected tokens have uneven confidences. Fast-dLLM++ leaves the model, diffusion process, and cache implementation entirely unchanged, making it a drop-in replacement for existing Fast-dLLM decoding. Experiments on GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, and MBPP with the LLaDA-8B model show that the theoretical improvement translates directly into empirical gains: profile-aware selection improves the accuracy--throughput frontier by exploiting safe parallelism that weakest-token rules miss, achieving up to 37\% higher throughput at comparable accuracy. Our anonymous code release is at https://github.com/Ringo-Star/FastdLLM_plusplus.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Fast-dLLM++, a training-free decoding method for diffusion LLMs that more efficiently commits multiple tokens in parallel by using the full confidence profile instead of only the lowest-confidence token, yielding higher throughput at similar accuracy.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs can in principle generate multiple tokens in parallel, but real-world inference is slowed because it is unclear which subset of predicted tokens can be safely committed together without harming accuracy. Existing Fast-dLLM uses a conservative rule based on a homogeneous high-confidence assumption that effectively lets the weakest token dominate the decision, underutilizing potential parallelism. The authors want to exploit heterogeneous token confidences to increase decoding speed without sacrificing accuracy and without retraining models.
AI.method: They propose Fast-dLLM++, a training-free extension to Fast-dLLM. It introduces Fréchet profile decoding, which selects commit sets using the entire sorted confidence profile of candidate tokens rather than a single worst-case confidence value. Mathematically, they generalize Fast-dLLM's factor selector to a heterogeneous-confidence setting: when tokens have varying confidences, the rule provides a provable heterogeneity bonus that allows more tokens to be safely committed in parallel. The underlying diffusion model, process, and KV-cache implementation remain unchanged so the method can act as a drop-in replacement for Fast-dLLM decoding.
AI.result: On several benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, MBPP) using the LLaDA-8B model, Fast-dLLM++ improves the accuracy–throughput trade-off compared with Fast-dLLM. The profile-aware selection rule consistently finds more safe parallelism, yielding up to 37% higher throughput at comparable accuracy levels, confirming that theoretical gains from exploiting heterogeneous confidence profiles translate into real inference speedups.
AI.conclusion: Fast-dLLM++ demonstrates that diffusion LLM decoding can be sped up further by leveraging full token confidence profiles instead of worst-token heuristics. The method is training-free, model-agnostic, and fully compatible with existing Fast-dLLM infrastructures, making it a practical drop-in upgrade that recovers the old rule in equal-confidence cases and adds a provable advantage when confidences are heterogeneous. This leads to substantially better throughput without notable accuracy loss across diverse reasoning and coding benchmarks.

title: When Helping Hurts and How to Fix It: Multi-Agent Debate for Data Cleaning
comment: 27 pages, 4 figures, 12 tables. Includes appendix with full experimental results, prompt templates, and dataset statistics
summary: When does multi-agent debate help data cleaning, and when does it hurt? Across three benchmarks, four model families, and over 6,000 task-condition pairs, we find debate's effect reverses sign: it degrades generation across all four models (-1.6 to -15.5pp) through critique-induced confusion (CIC), hallucinated Critic feedback that the Generator accepts uncritically, yet improves error detection (+27.4pp F1, d=1.0). We derive a debate benefit condition: debate helps when the probability of rescuing a wrong output (Critic verification odds weighted by fixability) exceeds the probability of destroying a correct one. A factorial experiment proves adversarial separation is essential: self-verification with identical tools fails, while a separate Critic with code-execution grounding and evidence-gated generation produces the first debate configuration to significantly exceed single-agent on a generative task (+5.3pp, p<0.05). The condition correctly predicts all nine task types and generalizes with zero false positives across 19 published comparisons in seven domains.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when multi-agent debate among LLMs helps or harms data-cleaning tasks, finding that it often hurts generation via critique-induced confusion but strongly helps error detection, and proposes a quantitative condition that predicts when debate is beneficial.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent debate is widely proposed to improve LLM reliability, but empirical results are mixed and poorly understood, especially for practical tasks like data cleaning. The authors aim to systematically characterize when debate helps versus hurts, identify failure modes, and design a configuration that reliably outperforms single-agent baselines.
AI.method: They run large-scale experiments across three data-cleaning benchmarks, four model families, and over 6,000 task-condition pairs comparing single-agent vs. debate setups. They analyze a failure mode they call critique-induced confusion, where a Generator model uncritically accepts hallucinated feedback from a Critic. They then derive a formal debate benefit condition based on probabilities of rescuing wrong outputs vs. corrupting correct ones, and test it via factorial experiments altering properties like adversarial separation, tool access, and evidence gating in the Critic.
AI.result: They find that standard debate generally degrades generative cleaning quality across all four models (−1.6 to −15.5 percentage points) due to critique-induced confusion, but it substantially improves pure error-detection performance (+27.4 F1, effect size d=1.0). The debate benefit condition accurately predicts when debate helps, across nine task types and 19 published comparisons in seven domains, with zero false positives. A key experimental result is that self-verification with identical tools fails to beat single-agent performance, whereas an adversarially separated Critic with code execution and evidence-gated generation yields a debate setup that significantly improves generative performance (+5.3pp, p<0.05).
AI.conclusion: Debate is not universally helpful: in generative data-cleaning tasks, naive debate can systematically harm performance via critique-induced confusion, even while it strongly boosts error detection. Debate helps only when the odds of correctly fixing wrong answers outweigh the risk of corrupting correct ones, a condition that can be formalized and empirically validated. Achieving reliable benefits requires strong adversarial separation and better grounding for the Critic, suggesting that future debate systems should be designed around these principles rather than assuming that more critique is always beneficial.

title: EURO-5K: When Does Domain Pretraining Matter? Benchmarking Transformers for EU Reporting Obligation Extraction
summary: Extracting reporting obligations from EU legislation is critical for assessing and reducing regulatory reporting burden. However, distinguishing reporting requirements from structurally similar provisions requires specialised legal understanding. Current legal NLP methods lack specialised datasets with clear guidelines and comparative evaluation of extraction paradigms and domain adaptation strategies. We curate EURO-5K, a corpus of sentence-level reporting obligations and challenging negative examples from 136 EU legislative acts. On this dataset, we train and compare discriminative token-classification models (BERT-style) and generative span-extraction models (LLMs), evaluating both full fine-tuning and parameter-efficient QLoRA against baselines (pattern and dependency-based extraction, few-shot prompting). Results show that fully fine-tuned generic and legal BERT models achieve similar performance (0.89 F1), while fine-tuned LLMs match encoder accuracy for sentence-level extraction. Legal pretraining offers only small gains for generative models. In contrast, it is clearly beneficial when adaptation capacity is constrained, as parameter-efficient tuning of Legal-BERT outperforms its generic counterpart. Learning curve analysis demonstrates that legal pretraining accelerates early learning with minimal data. All approaches converge around 3K samples with diminishing returns thereafter, validating dataset sufficiency. Cross-dataset evaluation on two external regulatory corpora shows that our models behave as specialised reporting obligation extractors rather than generic regulatory classifiers. We release EURO-5K, trained models, and an interactive demo with explainability visualizations and structured RDF export. These demonstrate that both paradigms and parameter-efficient training provide practical tools for regulatory compliance automation.

AI.tldr: EURO-5K is a new dataset and benchmark for extracting reporting obligations from EU laws, showing that both BERT-style and LLM-based models can accurately identify such obligations and that legal pretraining mainly helps in low-data or parameter-efficient settings.
AI.motivation: Organizations face a heavy regulatory reporting burden, but automatically identifying which parts of EU legislation impose reporting duties is hard. Existing legal NLP lacks a specialised, clearly annotated dataset for reporting obligations, as well as a systematic comparison of extraction paradigms (discriminative vs. generative) and adaptation strategies (full vs. parameter-efficient fine-tuning, legal vs. generic pretraining).
AI.method: The authors curate EURO-5K, a corpus of sentence-level reporting obligations and challenging negatives from 136 EU acts. They then train and compare: (1) discriminative token-classification models based on BERT-like encoders; (2) generative span-extraction models based on LLMs. They evaluate full fine-tuning vs. QLoRA-style parameter-efficient tuning and compare them against rule-based and prompting baselines. They also conduct learning-curve and cross-dataset generalization analyses.
AI.result: Fully fine-tuned generic and legal-domain BERT models both reach about 0.89 F1. Fine-tuned LLMs reach similar accuracy for sentence-level extraction. Legal pretraining yields modest gains for generative models in general, but becomes clearly beneficial when adaptation capacity is limited: parameter-efficient tuning of Legal-BERT outperforms generic BERT in this regime. Learning curves show legal pretraining speeds up learning with small data, and all models plateau around 3K training samples. Cross-dataset tests on two external corpora confirm that the models specialize in reporting obligation extraction rather than broad regulatory classification.
AI.conclusion: EURO-5K is a sufficient and practically useful benchmark for reporting obligation extraction from EU law. Both discriminative and generative paradigms are effective, and parameter-efficient training is viable for real-world deployment. Legal-domain pretraining is most valuable in low-data or constrained fine-tuning scenarios, accelerating learning and improving performance. The released dataset, models, demo, and RDF export can support automation of regulatory compliance workflows.

title: Handoff Debt: The Rediscovery Cost When Coding Agents Take Over Interrupted Tasks
summary: Coding-agent benchmarks evaluate whether a single uninterrupted agent can resolve a repository issue. Real software work is messier: tasks are interrupted, reassigned, reviewed, and resumed from partial states left by another agent or engineer. We study this missing dimension through \emph{handoff debt}: the rediscovery cost imposed when a predecessor's work is opaque or incomplete. Our takeover protocol interrupts a coding agent at deterministic handoff points, freezes the repository, and evaluates successor agents under four handoff views: repository state only, raw trace, summary notes, and structured notes. Across 75 source tasks, the protocol generates 181 handoff-point tasks and 724 takeover runs per successor model. Across three successor models, context-bearing handoffs reduce median agent events by 20--59\% and cumulative prompt tokens by 42--63\% relative to repository-only takeover. Solved-rate effects are smaller and model-dependent, but efficiency gains are consistent. These findings suggest that coding-agent evaluation should report not only whether a task is solved, but also how costly that work is for another agent to resume.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark and protocol for evaluating how well coding agents can hand off partially completed work to successor agents, showing that richer handoff context significantly improves efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing coding-agent benchmarks mostly test a single agent working uninterrupted on a task, which does not reflect real software development where tasks are frequently interrupted, reassigned, and resumed. The authors want to capture the often-overlooked cost for a new agent to understand and continue someone else’s incomplete work, termed "handoff debt".
AI.method: The authors design a takeover protocol that simulates handoffs between coding agents. They interrupt an agent at predetermined handoff points, freeze the repository, and then let successor agents attempt to complete the task under four different handoff conditions: (1) only the repository state, (2) the raw interaction trace of the predecessor, (3) a textual summary of that work, and (4) more detailed structured notes. They apply this across 75 original source tasks, yielding 181 handoff-point tasks and 724 takeover runs per successor model, and compare performance and efficiency across three successor models.
AI.result: Providing context-bearing handoffs (raw trace, summary, or structured notes) reduces the number of agent events required by 20–59% and total prompt tokens by 42–63% compared with having only the repository state. Improvements in final problem-solving success rate are smaller and vary by model, but the efficiency gains from context are robust across conditions.
AI.conclusion: Evaluations of coding agents should incorporate the cost of handoff and resumption, not just task completion rates. Richer handoff artifacts substantially lower the rediscovery cost (“handoff debt”) for successor agents, suggesting that benchmarks and system designs should emphasize producing and leveraging effective contextual notes and traces for future handoffs.

title: Chatbots Output Meaningful (but Problematic) Language
summary:
Are utterances by AI chatbots meaningful? Concretely, if a user asks, say, Anthropic's agent Claude, "What is the capital of Spain?" and Claude answers, "Madrid is the capital of Spain," does that sentence have its ordinary meaning -- and does it express a true proposition? Most ordinary users, as well as AI engineers, take the answer to be trivially "yes." However, many cognitive scientists, linguists, and philosophers of language argue that dominant intentionalist accounts of language and meaning deliver the opposite conclusion.
  Theorists more sympathetic to ordinary users' intuitions have therefore advocated a radical "de-anthropomorphization" of language, revising our understanding of mental states, intentions, and semantic content to capture the intuition that the outputs of LLMs are meaningful. We take a different approach. While we, too, argue that LLM outputs are meaningful, we contend that a proper theory of human language already applies, as is, to current chatbots. Meaning is a low bar: claiming that LLM outputs are meaningful does not require positing mental states, intentions, rationality, or the cognitive capacities requisite for communication in LLMs -- or, indeed, making any other anthropomorphic assumptions. People do have communicative intentions (typically successful ones), but nevertheless, even in humans, language production can depart from what the speaker has in mind.
  Our view has important consequences for how we should theorize about -- and critically engage with -- both human linguistic output and synthetically generated text. In particular, to say that chatbots produce meaningful text is not by any means to endorse what they output, or to assume that the technology is (or is not) good, powerful, appropriate, or useful.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI chatbot utterances are genuinely meaningful in the ordinary sense, and that we can explain this using existing theories of human language without attributing human-like minds or intentions to AI systems.
AI.motivation: There is a growing debate about whether sentences produced by large language models (LLMs) genuinely have meaning and express true or false propositions. Many ordinary users and engineers assume they do, but some cognitive scientists, linguists, and philosophers claim that leading theories of meaning imply the opposite, because these theories tie meaning to speakers’ mental states and intentions, which LLMs lack. In response, some theorists propose radically revising our concepts of language and mentality to fit LLMs. The paper is motivated by this tension and aims to preserve both common-sense judgments about LLM outputs and a robust, non-revisionary theory of language.
AI.method: The authors use conceptual and philosophical analysis rather than empirical experiments. They critically examine intentionalist theories of meaning and the assumptions that lead some scholars to deny meaning to LLM outputs. They then argue, by drawing on a “proper theory of human language,” that meaning does not in fact require attributing mental states, intentions, or rational agency to the producer of an utterance. They use thought experiments and reflections on cases where even human language production diverges from speakers’ intentions to show that linguistic meaning can be detached from underlying mental content. On that basis, they extend the same theory straightforwardly to chatbot outputs.
AI.result: They show that once we clarify how linguistic meaning works in human cases, it becomes clear that LLM outputs meet the minimal conditions for being meaningful in the ordinary sense and can express true or false propositions. They reject the claim that intentionalist accounts force us to deny meaning to LLMs and argue that we do not need a radical “de-anthropomorphization” of language. Instead, the same low-bar criteria for meaning that already apply to some human linguistic phenomena also apply to text generated by chatbots.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that current AI chatbots produce meaningful text according to an adequate, existing theory of human language, and that this does not require attributing human-like mental states, intentions, or communicative capacities to the systems. Meaning is a “low bar”: recognizing that LLM outputs are meaningful does not imply that the systems are good, reliable, morally acceptable, or appropriate for any particular use. This distinction has important implications for how we theorize about, evaluate, and critique both human discourse and AI-generated text: we can treat chatbot outputs as semantically contentful while still being critical, cautious, or even skeptical about their epistemic and practical value.

title: Large AI Models in Dental Healthcare: From General-Purpose Systems to Domain-Specific Foundation Models
summary:
Background: Oral diseases affect nearly 3.5 billion people worldwide, yet the comparative clinical potential of large-scale AI models in dentistry remains poorly understood. Three distinct model categories have emerged: language-generative models, discriminative vision foundation models, and dental-specific foundation models, with no unified review examining their relationships and collective limitations.
  Methods: Following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, we systematically searched four databases (PubMed, Google Scholar, Scopus, arXiv), screened independently by two reviewers. After applying inclusion/exclusion criteria, 97 studies (2020-2026) were included. We propose a two-dimensional classification framework organizing models by architectural paradigm and dental specialization degree.
  Results: Language-generative models excel at text-based tasks (clinical reasoning, licensing exams, patient communication) but show inconsistent performance on image-dependent diagnostics. Adapted SAM and CLIP variants achieve strong tooth segmentation and lesion detection results. Dental-specific models (DentVFM, DentVLM, OralGPT) demonstrate strongest performance on complex multimodal tasks. Integrated pipelines consistently outperform single-model approaches. A data asymmetry is observed: dental-specific pretraining concentrates almost entirely in the vision domain, reflecting scarce large-scale dental text corpora.
  Conclusions: General-purpose and dental-specific models play complementary roles; the most effective systems combine both within structured pipelines. Safe autonomous deployment requires resolving three persistent barriers: hallucination in generative models, limited annotated dental datasets, and absent standardized clinical evaluation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A scoping review of large-scale AI models in dentistry shows that general-purpose language models, vision foundation models, and dental-specific models have complementary strengths, and that combined, pipeline-based systems are currently most promising for clinical applications.
AI.motivation: Despite widespread oral disease and rapid advances in large-scale AI, there is no unified overview of how different categories of foundation and generative models compare and interact in dental applications. The field lacks a clear taxonomy of model types, understanding of their relative strengths and weaknesses, and guidance for integrating them into clinically useful systems.
AI.method: The authors conducted a PRISMA-ScR–guided scoping review across four major databases (PubMed, Google Scholar, Scopus, arXiv) covering studies from 2020–2026. Two reviewers independently screened records based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria, ultimately including 97 studies. They then proposed a two-dimensional classification framework arranging AI models by architectural paradigm (e.g., language-generative vs vision-discriminative) and degree of dental specialization (general-purpose vs dental-specific).
AI.result: The review finds that large language models perform well on text-centric tasks (clinical reasoning, exams, patient communication) but inconsistently on image-heavy diagnostics. Vision foundation models such as SAM and CLIP, when adapted, provide strong performance in tasks like tooth segmentation and lesion detection. Dental-specific foundation models (e.g., DentVFM, DentVLM, OralGPT) show the best results on complex multimodal tasks. Systems that integrate multiple models in pipelines consistently outperform single-model setups. The analysis also reveals an imbalance: dental-specific pretraining predominantly targets vision data due to a lack of large, domain-specific dental text corpora.
AI.conclusion: General-purpose and dental-specific foundation models are not competing but complementary; combining them in structured, multimodel pipelines yields the best current performance for dental AI. However, routine and safe clinical use is constrained by persistent problems: hallucinations in generative language models, scarcity of high-quality annotated dental datasets, and the lack of standardized clinical benchmarks for evaluation. Addressing these barriers is essential for autonomous and trustworthy deployment.

title: Memory Retrieval for Changing Preferences
summary: Long-context dialogue systems must decide both when to access memory and which parts of the interaction history are relevant. Existing approaches typically rely on heuristic retrieval signals or always-on memory usage, failing to account for the changing and potentially inconsistent nature of user preferences. In this work, we propose a unified framework for memory access and selection based on changing preferences. We formulate personalized memory retrieval as identifying which historical turns provide evidence about a user's latent preference state, rather than relying on surface-level semantic similarity. To this end, we quantify the utility of each memory turn using a Bayes factor, defined as the improvement in the model's likelihood of the reference response when the turn is included in context. This provides a principled measure of evidence strength and a unified signal for both memory access and selection. By framing memory retrieval as utility estimation, the model learns to identify salient turns and regulate memory usage based on expected utility. Experiments on four heterogeneous memory benchmarks show that our approach outperforms existing embedding-based retrieval on long-context, preference-intensive tasks where modeling changing preferences is essential, while remaining competitive in low-density regimes where semantic similarity suffices.

AI.tldr: A framework for long-context dialogue systems that uses Bayesian utility-based memory retrieval focused on evolving user preferences, outperforming heuristic and embedding-based methods on preference-heavy tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context dialogue systems struggle to decide when to use memory and which historical turns matter, especially as user preferences change and can be inconsistent. Heuristic or always-on memory strategies ignore the latent, evolving nature of preferences and often rely on superficial semantic similarity, which is inadequate for preference modeling.
AI.method: They reformulate memory retrieval as identifying dialogue turns that provide evidence about a user's latent preference state, instead of using surface-level similarity. For each candidate memory turn, they compute a Bayes factor: how much including that turn in the context increases the model's likelihood of the reference response. This Bayes-factor-based utility becomes a unified signal for both whether to access memory and which memories to select, allowing the system to learn to regulate memory usage based on expected utility.
AI.result: Across four heterogeneous memory benchmarks, the Bayes factor utility-based retrieval outperforms standard embedding-based retrieval methods on long-context, preference-intensive dialogue tasks that require tracking changing user preferences. In scenarios where relevant information is sparse and simple semantic similarity is usually sufficient, their method remains competitive rather than degrading performance.
AI.conclusion: Treating memory retrieval as Bayesian utility estimation over dialogue history yields a principled mechanism for deciding when and what to remember, particularly effective for tasks involving evolving user preferences. This unified, evidence-based approach improves retrieval quality in complex long-context settings without sacrificing performance in simpler regimes.

title: What Benchmarks Don't Measure: The Case for Evaluating Abstention Competence in Autonomous Agents
comment: ACM CAIS 2026: RLEval Workshop Oral Presentation(Best Paper Award)
summary: Benchmarks for autonomous agents measure whether agents complete tasks, yet this framing is systematically blind to whether an agent should have proceeded at all. Agents trained under human-feedback objectives develop a structural tendency to proceed even when they lack the inputs, evidence, or authorization to act safely, a disposition we term compliance bias, because both the reward signal and the benchmark scoring regime treat proceeding as the correct default regardless of whether the preconditions for safe action are present. We make three contributions. We first show that compliance bias originates in reward hacking within human-feedback pipelines and is entrenched by prominent agent benchmarks, which either penalize agents for pausing or are architecturally unable to distinguish a principled pause from a silent failure. We then introduce a three-gap taxonomy of abstention-warranted scenarios, covering specification gaps where required information is absent, verification gaps where world state cannot be confirmed, and authority gaps where explicit authorization has not been given, which together provide a principled basis for constructing abstention-aware agent benchmarks. Finally, we propose abstention evaluation protocols (Safety Rate, Usability Rate, and Informed Refusal Rate) and report preliminary results across 144 enterprise agent scenarios and five model families, in which a runtime-enforced abstention mechanism achieves up to 89.2% hazardous-action blocking and 87.5% usability on authorized scenarios, demonstrating that the safety--usability tradeoff is tunable rather than inherent and that its shape varies substantially across model families. We treat this as preliminary work and offer the taxonomy and composite metrics as a starting point for further conversations.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current benchmarks and human-feedback training for autonomous AI agents encourage them to act even when they should abstain, and proposes a taxonomy and metrics to evaluate and improve agents’ ability to safely refuse to act.
AI.motivation: Existing agent benchmarks and RLHF-style training focus almost exclusively on task completion. This overlooks the critical safety question of when an agent should decline to act due to missing information, unverifiable world state, or lack of authorization. This bias towards always proceeding—termed compliance bias—can drive unsafe behaviors in real-world deployments, especially in enterprise settings. The authors aim to expose and characterize this bias and provide tools to benchmark and tune agents’ propensity to abstain when appropriate.
AI.method: Conceptual and empirical. Conceptually, the authors (1) analyze how human-feedback pipelines and current agent benchmarks structurally favor proceeding over abstaining and define the notion of compliance bias; (2) develop a three-gap taxonomy—specification gaps, verification gaps, and authority gaps—to categorize scenarios where agents should abstain. Empirically, they design abstention-aware evaluation protocols—Safety Rate, Usability Rate, and Informed Refusal Rate—and run experiments on 144 enterprise agent scenarios across five model families, comparing baseline agents with a runtime-enforced abstention mechanism that can block hazardous actions and require authorization or clarification.
AI.result: The study finds that current training and benchmark regimes embed a systematic tendency for agents to proceed by default, even when safety preconditions are not met. Using the proposed abstention-aware evaluation, a runtime-enforced abstention mechanism can block up to 89.2% of hazardous actions while still achieving 87.5% usability on authorized scenarios. Results also show that the tradeoff between safety (blocking hazardous actions) and usability (success on authorized tasks) is adjustable via design choices and that different model families exhibit notably different safety–usability profiles.
AI.conclusion: Compliance bias is a structural artifact of how human-feedback training and benchmarks are currently designed, not just a model quirk. To address this, benchmarks must explicitly represent and reward correct abstention, distinguishing principled pauses from failures. The proposed three-gap taxonomy and composite abstention metrics offer a framework to build such benchmarks and to tune the safety–usability balance of autonomous agents. The work is preliminary but intended as a foundation for further research on abstention-aware agent evaluation and design.

title: Predicting Inference-Time Scaling Gains from Labeled Validation-Set Output Statistics
summary: Best-of-$N$ inference scaling (drawing $N$ candidate answers from a language model and returning the one a reward model ranks highest) improves accuracy by an amount that varies across models, but predicting that amount in advance currently requires running the procedure end-to-end. Prior work links cheap statistics of a model's sampled outputs and validation-set correctness (how often samples agree, how diverse they are, how confident the model is, and where correct samples appear) to model behavior, but does not isolate which of these form a stable, compact predictor of best-of-$N$ gain. We fit ridge predictors on features computed from a single labeled validation-set sampling pass, use bootstrap-Lasso as a stability analysis of the candidate feature set, and give a concentration analysis with an explicit linear-approximation residual. Across three base-model families, six post-training methods, and math and reasoning task domains, the stability analysis identifies a strict three-feature core spanning prompt-level agreement spread, label-assisted first-correct-sample position, and completion-length variance; a compact ridge predictor built from this core plus an entropy add-on reaches Spearman $ρ= 0.90$ with actual best-of-$N$ gain under a reward-model verifier. The intended use is labeled validation-set screening of candidate configurations before paying the full reward-model scoring cost.

AI.tldr: They build a small set of statistical features from one sampling pass of a language model that can accurately predict how much best-of-N inference will improve performance, without having to run full best-of-N with a reward model.
AI.motivation: Best-of-N inference with a reward model can significantly improve language model accuracy, but it is expensive because it requires scoring many samples. Practitioners would like to know in advance which model and post-training setups are worth this cost, using only a cheap validation procedure. Existing work uses many loosely related statistics of model outputs but does not clarify which small subset reliably predicts best-of-N gains across models and tasks.
AI.method: They sample once from the model on a labeled validation set and compute many candidate features about the samples, such as agreement between samples, diversity, confidence, where the first correct sample appears, and length variance. They then train ridge regression predictors to map these features to the observed best-of-N performance gain. To understand which features matter robustly, they run a bootstrap-Lasso stability selection procedure. They also perform a concentration analysis to bound how well a linear predictor can approximate the true best-of-N gain, with an explicit residual term. They evaluate across multiple model families, post-training methods, and math/reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: The stability analysis consistently highlights a compact core of three predictive features: (1) spread of agreement across prompts, (2) label-assisted position of the first correct sample, and (3) variance in completion lengths. A ridge regression model using these three features plus an entropy-based feature achieves a high rank correlation (Spearman ρ≈0.90) with the true best-of-N gains measured via a reward-model verifier, across diverse settings.
AI.conclusion: A small, stable set of simple statistics from a single validation sampling pass can accurately predict the improvement from best-of-N inference under reward-model verification. This allows practitioners to cheaply screen and compare configurations before committing to expensive large-scale best-of-N runs, providing a practical tool for scaling and deploying language models more efficiently.

title: WISE-HAR: A Generalizable Ensemble Deep Learning Framework for WiFi-Based Human Activity Recognition
summary: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using WiFi signals has emerged as a transformative technology for smart homes, healthcare monitoring, security systems, and ambient assisted living. Unlike traditional camera-based systems that raise significant privacy concerns and fail in low-light conditions, or wearable sensors that require user compliance, WiFi-based HAR is non-intrusive, privacy-preserving, cost-effective, and works seamlessly in any lighting condition. This paper presents a comprehensive approach to recognize three distinct human activities: "No Presence" (empty room), "Walking", and "Walking + Arm-waving" using the Wallhack1.8k WiFi spectrogram dataset. We propose three key improvements to address the main challenges in WiFi-based HAR. First, to address high performance variance, we implement ensemble learning with five different CNN architectures (Deep CNN, Wide CNN, MobileNetV2, ResNet50V2, and EfficientNetB0). Second, to address the small dataset size limitation, we apply aggressive data augmentation techniques including time-warping, frequency masking, and noise addition. Third, to evaluate real-world generalization capability, we perform cross-scenario evaluation (training on Line-of-Sight and testing on Non-Line-of-Sight) and cross-antenna evaluation (training on Biquad antenna and testing on PIFA antenna). Our ensemble model achieved a test accuracy of 94.87% on the LOS scenario with Biquad antenna, outperforming the best individual model by 0.66%. Data augmentation improved Random Forest performance from 60% to 95%. Cross-scenario evaluation showed minimal accuracy drops of only 1.37% and 2.07%, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities. The results indicate that the proposed approach is robust, reliable, and suitable for real-world deployment in diverse environments with different hardware configurations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a WiFi-based human activity recognition system that uses ensemble CNN models and data augmentation to robustly classify three activities, achieving high accuracy and strong generalization across scenarios and hardware setups.
AI.motivation: Existing HAR methods using cameras or wearables suffer from privacy issues, environmental constraints (e.g., lighting), and user compliance requirements. WiFi-based HAR is promising but faces challenges such as high performance variance across models, limited labeled datasets, and poor generalization across environments and hardware. The paper aims to build a robust, privacy-preserving HAR system that works reliably in real-world, diverse deployment conditions.
AI.method: The authors use the Wallhack1.8k WiFi spectrogram dataset to recognize three activity classes: no presence, walking, and walking with arm-waving. They design an ensemble learning framework that combines five CNN architectures (Deep CNN, Wide CNN, MobileNetV2, ResNet50V2, EfficientNetB0). To tackle data scarcity, they apply aggressive data augmentation (time-warping, frequency masking, noise addition). They also perform cross-scenario (train on LOS, test on NLOS) and cross-antenna (train on Biquad, test on PIFA) evaluations to test generalization. Additionally, they assess how augmentation affects a traditional Random Forest baseline.
AI.result: The ensemble CNN model achieves 94.87% accuracy on the LOS scenario with the Biquad antenna, slightly outperforming the best single CNN by 0.66%. Data augmentation significantly improves Random Forest performance from 60% to 95%. Cross-scenario and cross-antenna evaluations show only small accuracy drops of 1.37% and 2.07%, indicating that the model generalizes well to different propagation conditions and hardware configurations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed WiFi-based HAR approach, combining ensemble CNNs with aggressive data augmentation, delivers high accuracy and strong robustness to environmental and hardware changes for recognizing a small set of human activities. The low accuracy degradation in cross-scenario and cross-antenna tests suggests it is suitable for real-world deployment in smart homes, healthcare, security, and ambient assisted living where privacy, non-intrusiveness, and generalization are critical.

title: A Locally Deployed RAG-Based Academic Advising System for Course Selection
comment: to be published in Elsevier's Procedia Computer. Sci. (KES 2026)
summary: The correct sequence of courses in the curriculum based on prerequisites between courses is of great importance for students to develop their knowledge and skills holistically. However, students crafting this sequence in isolation frequently struggle with recognition limitations and information overload that leads to confusion. Simultaneously, education institutions encounter difficulties in providing adequate academic advice for the correct sequence due to limited education resources. To address these challenges, we propose a locally deployed RAG-based academic advising system grounded in syllabus information. By combining large language models with retrieval from structured syllabus data, the system is designed to support course selection, prerequisite understanding, and personalized study planning in a privacy-preserving manner.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a locally deployed RAG-based advising system that uses syllabus data and large language models to help students choose courses, understand prerequisites, and plan their studies while preserving privacy.
AI.motivation: Students struggle to manually determine a correct course-taking sequence because of information overload and limited understanding of prerequisite structures, while institutions lack sufficient advising resources. There is a need for an automated, scalable, and accurate tool to guide course sequencing and selection.
AI.method: The authors design a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system that runs locally. It indexes structured syllabus information and uses large language models to answer student queries about course selection, prerequisites, and planning, leveraging retrieved syllabus data to ground responses.
AI.result: The system can generate course recommendations, explain prerequisite relationships, and assist with personalized study plans by drawing on structured syllabus data, all while keeping data processing local for privacy. (Detailed empirical results are not given in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: A locally deployed, syllabus-grounded RAG architecture is a promising solution for scalable and privacy-preserving academic advising, improving support for course sequencing and planning without heavily relying on human advisors.

title: Inducing Reasoning Primitives from Agent Traces
summary: ReAct-style LLM agents often rediscover the same reasoning routines across problems, yet leave those routines trapped in transient scratchpads. We introduce Reasoning Primitive Induction, a single-pass method that mines successful ReAct traces, clusters recurrent reasoning moves, and converts the most frequent moves into a compact library of typed pseudo-tools. Each pseudo-tool is specified by a natural-language docstring interpreted by an LLM at invocation time, and a standard ReAct loop composes these primitives at test time. The central result is that induced libraries outperform the very agent that generated their traces: by +44pp on RuleArena NBA (30 -> 74), +30pp on MuSR team allocation (38 -> 68), and +22pp on NatPlan meeting planning (7 -> 29). Across five comparable subtasks spanning narrative deduction, rule application, and constraint-satisfaction planning, a single fixed configuration improves over zero-shot Chain-of-Thought on every subtask, matches or surpasses expert-authored decompositions, and outperforms AWM at lower average inference cost.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to convert recurring reasoning steps from ReAct-style LLM agent traces into reusable 'pseudo-tools', leading to significantly better performance than the original agent and several strong baselines.
AI.motivation: ReAct-style LLM agents repeatedly discover useful reasoning patterns, but these patterns remain ephemeral in scratchpads and are not systematically reused or composed. The authors want to capture and formalize these recurrent reasoning moves so that models can reuse them as higher-level primitives, improving performance and efficiency across tasks without manual expert decomposition.
AI.method: They introduce Reasoning Primitive Induction, a single-pass pipeline that: (1) collects successful ReAct traces from an LLM agent, (2) mines and clusters recurring reasoning moves from those traces, and (3) converts the most frequent clusters into a library of typed pseudo-tools. Each pseudo-tool is defined with a natural-language docstring that another LLM interprets when it is called. At test time, a standard ReAct loop is augmented to plan and act using this induced library of primitives.
AI.result: Induced pseudo-tool libraries systematically outperform the base ReAct agent that generated the traces, with large accuracy gains: +44 percentage points on RuleArena NBA (30 -> 74), +30pp on MuSR team allocation (38 -> 68), and +22pp on NatPlan meeting planning (7 -> 29). Across five subtasks in narrative deduction, rule application, and constraint-satisfaction planning, a single fixed configuration beats zero-shot Chain-of-Thought on every subtask, matches or exceeds expert-authored decompositions, and outperforms the AWM method at lower average inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Automatically induced reasoning primitives from ReAct traces form an effective library of reusable pseudo-tools that improve reasoning performance and efficiency. This data-driven abstraction of recurrent reasoning moves can rival or surpass expert decompositions and strong baselines, suggesting that LLM agents can bootstrap their own higher-level reasoning skills from their interaction traces.

title: Pretraining Language Models on Historical Text
summary: We introduce TypewriterLM, a 7.24B History language model (LM) trained exclusively on English text predating 1913. Developing History LMs requires addressing challenges in data quality and availability, preventing temporal leakage, designing temporally consistent post-training pipelines, and constructing reliable evaluations. To address these issues, we construct TypewriterCorpus, a 54B-token historical corpus collected from diverse archival and linguistically annotated sources with extensive data cleaning and leakage mitigation procedures. Furthermore, we introduce lexically grounded instructing tuning, a post-training framework that constraints responses to remain directly grounded in historical source documents. Using this framework we construct two historical instruction tuning datasets: History-LIMA and History-SelfInstruct. To evaluate capability and temporal consistency, we introduce History-Event, a benchmark suite for evaluating competence, temporal grounding and data leakage. We release TypewriterLM and all associated resources to support future research on historical language models.

AI.tldr: TypewriterLM is a 7.24B historical language model trained only on pre-1913 English, with a carefully cleaned corpus, leakage prevention, a grounded instruction-tuning framework, and new evaluation benchmarks for temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing language models are typically trained on modern, web-scale data that mixes time periods and often leaks contemporary knowledge into historical tasks. This makes them unreliable for historically grounded analysis, temporal reasoning, and work by historians who require strict period-appropriate information. There is a need for language models that both use only historical sources and are evaluated for temporal consistency and data leakage, with transparent, shareable resources for the research community.
AI.method: The authors build TypewriterCorpus, a 54B-token corpus of English texts written before 1913, gathered from archival and linguistically annotated sources. They apply extensive data cleaning and procedures to prevent temporal leakage (i.e., modern content or metadata entering the training data). On top of a base model trained on this corpus, they propose lexically grounded instruction tuning, which constrains the model’s responses to remain explicitly grounded in quoted or referenced historical source documents. Using this framework they create two instruction datasets, History-LIMA and History-SelfInstruct. They also design History-Event, a benchmark suite that tests historical competence, temporal grounding, and evidence of data leakage by probing the model about historical events and timelines.
AI.result: They obtain TypewriterLM, a 7.24B-parameter language model specialized in historical English, accompanied by the TypewriterCorpus, the History-LIMA and History-SelfInstruct instruction-tuning datasets, and the History-Event evaluation benchmark. The model and resources are made publicly available. The abstract implies improved temporal consistency and grounding, as well as controlled leakage relative to generic LMs, though specific metrics are not given in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: A historically constrained language model can be effectively trained using a rigorously cleaned pre-1913 corpus and a lexically grounded instruction-tuning framework, yielding a system better aligned with historical sources and temporal constraints. The accompanying datasets and benchmarks form an open platform for further research on historical LMs, temporal grounding, and leakage detection.

title: AUDITFLOW: Executable Symbolic Environments for Structured Financial Reporting Verification
summary: Structured financial audit verification is difficult for language-model agents because correctness depends on structured evidence rather than text alone. A model must link reported facts to taxonomy concepts, traverse calculation or dimensional relations, and recompute expected values before applying an audit rule. We propose AuditFlow, a graph-grounded multi-agent framework that separates adaptive search from deterministic verification. AuditFlow builds a symbolic environment from a static US-GAAP taxonomy graph and a dynamic XBRL filing graph, and exposes it through typed tools for fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checking, and rule evaluation. Two junior auditors inspect each case from regulatory and evidentiary views, while a senior auditor resolves disagreements and can request further investigation. The final reports are fused through evidential aggregation to produce an audit verdict, expected value, evidence trail, and trustworthiness score. On a FinAuditing-derived FinMR sample, AuditFlow reaches 82.09% joint audit accuracy under GPT-5.5, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14.93 points. Removing deterministic checks drops accuracy to 17.91%, showing that the symbolic environment performs the verification step that the model cannot reliably replace.

AI.tldr: AuditFlow is a graph-grounded, multi-agent system that reliably verifies structured financial audits by combining LMs with a symbolic environment and deterministic checks.
AI.motivation: Language models struggle with structured financial audit verification because it requires mapping facts to a formal taxonomy, following calculation and dimensional relations, and numerically recomputing expected values, all of which go beyond text understanding. Existing LM agents often hallucinate or skip these precise verification steps, making them unreliable for real audit use cases that demand correctness and traceable evidence. The paper aims to build a framework where LMs coordinate with a symbolic structure to provide accurate, explainable audit decisions.
AI.method: The authors design AuditFlow, a multi-agent framework grounded in two interconnected graphs: a static US-GAAP taxonomy graph and a dynamic XBRL filing graph. These graphs are exposed to language-model agents via typed tools supporting fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical consistency checks, and rule evaluation. Two “junior auditor” agents independently analyze each case from regulatory and evidentiary perspectives; a “senior auditor” agent then compares their findings, resolves conflicts, and can trigger additional investigation. Deterministic verification (symbolic checks over the graphs) is separated from the agents’ adaptive search and reasoning. The system outputs include an audit verdict, an expected value, an explicit evidence trail, and a trustworthiness score. Performance is evaluated on a FinMR sample derived from the FinAuditing dataset under a GPT-5.5 backbone.
AI.result: On the FinMR benchmark derived from FinAuditing, AuditFlow achieves 82.09% joint audit accuracy when powered by GPT-5.5, surpassing the strongest baseline by 14.93 percentage points. An ablation that removes the deterministic symbolic checks leads to a drastic drop in accuracy to 17.91%, indicating that the symbolic environment is essential for correct verification and that the language model alone is insufficient for reliable structured audit reasoning.
AI.conclusion: AuditFlow demonstrates that coupling language-model agents with a graph-based symbolic environment and deterministic numerical verification enables much more reliable structured financial audit checking than LM-only approaches. The multi-agent design, graph-grounded tools, and evidential aggregation collectively provide accurate audit decisions with explicit evidence and calibrated trust scores. The large performance gap between the full system and the ablated version without deterministic checks suggests that future financial audit systems should treat LMs primarily as controllers over symbolic tools rather than as end-to-end verifiers.

title: Hint-Guided Diversified Policy Optimization for LLM Reasoning
summary: Recent developments in Large Language Models (LLMs) have showcased impressive reasoning capabilities, with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) being a promising enhancement strategy. However, existing reward mechanisms are constrained to the outcome-level correctness and lack explicit signals to guide the model to consider diverse solutions. In contrast, human problem solving typically involves evaluating multiple potential approaches and selecting the most reliable solution, a cognitive process that current RLVR frameworks do not explicitly incentivize. Inspired by this, we propose Hint-Guided Diversified Policy Optimization (HDPO), allowing the model to first list all potential candidate solution outlines as hints and then select the most reliable one for further reasoning. HDPO comprises two stages of Cold Start for Structured Reasoning and Hint-Guided Diversified Reinforcement Learning to incentivize the model to generate diverse and reliable solutions following the ``propose-select-think'' trajectory. Experimental results show that HDPO effectively boosts LLM reasoning and enhances the diversity of candidate solutions as well as the LLM's ability to identify reliable solutions.

AI.tldr: They introduce HDPO, a new RLVR-based training framework that makes an LLM first propose multiple solution sketches (“hints”), then select and fully solve with the most reliable one, improving reasoning and solution diversity.
AI.motivation: Existing RL with Verifiable Rewards for LLM reasoning only checks final-answer correctness and does not explicitly encourage the model to explore multiple approaches or to learn how to pick the best among them, unlike human problem solving which is naturally multi-path and evaluative.
AI.method: They design Hint-Guided Diversified Policy Optimization (HDPO), which has (1) a Cold Start for Structured Reasoning stage that teaches the LLM to generate structured candidate solution outlines, and (2) a Hint-Guided Diversified Reinforcement Learning stage where the model is rewarded for producing diverse candidate hints and for correctly selecting the most reliable one to elaborate, following a propose–select–think reasoning trajectory under RLVR-style verifiable rewards.
AI.result: Experiments show that HDPO improves the reasoning performance of LLMs, increases the diversity of candidate solution outlines, and strengthens the model’s ability to select reliable solutions compared to baseline RLVR or standard training approaches.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structuring reasoning as propose–select–think, and reinforcing both diversity and reliability of solution candidates, yields better LLM reasoning than outcome-only RLVR, suggesting that mimicking human-like multi-path evaluation is a fruitful direction for training reasoning models.

title: TriEval: A Resource-Efficient Pipeline for LLM Bias, Toxicity, and Truthfulness Assessment
summary: LLMs have evolved from basic chatbots to the backbone of the AI ecosystem, now widely used in healthcare, schools, and government services. The domain-wide adoption of LLMs necessitates continuous evaluation to ensure their safety and fairness. Common issues encountered after deploying LLMs include inconsistent outputs and hallucinations of incorrect information. Although numerous LLM evaluation tools exist, most are limited to testing a single parameter at a time or require massive computational resources that are not accessible to most researchers. TriEval addresses these challenges by evaluating LLM outputs across multiple parameters, including bias, toxicity, and truthfulness together, while minimizing computing resources. The pipeline is compatible with both open- and closed-source models and runs on a standard laptop without a GPU cluster. TriEval has been tested on four models: Llama 3 8B, Mistral 7B, Gemma 2 9B, and Claude Haiku. The results show clear differences between open-source and closed-source models, especially in terms of toxicity and truthfulness. TriEval is being released as open source to enable broader access for researchers with limited computational resources.

AI.tldr: TriEval is a lightweight, multi-metric evaluation pipeline for LLMs that jointly assesses bias, toxicity, and truthfulness across open- and closed-source models on modest hardware.
AI.motivation: LLMs are now widely deployed in critical domains like healthcare, education, and government, where safety, fairness, and reliability are essential. Existing evaluation tools either focus on a single aspect at a time (e.g., only bias or only hallucinations) or demand heavy computational resources, making them impractical for many researchers. There is a need for a practical framework that enables comprehensive safety evaluation of LLMs on standard hardware.
AI.method: The authors design TriEval, an evaluation pipeline that simultaneously measures multiple safety- and quality-related parameters of LLM outputs, specifically bias, toxicity, and truthfulness. The system is engineered to run efficiently on a standard laptop, supports both open- and closed-source models, and is applied to four representative LLMs: Llama 3 8B, Mistral 7B, Gemma 2 9B, and Claude Haiku. The pipeline likely defines standardized prompts and scoring procedures for each dimension and aggregates results to compare model behaviors.
AI.result: Using TriEval on the four models reveals systematic differences between open-source and closed-source LLMs, particularly regarding toxicity and truthfulness metrics. The abstract reports that these differences are "clear," suggesting that the framework can meaningfully discriminate models’ safety and reliability profiles even under constrained computational budgets.
AI.conclusion: TriEval demonstrates that it is feasible to jointly evaluate bias, toxicity, and truthfulness of multiple LLMs using modest computational resources, making comprehensive safety assessments more accessible. By releasing TriEval as open source, the authors aim to democratize LLM evaluation, enabling researchers and practitioners with limited hardware to compare and monitor models’ safety characteristics across domains.

title: Hallucinations as Orthogonal Noise: Inference-Time Manifold Alignment via Dynamic Contextual Orthogonalization
summary: Hallucination in Large Language Models (LLMs), characterized by the generation of content inconsistent with contextual facts or logical constraints -- remains a persistent challenge for reliable deployment. In this work, we address this issue through a geometric framework rooted in the linear representation hypothesis. We propose that hallucinations manifest as orthogonal noise relative to the semantic manifold of the residual stream. Specifically, we hypothesize that while attention heads ideally propagate information congruent with the context subspace, hallucinations arise when specific heads introduce components orthogonal to this subspace, disrupting the coherence of the latent representation. Based on this formulation, we introduce Dynamic Contextual Orthogonalization (DCO), an inference-time intervention method. DCO utilizes the input residual stream as a dynamic context anchor to perform orthogonal decomposition on attention head outputs. To distinguish between context-aligned semantic updates and divergent noise, DCO employs a layer-wise Z-score suppression mechanism that selectively attenuates outlier orthogonal components based on statistical distributions. Evaluations on Llama-3-8B and 70B across benchmarks such as XSum, NQ-Swap, and IFEval demonstrate that DCO achieves superior contextual faithfulness compared to state-of-the-art intervention baselines. Furthermore, DCO maintains high performance on knowledge-intensive tasks like TriviaQA and TruthfulQA, effectively mitigating the trade-off between hallucination suppression and parametric knowledge retention often observed in existing methods. Our findings validate the geometric interpretation of hallucinations and establish DCO as a computationally efficient approach for enforcing manifold alignment.Our code is available at https://github.com/Harry-Miral/DCO

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a geometric, inference-time method (DCO) that treats hallucinations in LLMs as orthogonal noise to the contextual semantic manifold and suppresses such components to improve faithfulness without harming knowledge use.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in LLMs remain a barrier to reliable real-world deployment, and existing mitigation methods often either require retraining, incur high computational cost, or suppress the model’s useful parametric knowledge along with hallucinations. The authors want a principled, efficient, inference-time method that reduces hallucinations while preserving performance on knowledge-intensive tasks.
AI.method: The authors adopt a geometric view based on the linear representation hypothesis, modeling the residual stream as a semantic manifold. They hypothesize that attention heads ideally pass information aligned with a context subspace, while hallucinations arise when some heads introduce components orthogonal to this subspace. They propose Dynamic Contextual Orthogonalization (DCO), an inference-time intervention that uses the input residual stream as a dynamic context anchor and performs orthogonal decomposition on each attention head’s output into context-aligned and orthogonal components. A layer-wise Z-score suppression mechanism then statistically detects outlier orthogonal components and selectively attenuates them, aiming to remove hallucination-inducing noise while keeping context-relevant semantics intact. DCO is applied without retraining and is computationally efficient.
AI.result: On Llama-3-8B and 70B, DCO is evaluated on hallucination-sensitive benchmarks such as XSum, NQ-Swap, and IFEval. It improves contextual faithfulness beyond state-of-the-art intervention baselines. Simultaneously, on knowledge-intensive benchmarks like TriviaQA and TruthfulQA, DCO maintains strong performance, showing minimal degradation in the model’s ability to use its stored knowledge. Empirical analyses support the claim that hallucination-related activity corresponds to orthogonal components in the representation space that can be selectively suppressed.
AI.conclusion: The work supports a geometric interpretation of hallucinations as orthogonal noise in the residual stream relative to a context-defined semantic manifold. Dynamic Contextual Orthogonalization offers a practical, inference-time mechanism to enforce manifold alignment by decomposing and attenuating orthogonal components of attention head outputs. This approach reduces hallucinations while preserving knowledge-intensive performance, and it is computationally efficient, making it a promising technique for more reliable deployment of LLMs.

title: RelGT-AC: A Relational Graph Transformer for Autocomplete Tasks in Relational Databases
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures. Code and model checkpoints available at https://github.com/jiangdmv/graph-transformer
summary: Relational databases underpin modern enterprise, scientific, and healthcare systems, yet predictive machine learning on such data remains challenging due to their multi-table, heterogeneous, and temporal structure. Relational Deep Learning (RDL) addresses this by representing databases as heterogeneous graphs and applying graph neural networks (GNNs) directly. RelBench v2 recently introduced autocomplete tasks -- a practically motivated task type where the goal is to predict an existing column value from relational context, analogous to an intelligent form-filling assistant. We propose RelGT-AC (Relational Graph Transformer for Autocomplete), extending the RelGT architecture with three targeted contributions: (1) a column masking strategy that prevents trivial solutions by masking the target column during subgraph encoding; (2) a unified task head supporting binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression autocomplete tasks within a single model; and (3) a TF-IDF text encoder that automatically detects and encodes free-text columns, recovering strong lexical signal that categorical encoders discard. Across 7 tasks spanning 3 RelBench v2 datasets (rel-trial, rel-f1, rel-stack), RelGT-AC outperforms the GraphSAGE baseline on all 3 regression autocomplete tasks and achieves up to +10 AUROC points on text-heavy eligibility tasks via the TF-IDF encoder.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RelGT-AC, a relational graph transformer for autocomplete tasks on relational databases, improving over GraphSAGE baselines, especially on text-heavy and regression tasks, via better masking, a unified prediction head, and a TF-IDF encoder for free-text columns.
AI.motivation: Relational databases are central to many real-world systems, but applying predictive ML is difficult because data is spread over multiple heterogeneous, temporal tables. Recent work (RDL and RelBench v2) frames this as learning on heterogeneous graphs and defines practical autocomplete tasks akin to intelligent form filling. However, existing models struggle with trivial leakage of target information, fragmented handling of different task types, and poor usage of free-text columns. There is a need for a unified, leakage-robust, and text-aware model for relational autocomplete.
AI.method: The authors extend the Relational Graph Transformer (RelGT) to an autocomplete setting (RelGT-AC) with three key changes: (1) a column masking strategy that explicitly masks the target column when building the subgraph for prediction, avoiding trivial solutions that directly read the answer; (2) a unified task head that can handle binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression autocomplete tasks within a single model, removing the need for task-specific architectures; and (3) integration of a TF-IDF-based text encoder that automatically detects free-text columns and encodes them with bag-of-words style lexical features, complementing or replacing categorical encoders that ignore token-level information.
AI.result: On 7 autocomplete tasks from 3 RelBench v2 datasets (rel-trial, rel-f1, rel-stack), RelGT-AC outperforms a GraphSAGE baseline on all 3 regression autocomplete tasks, and achieves up to +10 AUROC improvement on text-heavy eligibility prediction tasks when using the TF-IDF text encoder. These results demonstrate gains in both numerical prediction and text-informed classification scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RelGT-AC shows that a relational graph transformer, augmented with careful target-column masking, a unified prediction head, and explicit TF-IDF text encoding, can significantly improve autocomplete performance on relational databases. The approach better leverages relational structure and free-text information while remaining broadly applicable across multiple task types and datasets.

title: SEA-Embedding: Open and Reproducible Text Embeddings for Southeast Asia
summary: Text embeddings are fundamental to many downstream applications, making robustness important for real-world NLP. However, most recent state-of-the-art embedding models are not reproducible because they rely on closed or undisclosed training data, and they remain insufficiently robust for Southeast Asian languages. We present SEA-Embedding, a fully open and reproducible text-embedding pipeline for Southeast Asian languages trained only on publicly available data, and use it to study three core factors of robust embedding design: data composition, training objective, and base encoder initialization. SEA-Embedding achieves state-of-the-art results on SEA-BED while enabling systematic and reproducible analysis of robust text embeddings for the region.

AI.tldr: Open, reproducible, and robust text-embedding pipeline for Southeast Asian languages.
AI.motivation: Recent state-of-the-art text embedding models lack reproducibility due to closed or undisclosed training data and show insufficient robustness for Southeast Asian languages, creating a need for open, reliable, and regionally robust embeddings.
AI.method: The authors build SEA-Embedding, a text-embedding pipeline trained solely on publicly available data for Southeast Asian languages, and systematically vary three factors—data composition, training objective, and base encoder initialization—to study their impact on robustness.
AI.result: SEA-Embedding attains state-of-the-art performance on the SEA-BED benchmark for Southeast Asian languages and enables controlled experiments on robustness factors.
AI.conclusion: An open, reproducible text-embedding pipeline tailored to Southeast Asian languages can both match or surpass state-of-the-art performance and serve as a transparent testbed for analyzing how data, objectives, and initialization affect embedding robustness.

title: ToolGate: Token-Efficient Pre-Call Control for Tool-Augmented Vision-Language Agents
summary: Tool-augmented vision-language agents can acquire external perceptual evidence through OCR, detection, segmentation, and other tools, but executing every proposed tool call is costly and sometimes unnecessary. We study the pre-call control problem: after a ReAct-style VLM agent proposes a perceptual tool call, should the call be executed, or skipped before its output enters the context? Across five benchmarks, we find that the baseline agent exhibits poor local selectivity: helpful and harmful calls occur at similar rates (11.8% vs. 9.9%), while most calls do not change the immediate forced-answer prediction. We introduce ToolGate, a lightweight external controller that predicts execute/skip decisions from trajectory text and simple structural features. Across two Qwen3-VL backbones, ToolGate reduces token cost to 64-69% of the unrestricted ReAct baseline while preserving average accuracy in cross-domain settings. With matched-domain trajectory training on Qwen3-VL-30B, it further improves average accuracy by 1.65 points. These results show that tool-augmented VLM agents benefit not only from better perceptual tools, but also from explicit control over when tool outputs are worth paying for.
