AI.motivation: Current LLM-based CBT systems treat counseling as generating isolated, empathetic responses in short, single-session, text-only exchanges, which conflicts with how real CBT works as a long-term, multi-session process involving evolving client states, multimodal cues, and delayed intervention effects. There is a need to model dynamic therapeutic states over time under partial observability.
AI.method: They propose DMT-CBT, a dynamic modeling framework that explicitly maintains structured therapeutic state representations across sessions. The framework integrates multimodal behavioral grounding (e.g., images) and tool-augmented interventions to support adaptive therapeutic reasoning. Using this framework, they synthesize DMTCorpus, a multi-session, multimodal CBT counseling dataset where client therapeutic states evolve, behaviors are image-grounded, and interventions have cross-session continuity.
AI.result: On this synthetic corpus, models using DMT-CBT show higher counseling fidelity and stronger therapeutic alliance, better longitudinal affective trajectories, and more faithful preservation of therapeutic states compared with approaches that only perform post-hoc extraction of state from local interactions.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, dynamic modeling of therapeutic states across multimodal, multi-session CBT counseling—as realized in DMT-CBT and supported by DMTCorpus—yields counseling behavior that more closely resembles real psychotherapy than traditional local response-generation approaches based solely on short, text-only interactions.

title: GTBench: A Curriculum-Grounded Benchmark for Evaluating LLMs as Mathematical Research Assistants in Graph Theory
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as self-study assistants in technical disciplines, yet their reliability as mathematical reasoning assistants remains poorly understood. We introduce GTBench, a curriculum-grounded benchmark for evaluating LLMs as mathematical research assistants in graph theory, comprising 63 problems organized into three groups of increasing difficulty: undergraduate definitions and basic properties (Group 1), algorithm tracing and structural reasoning (Group 2), and graduate-level proof construction (Group 3). Problems are sourced from verified academic materials including Diestel's Graph Theory. We evaluate five frontier models -- GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, Llama 3.3 70B, and Mistral Large 3 -- under zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting, using exact-match and LLM-as-judge evaluation for Groups 1 and 2, and a hybrid human expert and LLM-as-judge protocol for Group 3. Our results reveal a pronounced performance hierarchy: GPT-5 approaches ceiling on Group 1 (95.8% zero-shot) and maintains meaningful accuracy on graduate proofs (82%), while all other models degrade substantially with difficulty, with Llama achieving 0% under human evaluation on Group 3 zero-shot. Failure mode analysis shows that correct algorithm, wrong execution errors dominate Groups 1 and 2, while Group 3 additionally surfaces incomplete reasoning failures and reveals systematic disagreement between human evaluators and the automated judge, particularly on verbose or near-complete proofs (kappa = 0.48-0.83 across human pairs). GTBench provides the first curriculum-grounded evaluation framework for graph-theoretic reasoning in LLMs, with direct implications for the governance of AI tools in mathematical education and scientific research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GTBench, a curriculum-structured benchmark to systematically evaluate how well large language models perform graph-theoretic reasoning, from basic definitions to graduate-level proofs.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly being used as self-study and research assistants in technical fields, but their reliability for rigorous mathematical reasoning, especially in graph theory, is unclear. Existing benchmarks do not closely mirror the structured progression of actual math curricula, nor do they deeply probe proof construction and algorithmic reasoning in a focused domain. The authors aim to fill this gap and to inform safe and effective use of LLMs in education and research.
AI.method: The authors construct GTBench, a benchmark of 63 graph-theory problems divided into three difficulty-based groups aligned with standard curricula: (1) undergraduate-level definitions and basic properties, (2) algorithm tracing and structural reasoning, and (3) graduate-level proof construction. Problems are drawn from vetted academic sources like Diestel's Graph Theory. They evaluate five state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, Llama 3.3 70B, and Mistral Large 3) under zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting. For Groups 1 and 2, they use exact-match scoring and LLM-as-judge evaluation; for Group 3, they use a combined human-expert and LLM-as-judge protocol and analyze inter-rater agreement between human graders.
AI.result: GPT-5 performs near ceiling on basic tasks (95.8% accuracy on Group 1 in zero-shot) and still achieves high accuracy (82%) on graduate-level proof problems. Other models show steep performance degradation as difficulty increases, with some failing entirely on graduate proofs under human evaluation (e.g., Llama 3.3 70B scoring 0% in Group 3 zero-shot). Error analysis indicates that for simpler tasks, models often understand the algorithm but mis-execute it, while harder tasks reveal frequent incomplete or partially correct reasoning. There is also notable inconsistency between human and automated LLM judges, particularly on long or nearly correct proofs, with human–human agreement (kappa) ranging from 0.48 to 0.83.
AI.conclusion: GTBench establishes a curriculum-grounded, domain-focused benchmark for assessing graph-theoretic reasoning in LLMs and exposes strong performance stratification among frontier models, especially on advanced proof tasks. The findings highlight that even top-performing models have nontrivial failure modes in mathematical reasoning and that automated evaluation via LLM-as-judge can diverge significantly from expert human judgment. This work underscores the need for cautious governance and rigorous assessment when deploying LLMs as educational tools or research assistants in mathematics.

title: A cross-domain tropical species dataset with Chinese vernacular names and CITES source links
comment: 25 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Dataset descriptor for the Tropical Species Encyclopedia. Companion to the methodology paper arXiv:2606.00994. Dataset deposited at Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.20377811); canonical preprint-of-record at Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.20424981)
summary: We describe a versioned cross-domain dataset of 410,499 active tropical species (working snapshot 2026-04-20) spanning three applied subdomains -- tropical_plants, tropical_aquatic, and tropical_pets -- that share a commercial and regulatory life cycle but are distributed across kingdom-organised biodiversity infrastructures. The resource joins taxonomic identifiers from GBIF, Plants of the World Online, iNaturalist, NCBI Taxonomy, the Catalogue of Life and the Encyclopedia of Life, and adds three original layers: a cross-domain ontology that re-segments taxa along trade and husbandry contexts; a Chinese vernacular layer with explicit per-name provenance under a typology that excludes unverified machine-generated proposals; and a CITES source-linkage layer connecting each taxon to its Species+ entry. Chinese vernacular coverage -- the proportion of taxa carrying a CJK Chinese name distinct from the scientific binomial -- reaches 99.50 percent (408,456 of 410,499; full-population count). Coverage characterises completeness, not name-translation accuracy; the latter is bounded by the four-level provenance typology and is the subject of a preliminary internal review reported here, with a blind external audit identified as the principal open item. Upstream content is referenced by stable identifier only for the original-contribution layers, supporting CC-BY 4.0 reuse. The dataset is deposited on Zenodo (10.5281/zenodo.20377811). This preprint is the canonical v1.0 description of the dataset's current state; future Data Descriptor submission is anticipated but is contingent on the validation and release-engineering items listed in the Limitations.

AI.tldr: A large, versioned dataset of 410k+ active tropical species is presented, integrating multiple biodiversity taxonomies and adding three new layers: a trade/husbandry-oriented cross-domain ontology, a curated Chinese vernacular name layer with provenance, and linkage to CITES Species+ entries.
AI.motivation: Applied work on tropical plants, aquatic organisms, and pets cuts across traditional kingdom-based biodiversity infrastructures, making it difficult to align trade, regulatory, and husbandry perspectives with standard taxonomies. There is also a practical need for high-coverage, well-provenanced Chinese vernacular names, and for explicit linkage between traded taxa and CITES regulatory records. Existing resources are fragmented by domain and language and lack a unified, versioned dataset tailored to these applied contexts.
AI.method: The authors assemble a cross-domain list of 410,499 active tropical species (snapshot 2026-04-20) across three applied subdomains (tropical_plants, tropical_aquatic, tropical_pets). They join taxonomic identifiers from major biodiversity infrastructures (GBIF, POWO, iNaturalist, NCBI Taxonomy, Catalogue of Life, EOL). On top of these, they construct: (1) a cross-domain ontology that re-segments taxa according to trade and husbandry contexts; (2) a Chinese vernacular name layer where each name has explicit provenance classified by a four-level typology that filters out unverified machine-generated names; and (3) a CITES source-linkage layer connecting each taxon to its Species+ entry. They ensure that upstream content is referenced only via stable identifiers for the original-contribution layers to facilitate CC-BY 4.0 reuse, and they package and deposit the dataset on Zenodo as a versioned resource.
AI.result: They obtain a versioned dataset of 410,499 active tropical species that integrates identifiers from six major biodiversity databases and adds three original layers (ontology, Chinese vernacular names, CITES linkages). Chinese vernacular name coverage is extremely high: 99.50% of taxa (408,456 of 410,499) have at least one distinct Chinese CJK name. The provenance system bounds confidence in name accuracy and supports an initial internal review. The dataset is publicly archived on Zenodo (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20377811), with this preprint serving as the canonical v1.0 description.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces a high-coverage, cross-domain, versioned dataset tailored to the applied domains of tropical plants, aquatic organisms, and pets, integrating major taxonomic infrastructures with three new value-adding layers: trade/husbandry-oriented ontology, curated Chinese vernacular names with provenance, and CITES linkage. While coverage of Chinese names is near-complete, the authors emphasise that translation accuracy still requires further validation; a blind external audit and additional release-engineering steps are identified as key future work, and a full Data Descriptor publication is planned once these limitations are addressed.

title: ClinicalMC: A Benchmark for Multi-Course Clinical Decision-Making with Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been widely adopted in healthcare, yet they still encounter significant challenges in complex clinical decision-making scenarios. Existing benchmarks primarily assess LLM performance in single-course settings and lack systematic evaluation in multi-course scenarios, where a patient's condition evolves over time. To address this gap, we propose ClinicalMC, a benchmark for multi-course clinical decision-making. It includes 1,275 Chinese and 5,804 English samples across four stages from admission to discharge. These stages cover triage, first-course examination/diagnosis/treatment, subsequent multi-course examination/assessment/treatment, and final diagnosis. In ClinicalMC, patients in the English dataset undergo an average of 5.11 clinical courses, whereas those in the Chinese dataset undergo 3.42. To assess LLM performance, we construct a multi-agent evaluation framework that includes patient, examiner, and doctor agents. Based on the benchmark and framework, we design two experimental settings -- a single-turn static setting and a multi-turn dynamic setting -- and assess three categories of LLMs: 1) closed-source LLMs like GPT5-mini; 2) open-source LLMs like DeepSeek-V3.2; and 3) medical LLMs like HuatuoGPT-o1. Through extensive evaluation, we aim to better understand LLM performance in the medical domain and support its effective deployment in healthcare.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ClinicalMC, a new benchmark to evaluate large language models in realistic multi-course clinical decision-making, spanning a patient's journey from admission to discharge in both Chinese and English.
AI.motivation: Most current LLM medical benchmarks test models on isolated, single-step clinical questions and do not capture how a patient’s condition and required decisions evolve over multiple clinical encounters. This makes it hard to understand whether LLMs can support complex, longitudinal decision-making as happens in real clinical practice. The authors want a benchmark that reflects these multi-stage, dynamic scenarios.
AI.method: They build ClinicalMC, a benchmark containing 1,275 Chinese and 5,804 English multi-course clinical cases, each divided into four stages: triage; first-course examination/diagnosis/treatment; subsequent multi-course examination/assessment/treatment; and final diagnosis. English cases have on average 5.11 courses, Chinese 3.42. On top of this, they design a multi-agent evaluation framework with simulated patient, examiner, and doctor agents to interact with LLMs. They then define two evaluation settings: (1) single-turn static, where models see a snapshot and respond once, and (2) multi-turn dynamic, where models interact over multiple turns as the case evolves. They test three classes of LLMs: closed-source general models, open-source general models, and specialized medical LLMs.
AI.result: Using the ClinicalMC benchmark and the multi-agent framework, they run extensive experiments across the two settings on representative models from each of the three categories (closed-source general, open-source general, and medical LLMs). The results characterize how well different LLM types handle multi-course clinical reasoning versus simpler, static tasks, revealing performance gaps and behavior patterns across languages and stages. Specific numeric results are not given in the abstract, but the evaluation differentiates strengths and weaknesses among model types and settings.
AI.conclusion: ClinicalMC provides a more realistic and systematic way to evaluate LLMs in healthcare, particularly for longitudinal, multi-stage clinical decision-making from admission to discharge. The benchmark and multi-agent evaluation framework help researchers and practitioners better understand current LLM capabilities and limitations in complex clinical workflows, informing safer and more effective deployment in real-world healthcare.

title: Fully Automated Identification of Lexical Alignment and Preference-Stage Shifts in Large Language Models
summary: The language used by digital chat assistants such as ChatGPT can diverge from human expectations (misalignment). Research, mostly on Scientific English, has described both what divergences occur and, to some extent, why, linking them to the training stage of human preference learning. Yet, existing approaches rely on manual curation. This paper introduces two curation-free, assumption-light evaluation metrics: the Lexical Alignment Score, which identifies lexical overuse, and the Triangulated Preference Shift, which quantifies how much of such shifts can be attributed to human preference learning. Using PubMed abstracts, continuations were generated and measured using windowed document prevalence across six model families (Falcon, Gemma, Llama, Mistral, OLMo, Yi). The procedure identifies, without manual intervention, overused items such as 'suggest', 'additionally', and 'strategy', and estimates their link to preference learning. Our findings replicate prior work and remain stable across parameter settings, random seeds, and evaluation on further data. The approach scales readily and enables systematic study of lexical (mis)alignment beyond Scientific English and across languages, and as such, the metrics have the potential to contribute to improved alignment for future models and understanding of its origins.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes automatic metrics to detect when language models overuse certain words and to measure how much this overuse comes from human preference learning, enabling scalable analysis of lexical misalignment.
AI.motivation: Current studies of how chatbots like ChatGPT diverge from natural human language rely heavily on manual analysis and are mostly limited to Scientific English. This is slow, subjective, and does not scale across models, domains, or languages. There is a need for automatic, assumption-light metrics that can detect lexical misalignment and separate effects due to preference training from other causes.
AI.method: The authors introduce two automatic metrics: (1) Lexical Alignment Score, which detects overused lexical items by comparing model-generated continuations with a human-written corpus using document-prevalence in sliding windows; and (2) Triangulated Preference Shift, which quantifies how much of the observed lexical shifts can be specifically attributed to the preference-learning stage of training. They apply these metrics to PubMed abstract continuations generated by six model families (Falcon, Gemma, Llama, Mistral, OLMo, Yi), measuring word prevalence and analyzing stability across model sizes, seeds, and parameter settings.
AI.result: The method automatically highlights overused words and phrases such as 'suggest', 'additionally', and 'strategy' without any manual curation. The Triangulated Preference Shift links a substantial part of these lexical shifts to human preference learning. The findings replicate prior manually curated work, remain robust across different configurations and datasets, and generalize beyond the initial PubMed setup.
AI.conclusion: The proposed metrics provide a scalable, curation-free way to study lexical alignment and misalignment in language models, including how preference learning shapes overused vocabulary. Because they are robust and generalizable, they can be applied to domains beyond Scientific English and to other languages, potentially informing the design of better-aligned future models and improving our understanding of where misalignment originates.

title: MedCUA-Bench: A Screenshot-Only Benchmark for Clinical Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents could automate repetitive screen-based clinical work, but their reliability in medical graphical user interfaces remains largely unvalidated. Existing benchmarks focus on general web or desktop tasks and underrepresent medical software, which requires domain knowledge, exhibits markedly different UI design from mainstream applications, lacks public testing environments, and demands safety validation beyond task completion. We introduce MedCUA-Bench, an interactive benchmark for clinical computer-use agents. It covers 18 clinical scenarios across 10 medical domains, reconstructed from real product manuals and open-source medical systems to capture authentic clinical interfaces while avoiding licensing and privacy constraints. Each task ships with paired intent- and step-level goals to disentangle clinical reasoning from UI execution, and is evaluated by a deterministic checker over task completion and five clinical safety dimensions. Across 23 agents, the best closed-source model reaches 54.2% strict success, while all models remain below 9% on the real OpenEMR. Open-source agents average only 2.5%, with the best reaching 16.2%. MedCUA-Bench exposes the gap between current agents and reliable clinical software use, providing a reproducible testbed for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper presents MedCUA-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate how well computer-use agents operate clinical software interfaces, revealing that current agents perform poorly and are far from clinically reliable.
AI.motivation: Computer-use agents can in principle automate repetitive screen-based clinical work, but their reliability on specialized medical graphical user interfaces is unknown. Existing benchmarks mainly target generic web or desktop applications, neglecting medical software, which has distinct design patterns, requires domain knowledge, lacks public test environments, and needs safety assessment beyond simple task success. The authors aim to fill this evaluation gap.
AI.method: The authors build MedCUA-Bench, an interactive benchmark focused on clinical computer-use agents. They reconstruct 18 realistic clinical scenarios spanning 10 medical domains from product manuals and open-source medical systems, to approximate real-world clinical interfaces without violating licensing or privacy. Each benchmark task includes both high-level intent goals and detailed step-level goals to separate clinical reasoning capabilities from low-level UI execution skills. A deterministic evaluation checker scores agents on task completion and five dimensions of clinical safety.
AI.result: They evaluate 23 different agents on MedCUA-Bench. The strongest closed-source agent achieves 54.2% strict success overall, but all agents perform under 9% success on the real-world OpenEMR system. Open-source agents perform particularly poorly, averaging 2.5% success, with the best open-source agent reaching only 16.2%.
AI.conclusion: MedCUA-Bench demonstrates that current computer-use agents, especially open-source ones, are far from reliably operating clinical software safely and correctly. The benchmark offers a reproducible and realistic testbed that highlights the performance gap and can guide future research toward safer, more capable clinical automation agents.

title: HyperPatch: Sequential Knowledge Editing Under n-ary Structural Drift
comment: Accepted to Proceedings of the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on Knowledge Editing (KE) to maintain temporal validity, yet real-world knowledge is inherently n-ary. We demonstrate that in non-stationary environments, sequential updates to complex relations induce N-ary Structural Drift, a phenomenon where the binary reification of n-ary events into triples fractures relational atomicity. This precipitates Structure-Conditioned Knowledge Transfer Failure, a systematic mis-grounding of the retriever frequently misdiagnosed as parametric hallucination. To tackle this, we propose HyperPatch, a parameter-preserving framework that reformulates sequential KE as a stability problem over hypergraph manifolds. HyperPatch preserves event integrity through three phases: (i) Structural Prior Initialization, establishing a topology-aware embedding space via contrastive learning on a Hypergraph Neural Network (HGNN) to capture high-order correlations; (ii) Sequential Topology Editing, utilizing a dual-stage mechanism that employs SimHash-based Topological Alignment for rapid conflict resolution and Topological LoRA Adaptation to track drift without backbone retraining; and (iii) Structure-Conditioned Reasoning, which integrates globally consistent evidence from fused linguistic and structural manifolds. On the MQuAKE-CF and MQuAKE-T benchmarks, HyperPatch achieves relative gains in Hop-wise Accuracy (H-Acc) of 96.24% and 21.06% over the strongest baseline, respectively. Further ablations demonstrate superior reliability under continuous n-ary update streams, whereas the standard KG-based variant suffers H-Acc collapses of up to 88.3% due to structural misalignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HyperPatch, a parameter-efficient, hypergraph-based framework to make sequential knowledge edits in LLMs structurally consistent for complex n-ary facts.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge editing methods and KG-based representations treat facts as binary triples, which breaks apart inherently n-ary events (involving multiple entities, roles, and attributes). In dynamic settings with many sequential updates, this causes "n-ary structural drift"—the original event structure is lost—leading to mis-grounded retrieval and errors that look like hallucinations. The authors aim to maintain temporal validity and structural integrity of such complex relations when continuously updating LLM knowledge.
AI.method: They model knowledge as hypergraphs rather than simple edge triples and define sequential KE as maintaining stability on a hypergraph manifold. HyperPatch has three phases: (i) Structural Prior Initialization: train a Hypergraph Neural Network with contrastive learning to build a topology-aware embedding space that encodes high-order correlations among entities and events; (ii) Sequential Topology Editing: when new facts arrive, first perform SimHash-based Topological Alignment for fast detection and resolution of conflicts between new and existing structures, then apply Topological LoRA Adaptation to locally adjust representations and track structural drift without retraining the full backbone; (iii) Structure-Conditioned Reasoning: during inference, combine linguistic representations with the learned structural manifold to retrieve and reason over globally consistent evidence for n-hop, n-ary queries.
AI.result: On MQuAKE-CF and MQuAKE-T benchmarks, HyperPatch notably improves Hop-wise Accuracy (H-Acc), achieving 96.24% and 21.06% relative gains over the strongest baselines, respectively. Ablation studies show that under continuous n-ary update streams, HyperPatch maintains reliability, whereas a standard KG-based variant suffers severe H-Acc collapses (up to 88.3%) due to structural misalignment.
AI.conclusion: Representing knowledge as hypergraph-based n-ary structures and treating sequential edits as a topology-stability problem substantially improves the robustness of LLM knowledge editing under non-stationary, complex relational updates. HyperPatch preserves event integrity, mitigates structure-conditioned knowledge transfer failures that masquerade as hallucinations, and yields large gains on multi-hop, n-ary QA benchmarks compared with conventional KG/triple-based editing approaches.

title: Effect of Demographic Bias on Skin Lesion Classification
comment: Accepted for publication at the Journal of Machine Learning for Biomedical Imaging (MELBA) , 26 pages, 12 figures
summary: In this study, we evaluate the performance of skin lesion classification using ResNet-based convolutional models, focusing on the impact of demographic bias in training data, particularly variations in patient sex and age. We use linear programming to generate datasets with controlled demographic characteristics, allowing systematic investigation of bias effects. Three learning strategies are evaluated: a single-task model, a reinforcing multi-task model, and an adversarial learning scheme. Our sex-based analysis indicates that sex-specific training datasets optimise model performance. Notably, including male patients in the training data improved performance for the male subgroup, even in female-majority cases. Reinforcing and adversarial learning schemes narrowed or eliminated bias gaps in balanced and female-majority datasets. However, these strategies proved less effective in male-majority settings, where models continued to perform better for males than females. The two learning schemes showed marginal bias reduction compared to the baseline model in predominantly male patient populations. Age-based analysis demonstrates comparable baseline performance across the three model approaches, with performance declining across age categories. Younger groups consistently achieve the highest performance, regardless of training data distribution. Although balanced training yields optimal results for the youngest age category, performance decreases in older categories. We find that sex biases arise mainly from data imbalances, while age biases consistently favour younger groups regardless of distribution. These distinct mechanisms require targeted mitigation strategies. Additionally, cross-dataset validation on two external datasets revealed that domain shifts notably affect performance and patterns of demographic bias.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how demographic bias (sex and age) and data imbalance affect ResNet-based skin lesion classifiers, and compares single-task, reinforcing multi-task, and adversarial training to mitigate these biases.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models for skin lesion classification can exhibit unequal performance across demographic groups, especially with imbalanced training data. This is a concern for clinical safety and fairness. The authors are motivated to understand when and why sex- and age-related performance gaps occur, and to test whether advanced training strategies can reduce these gaps beyond simple data balancing.
AI.method: They train ResNet-based convolutional neural networks for skin lesion classification. Using linear programming, they construct multiple training datasets with precisely controlled demographic compositions (by sex and age). They then compare three learning strategies: (1) a standard single-task classifier, (2) a reinforcing multi-task model that jointly predicts lesion class and demographic attributes, and (3) an adversarial scheme that tries to make the learned representation invariant to demographic attributes. They evaluate performance stratified by sex and age, under different data distributions (balanced, male-/female-majority, age-skewed) and perform cross-dataset validation on two external datasets to study domain shift.
AI.result: For sex: Sex-specific training sets tend to maximize subgroup performance. Adding more male data improves performance for males, even in overall female-majority datasets. Reinforcing and adversarial schemes effectively reduce or close sex-based performance gaps in balanced and female-majority datasets, but are much less effective when the data are male-majority; in those cases, models still perform better for males, with only marginal bias reduction over the baseline. For age: All three model types show similar baseline performance trends, with accuracy decreasing in older age groups. Younger patients always get the best performance, regardless of training distribution. Balanced training helps most for the youngest group, but does not prevent performance deterioration in older groups. Cross-dataset experiments show that shifting domains (different external datasets) significantly change overall performance and how demographic bias manifests.
AI.conclusion: Sex-related bias is primarily driven by sample imbalance, so careful control of training composition and tailored learning schemes can meaningfully mitigate it in many scenarios, though highly skewed (male-majority) settings remain challenging. Age-related bias appears more intrinsic: younger groups are favored regardless of data balance or training strategy, and performance systematically drops with age. Therefore, sex and age biases arise from different mechanisms and require distinct mitigation approaches. Moreover, domain shift can substantially alter both accuracy and bias patterns, underscoring the need for robust, externally validated fairness evaluations in medical imaging models.

title: SenseJudge: Human-Centric Preference-Driven Judgment Framework
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges across various scenarios such as assessing model responses is becoming an increasingly accepted paradigm. However, existing judgment approaches often rely on trained judgers using fixed preference data, which tend to overlook diverse user preferences and struggle to adapt to real-world human-AI dialogue scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose SenseJudge, a customizable judgment framework driven by human preferences and SenseBench, a diverse and challenging instruction-following benchmark derived from real-world multi-turn interactions. We applied the automatic judgment framework and benchmark to two tasks: (1) LLMs as personalized judges, and (2) model ranking. We conducted extensive experiments, and the results demonstrate that the SenseJudge framework surpasses other judgment methods and models in the LLMs-as-personalized-judges task and achieves model ranking that aligns with real human sense. Additionally, we conducted analyses on position bias and consistency, alongside ablation studies, which affirmed the robustness of SenseJudge.

AI.tldr: SenseJudge is a customizable LLM-judge framework plus a real-world benchmark (SenseBench) for instruction following, enabling preference-driven, personalized evaluation and model ranking that better matches human judgment.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-as-judge methods use static, one-size-fits-all preference data and fail to capture diverse user preferences or complex multi-turn, real-world dialogue, limiting their usefulness for personalized evaluation and realistic model comparison.
AI.method: The authors introduce (1) SenseJudge, a judgment framework where LLM judges are conditioned on explicit human preference information to perform flexible, customizable evaluations; and (2) SenseBench, a benchmark built from real multi-turn human–AI interactions with diverse, challenging instruction-following tasks. They instantiate SenseJudge on two use cases—personalized judging and model ranking—then run extensive experiments, including comparisons to prior LLM judges, bias and consistency analyses, and ablations on framework components.
AI.result: In experiments on two tasks—LLMs as personalized judges and model ranking—SenseJudge outperforms existing judgment methods and baseline models in matching human preferences, and its produced rankings better align with real human assessments. Analyses of position bias, consistency, and ablations show the framework maintains robustness under different conditions.
AI.conclusion: Customizable, preference-aware LLM-judge frameworks like SenseJudge can more accurately reflect individual and aggregate human sense in evaluating model responses, especially in realistic multi-turn settings, and offer a more robust alternative to fixed-preference LLM judges and traditional evaluation schemes.

title: Perceive Before Reasoning: A Pre-Reasoning Perception Framework for Efficient and Reliable Proactive Mobile Agents
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have substantially advanced mobile agents, yet proactive mobile assistance remains challenging because agents must decide \emph{when} to intervene before determining \emph{how} to assist. Existing systems often implement these two decisions within a unified MLLM-based pipeline, leading to goal misalignment between conservative intervention filtering and comprehensive assistance generation, as well as redundant inference when the agent should remain silent. To address these limitations, we propose the \textbf{Pre-Reasoning Perception Framework (PRPF)}, a two-stage framework built on perceiving before reasoning. PRPF introduces a lightweight Multimodal Proactive Perceptor (MPP) for intervention gating and context compression, and activates the Proactive Agent Reasoner (PAR) only when intervention is warranted. Experiments on the ProactiveMobile benchmark show that PRPF substantially reduces false trigger rates (FTR) while improving success rates (SR) and inference efficiency over the ProactiveMobile baseline.

AI.tldr: They propose a two-stage “perceive before reason” framework so mobile agents only invoke heavy MLLM reasoning when intervention is truly needed, reducing false triggers and cost while improving assistance quality.
AI.motivation: Proactive mobile assistants must first decide when to intervene, then how to help. Current MLLM-based agents entangle these decisions in one pipeline, causing conflicts between cautious intervention and rich assistance, and wasting computation when they should stay silent. A better architecture is needed to decouple lightweight triggering from heavyweight reasoning.
AI.method: They introduce the Pre-Reasoning Perception Framework (PRPF), which splits the agent into two stages: (1) a lightweight Multimodal Proactive Perceptor (MPP) that performs intervention gating (deciding whether to act) and context compression, processing multimodal inputs cheaply; and (2) a Proactive Agent Reasoner (PAR), a heavier MLLM-based module that is only activated when MPP decides that intervention is warranted, then generates the actual assistance. They evaluate on the ProactiveMobile benchmark against a unified-pipeline baseline.
AI.result: On the ProactiveMobile benchmark, PRPF lowers false trigger rates (fewer unnecessary interventions), while simultaneously increasing success rates (more effective help when it does act) and improving inference efficiency (less redundant heavy MLLM computation) compared to the ProactiveMobile baseline system.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling perception/gating from reasoning in proactive mobile agents—via a lightweight multimodal perceptor followed by a conditional heavy reasoner—better aligns the goals of cautious intervention and comprehensive assistance. This architecture reduces false triggers, boosts task success, and improves efficiency for multimodal mobile assistants.

title: MemTrain: Self-Supervised Context Memory Training
summary: Memory is an indispensable capability for long-horizon LLM agents, enabling them to preserve and utilize information accumulated across extended interactions. Existing memory-agent approaches are typically trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning on downstream tasks. However, collecting high-quality annotated problems for memory-intensive scenarios is costly, and the resulting training data often lack sufficient diversity to cover general memory behaviors. In this work, we propose MemTrain, a self-supervised training framework for generally enhancing the context-memory capability of LLM agents for more effective downstream post-training. MemTrain introduces two coupled proxy tasks over unlabeled Wikipedia corpora: (1) an end-to-end masked reconstruction objective, which requires the model to recover masked entities after multiple rounds of memory updates, thereby encouraging memory maintenance from the final outcome perspective; and (2) an intermediate memory recall objective, which requires the model to reconstruct masked historical information using intermediate memory states, encouraging faithful compression and memory completeness throughout the interaction process. The two objectives are jointly optimized using GRPO. Extensive experiments on long-text QA and search-based QA benchmarks demonstrate that MemTrain consistently improves downstream memory-intensive reasoning performance across different models, achieving gains of up to 17.67 points over direct task-specific post-training.

AI.tldr: MemTrain is a self-supervised framework that improves LLM agents’ long-horizon memory using proxy tasks on unlabeled text instead of expensive RL on downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents need robust memory to handle extended interactions, but current methods rely on end-to-end RL with curated, memory-intensive downstream tasks. Such data are costly to collect and often lack diversity, so models tend to overfit to specific scenarios and fail to develop general memory skills. A scalable, label-free way to train general-purpose memory mechanisms is needed.
AI.method: MemTrain uses unlabeled Wikipedia text to define two coupled proxy objectives that simulate long-horizon memory use. (1) A masked reconstruction objective: entities are masked and the model must recover them after several rounds of interaction and memory updates, encouraging effective long-term retention from the final-task perspective. (2) An intermediate memory recall objective: the model must reconstruct masked historical information based on intermediate memory states, encouraging faithful compression and completeness of memory during the interaction. These two self-supervised tasks are jointly optimized with GRPO, an RL-style optimization method, to train the agent’s memory-update and usage policies without task-specific labels.
AI.result: On long-text QA and search-based QA benchmarks that stress memory-intensive reasoning, MemTrain consistently boosts performance across multiple base models. It achieves improvements of up to 17.67 points compared with directly applying task-specific post-training alone, showing that its general memory training transfers effectively to diverse downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised proxy tasks over large unlabeled corpora can effectively train general-purpose memory mechanisms in LLM agents. By jointly optimizing final-outcome masked reconstruction and intermediate memory recall with GRPO, MemTrain builds stronger context-memory capabilities that substantially enhance downstream memory-intensive reasoning, while avoiding the cost and narrowness of annotated RL training on specific tasks.

title: Solipsistic Superintelligence is Unlikely to be Cooperative
comment: 24 pages, 1 figure, Accepted at Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning, 2026
summary: AI's central challenge is shifting from capability to coexistence. The dominant paradigm in AI research focuses on developing powerful agents that treat the world as an exogenous and stationary source of feedback. We contend that superintelligence, an extremely capable task solver, born out of such a solipsistic approach to AI design, is unlikely to be cooperative. Deploying AI systems induces endogenous non-stationarity, resulting in a train-test-deploy gap where historical distributions diverge from the deployment context. We refer to this as the self-undermining property of unilateral optimization. Closing this gap requires AI that participates in cooperation: the equilibrium-selection process through which multiple actors navigate their interdependence. We call for a non-solipsistic research paradigm that treats this interdependence as a core design principle rather than approaching cooperation as a task to solve. This entails building dynamic evaluation testbeds involving adaptive counterparties, treating institutions as design primitives, and preserving human agency as a structural feature of the systems we build.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that as AI systems grow more capable, the core problem becomes how they coexist and cooperate with humans and other agents, rather than just improving raw capabilities. It critiques the current “solipsistic” paradigm that optimizes in a static world, and proposes a new paradigm where AI is explicitly designed as a participant in cooperative, institutional, and human-centered dynamics.
AI.motivation: Current AI research largely treats the environment as exogenous and stationary, assuming that better optimization in this environment directly yields better outcomes. However, once powerful AI systems are deployed, they themselves change the environment and incentives, creating a gap between training and deployment and risking uncooperative, misaligned behavior—especially in the limit of “superintelligent” systems. The authors are motivated to solve this mismatch and to prevent self-undermining dynamics of unilateral optimization.
AI.method: This is a conceptual/theoretical paper. The authors introduce and analyze the idea of the “self-undermining property of unilateral optimization” and argue, using game-theoretic and institutional reasoning, that AI needs to be framed as a participant in cooperative equilibrium selection among multiple agents. They then outline design principles for a non-solipsistic paradigm, including dynamic evaluation testbeds with adaptive counterparties, explicit institutional design, and embedding human agency structurally in AI systems.
AI.result: The paper’s main results are conceptual contributions: (1) identification and formalization of the train-test-deploy gap caused by endogenous non-stationarity when powerful AI systems are deployed; (2) the notion that unilateral optimization leads to self-undermining behavior and risks non-cooperative superintelligence; and (3) a proposed research agenda that reframes cooperation not as a downstream task but as a core design principle, supported by concrete directions for testbeds and system design.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that continued focus on building ever more capable unilateral optimizers is unsafe and conceptually misguided in a world where AI systems affect and are affected by other agents and institutions. To build cooperative and safe advanced AI (including potential superintelligence), research must adopt a non-solipsistic paradigm that treats interdependence, cooperation, institutions, and human agency as first-class design objects. This shift requires new evaluation environments, institutional primitives, and structural safeguards to ensure AI systems participate constructively in cooperative equilibria.

title: AI Rater Discrimination Depends on Scoring Protocol in Complex Clinical Decision-Making
comment: 11 pages, 4 main figures, 8 supplementary figures, 9 supplementary tables
summary: Clinical AI evaluation increasingly delegates scoring to large language models (LLMs) acting as AI raters, yet their scoring behavior across evaluation conditions has not been quantitatively characterized. We address this gap through a factorial study of AI rater behavior in adult type 2 diabetes (T2D) pharmacotherapy at 12-month outpatient follow-up, a clinical task involving complex decision-making operationalized across seven evaluation questions. Four open-source LLMs served simultaneously as clinical decision support system (CDSS) models and AI raters. Each CDSS output was scored under two scoring protocols: a rubric-anchored Gold Rubric (GR) protocol incorporating a patient-specific rubric, and a rubric-free Non Gold Rubric (Non-GR) protocol. Linear mixed effects models crossed the scoring protocol factor with five design factors -- CDSS model, CDSS prompt configuration (document-referenced generation [DRG] vs.\ Baseline), rater model, prompt character, and prompt type -- and estimated main effects together with their protocol interactions. Across all questions, AI raters yielded consistently higher scores within a very narrow range (74--78 points on average) under Non-GR compared to those under GR (7.69 to 49.64 points lower mean scores; 1.68 to 3.67 times wider interquartile ranges). Within each question, GR amplified the AI rater's discrimination between DRG and Baseline CDSS outputs by factors of 1.76 to 5.10, while also revealing substantial behavioral variation across rater models that Non-GR suppressed. These findings support rubric anchoring as the scoring protocol that preserves discriminative power in clinical AI evaluation; rubric-free scoring cannot substitute when questions require patient-specific or jurisdiction-specific criteria that rater models cannot infer from parametric knowledge alone.

AI.tldr: The paper quantitatively studies how LLMs behave as scorers (“AI raters”) in clinical AI evaluation and finds that rubric-anchored scoring preserves discrimination, while rubric-free scoring inflates and compresses scores.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to score clinical AI systems, we lack a quantitative understanding of how reliable and discriminative their scoring is under different evaluation setups, especially for complex, patient-specific decision-making tasks.
AI.method: The authors run a factorial study in a type 2 diabetes pharmacotherapy follow-up task with seven evaluation questions. Four open-source LLMs act both as clinical decision support (CDSS) generators and as AI raters. Each CDSS output is scored under two protocols: (1) Gold Rubric (GR), which uses a patient-specific rubric, and (2) Non-Gold Rubric (Non-GR), which is rubric-free. They fit linear mixed effects models with factors: scoring protocol, CDSS model, CDSS prompt configuration (document-referenced vs baseline), rater model, prompt character, and prompt type, and analyze main effects and interactions.
AI.result: AI raters systematically give higher and tightly clustered scores (around 74–78) under rubric-free Non-GR, compared to much lower and more dispersed scores under GR, with GR scores on average 7.69 to 49.64 points lower and having 1.68 to 3.67 times wider IQRs. GR also sharpens the contrast between document-referenced and baseline CDSS outputs by factors of 1.76–5.10 and exposes substantial differences between rater models that remain hidden under Non-GR.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-anchored scoring is crucial for preserving discriminative power and revealing genuine performance differences in clinical AI evaluation. Rubric-free LLM scoring tends to inflate and compress scores, obscuring differences between systems and raters, and is inadequate when evaluation depends on patient- or jurisdiction-specific criteria that are not explicit in the model’s internal knowledge.

title: Do Real-World Datasets Contain Natural Experiments? An Empirical Study Using Causal Feature Selection
summary: In nature, events that affect some individuals or groups but not others constitute an implicit intervention and are known as natural experiments. For example, the COVID-19 pandemic was an intervention by the coronavirus on the sub-population infected with COVID. We ask, do natural experiments occur in existing real-world datasets? If yes, how should we treat them? To detect natural experiments in data, we use causal discovery to recover the underlying causal graph and perform feature selection based on causal links. If downstream performance improves by treating the data as interventional rather than observational, we argue that this suggests the dataset contains natural experiments. We first validate this hypothesis by simulating datasets with and without natural experiments using synthetic graphs. We then perform a systematic empirical evaluation on a large suite of real-world datasets. Our results indicate that real-world datasets do contain natural experiments and we can take advantage of those natural experiments to improve model performance using causal inference. Our work represents the initial foray into this area, offering a preliminary exploration within a limited scope.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether existing real-world datasets contain implicit interventions (natural experiments) and shows they can be exploited to improve model performance using causal inference methods.
AI.motivation: Natural experiments, where some units are affected by an intervention and others are not, are powerful for causal inference but are usually studied when explicitly known (e.g., COVID-19 infection). It is unclear whether such implicit interventions exist in standard machine learning datasets and, if they do, how to detect and leverage them. The authors aim to bridge this gap by providing a way to detect natural experiments inside arbitrary datasets and to test whether treating data as interventional yields benefits.
AI.method: 1) Use causal discovery methods to reconstruct an approximate causal graph from a given dataset. 2) From this graph, perform feature selection based on causal relationships (rather than purely associational criteria). 3) Evaluate predictive models under two regimes: treating the data as purely observational vs. as interventional (i.e., explicitly accounting for detected natural experiments). 4) Validate the approach on synthetic data where the presence/absence of natural experiments is controlled. 5) Conduct a systematic empirical study on many real-world datasets to see when and how the interventional treatment improves downstream performance.
AI.result: On synthetic data, when natural experiments are present, models that treat the data as interventional (using the causal-graph-based feature selection) achieve better performance than purely observational treatments; when no natural experiments are present, such gains do not appear, supporting the method’s validity. On a wide range of real-world datasets, the authors find performance improvements consistent with the presence of natural experiments, suggesting that such implicit interventions are indeed common in practice and can be exploited.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides initial evidence that natural experiments are embedded in many standard real-world datasets and that causal-discovery-based techniques can detect and leverage them to improve predictive modeling. This constitutes an early, limited-scope exploration, but it opens a new direction: systematically searching for and exploiting natural experiments in arbitrary datasets to enhance causal and predictive performance.

title: Sample-Size Scaling of the African Languages NLI Evaluation
comment: Accepted at the AfricaNLP Workshop, EACL 2026
summary: African languages have very little labelled data, and it is unclear if augmenting the quantity of annotation data reliably enhances downstream performance. The study is a systematic sample-size scaling study of natural language inference (NLI) on 16 African languages based on the AfriXNLI benchmark. Under controlled conditions, two multilingual transformer models with roughly 0.6B parameters XLM-R Large fine-tuned on XNLI and AfroXLM-R Large are tested on sample sizes of between 50 and 500 labeled examples and average their results across random subsampling runs. As opposed to the usual belief of monotonic increase with increased data, we find a strongly language sensitive and often non-monotonic scaling behavior. Some languages show early saturation or decrease in performance with sample size as well as high variance in low resource regimes. These results indicate that the volume of data is not enough to guarantee stable profits to African NLI, creating the necessity of language sensitive datasets creation and stronger multi-lingual modelling strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how increasing labeled data size affects natural language inference performance in 16 African languages and finds that more data does not always lead to better results; effects are language-sensitive and often non-monotonic.
AI.motivation: African languages suffer from a scarcity of labeled data for NLP tasks, and it is commonly assumed that adding more annotated data will steadily improve model performance. However, this assumption has not been rigorously tested for African languages and NLI, especially under controlled scaling of sample sizes. The authors want to understand whether simply increasing the number of labeled examples reliably benefits performance, and how this effect varies across different African languages.
AI.method: The authors perform a systematic sample-size scaling study on the AfriXNLI benchmark covering 16 African languages. They evaluate two multilingual transformer models of similar size (~0.6B parameters): XLM-R Large fine-tuned on XNLI, and AfroXLM-R Large. For each language and model, they train with varying numbers of labeled NLI examples, from 50 to 500, using random subsampling to create multiple runs per sample size. They then average performance metrics across these runs to analyze scaling behavior while controlling other factors.
AI.result: The study reveals that performance scaling with additional labeled data is highly language dependent and often non-monotonic. Some languages exhibit early saturation of performance, where adding more data yields little or no benefit, while others even show decreases in performance as sample size grows. There is also high performance variance in low-resource regimes. Overall, the observed behavior contradicts the standard expectation that more annotated data always leads to steady performance gains.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that simply increasing the volume of labeled data is insufficient to ensure stable and improved NLI performance for African languages. The authors argue that dataset creation must be more language-sensitive, accounting for linguistic and data characteristics of each language, and that more robust multilingual modeling strategies are needed to effectively support African languages in NLI tasks.

title: Distilling Answer-Set Programming Rules from LLMs for Neurosymbolic Visual Question Answering
comment: Under consideration in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: Visual Question Answering (VQA) is the task of answering questions about images, requiring the integration of multimodal input and reasoning. Modular approaches that incorporate logic-based representations into the reasoning component offer clear advantages over end-to-end trained systems, particularly in terms of interpretability. However, adapting or extending these representations when task requirements change can place a significant burden on developers. To address this challenge, we present an approach for distilling rules from Large Language Models (LLMs). Our method prompts an LLM to extend an initial VQA reasoning theory, expressed as an answer-set program, to meet new requirements of the task. Examples from VQA datasets guide the LLM, validate the results, and help correct erroneous rules by leveraging feedback from the ASP solver. We demonstrate that our approach is effective across diverse VQA datasets. Notably, only a few examples are needed to elicit correct rules from LLMs. Our experiments suggest that rule distillation from LLMs is a promising alternative to traditional data-driven rule learning approaches. Under consideration in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP).

AI.tldr: They use large language models to automatically generate and refine logical rules (in answer-set programming form) for visual question answering, making modular VQA systems easier to adapt while keeping them interpretable.
AI.motivation: Modular, logic-based VQA systems are interpretable, but updating their rule sets when task requirements change is labor-intensive and requires expert knowledge. The authors want a way to automate or greatly simplify the process of creating and maintaining these logical reasoning theories, especially when moving to new datasets or adding new types of questions.
AI.method: They start from an initial logic-based reasoning theory for VQA, encoded as an answer-set program (ASP). They then prompt a large language model to propose rule extensions or modifications so that the theory can handle new task requirements or datasets. Example VQA instances provide supervision: the LLM is guided with examples, its proposed rules are checked with an ASP solver, and solver feedback (e.g., failures or wrong answers) is used to refine and correct the rules through further interaction with the LLM. This yields a distillation process where rules are incrementally improved and distilled from the LLM into an explicit symbolic theory.
AI.result: Across multiple VQA datasets, the distilled rule sets work effectively, meeting new reasoning requirements with only a small number of examples needed to guide the LLM. The experiments show that the LLM-generated rules are generally correct and useful, and that this approach can match or approximate the performance of more traditional, data-intensive rule-learning methods while being more sample-efficient.
AI.conclusion: Rule distillation from LLMs into ASP-based reasoning modules is a viable and promising alternative to conventional, data-driven rule learning in VQA. It maintains interpretability, reduces the manual effort needed to adapt logical theories to new tasks or datasets, and requires only a few examples to derive accurate new rules. This suggests a broader future role for LLMs as assistants in building and evolving symbolic reasoning components in multimodal systems.

title: WebRISE: Requirement-Induced State Evaluation for MLLM-Generated Web Artifacts
summary: Existing benchmarks for MLLM-generated web artifacts assess interaction through local evidence and miss the requirement-induced states and transitions that determine whether a page works. We introduce WebRISE, which compiles task requirements into Interaction Contract Graphs (ICGs) of observable states, user-intent transitions, and DOM/visual assertions for implementation-agnostic browser execution. WebRISE spans 442 tasks across five input modalities (Text, Markdown, Sketch, Image, Video), with 5,495 transitions and 5,271 requirement checks that separate user-stated functions from implicit product-level constraints. Across 14 MLLMs, even the strongest model reaches only 65.6% transition validity and 66.3% requirement coverage, and visual quality is no proxy for behavior (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B on Markdown: V=80.8 yet T=15.5). Video gives the strongest interaction signal (+10.6 pp implicit coverage over Text), while implicit constraints persist; defect injection shows ICG-based scoring detects state errors at 2-16x the rate of checkpoint-style evaluation.

AI.tldr: WebRISE is a new benchmark that evaluates whether web pages generated by multimodal LLMs truly satisfy interaction requirements, not just look good.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of MLLM-generated web pages mostly rely on local cues or surface-level signals like visual quality or simple checkpoint tests. These miss whether a page actually supports the required user interactions and respects both explicit and implicit product constraints. There is a need for a requirement-centric, interaction-aware framework to measure functional correctness of generated web artifacts across modalities.
AI.method: The authors design WebRISE, which converts task requirements into Interaction Contract Graphs (ICGs). An ICG encodes observable states, user-intent transitions between those states, and DOM/visual assertions that must hold, but is agnostic to the specific implementation. They build a benchmark of 442 tasks over five input modalities (Text, Markdown, Sketch, Image, Video). For each task they define transitions and requirement checks that distinguish explicit user-requested functions from implicit product-level constraints. They then evaluate 14 MLLMs on these tasks, measuring transition validity, requirement coverage, and comparing ICG-based detection of errors with simpler checkpoint-style evaluation, including via defect injection experiments.
AI.result: WebRISE contains 442 tasks, 5,495 transitions, and 5,271 requirement checks across five modalities. Across 14 MLLMs, the best model attains only about 65.6% transition validity and 66.3% requirement coverage, showing substantial room for improvement. Visual quality does not reliably indicate correct behavior; for example, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B scores 80.8 in visual quality on Markdown tasks but only 15.5 in transition validity. Video input gives the richest interaction signal, improving implicit requirement coverage by 10.6 percentage points over text, yet many implicit constraints still fail. In defect injection experiments, ICG-based scoring detects state-level errors 2–16 times more often than checkpoint-style evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Requirement-driven, interaction-centric evaluation reveals significant gaps in the functional reliability of MLLM-generated web artifacts that are invisible to visual-only or local checks. WebRISE and its Interaction Contract Graphs provide a more sensitive and implementation-agnostic way to test whether generated pages truly satisfy both explicit and implicit product requirements, especially in interactive, multimodal settings.

title: A Negative Result on Cross-Model Activation Transfer in a Pythia Multi-Hop Setting
summary: Recent work shows that language models can transmit behavioural traits through hidden signals in generated data during training. We ask whether a more direct and stricter channel is also viable: can one language model communicate useful intermediate reasoning state to another at inference time by translating and injecting hidden activations, rather than by passing natural-language text? We test this question in a controlled Pythia-160M to Pythia-410M multi-hop reasoning setting. A linear translation layer learns a strong normalized-space map between sender and receiver hidden states, with normalized cosine similarity near 0.97 across seeds. However, when the translated activations are injected into the receiver at inference time, they do not improve downstream answering. Low-strength additive injection remains near the no-injection baseline, with confidence intervals that cross zero. Replacement-style injection is consistently destructive, and rescaling translated vectors to the receiver hidden-state norm does not rescue performance. The result is therefore a scoped negative result: in this setting, offline representational alignment is not sufficient for useful causal communication inside the receiver.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether one language model can directly transmit intermediate reasoning states to another via hidden activations, rather than natural language, and finds that even with strong representational alignment, such activation injection does not improve performance.
AI.motivation: While prior work shows language models can transmit behavioral traits through hidden signals in training data, it is unclear whether a stricter, direct channel of communication—via internal activations at inference time—can enable useful sharing of reasoning states. Understanding this would clarify whether aligned internal representations are sufficient for causal information transfer between models.
AI.method: The authors use a controlled multi-hop reasoning setup involving a smaller Pythia-160M model as sender and a larger Pythia-410M model as receiver. They train a linear translation layer to map hidden states from the sender to the receiver’s representational space, achieving high normalized cosine similarity (~0.97). They then inject these translated activations into the receiver model at inference time using both low-strength additive and replacement-style methods, with and without rescaling to match the receiver’s hidden-state norms. They evaluate downstream QA performance to see if the injected information helps.
AI.result: The translation layer achieves very strong alignment between sender and receiver hidden states in normalized space, with cosine similarity around 0.97 across random seeds. However, injecting these translated activations into the receiver does not improve performance: low-strength additive injection results stay close to the no-injection baseline and have confidence intervals crossing zero, while replacement-style injections predictably harm performance. Rescaling translated vectors to match receiver norm also fails to help.
AI.conclusion: The study finds that even highly aligned representations between models are not enough, by themselves, to enable effective causal communication through activation injection. In this controlled Pythia-160M-to-410M multi-hop reasoning setting, offline representational alignment does not translate into useful sharing of intermediate reasoning states, yielding a scoped negative result and suggesting that additional structure or mechanisms are needed for such communication.

title: LEAP: Supercharging LLMs for Formal Mathematics with Agentic Frameworks
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong informal mathematical reasoning but struggle to generate mechanically verifiable proofs in formal languages like Lean. We present LEAP, an agentic framework that enables general-purpose foundation models to achieve state-of-the-art performance on automated formal theorem proving. LEAP leverages foundation model capabilities, such as informal reasoning, instruction following, and iterative self-refinement. By decomposing complex problems into smaller units, the system bridges formal proof construction with informal blueprints through continuous interaction with the Lean compiler. To provide a rigorous evaluation beyond increasingly saturated benchmarks, we introduce Lean-IMO-Bench, a benchmark of IMO-style problems formalized in Lean, with short statements yet highly non-routine and multi-step proofs across a wide range of difficulty levels. Empirically, on the latest 2025 Putnam Competition, an annual mathematics competition for undergraduate students in North America, LEAP solves all 12 problems, matching recent breakthroughs by frontier formal mathematical models. On Lean-IMO-Bench, LEAP boosts the one-shot formal solve rate of general-purpose LLMs from below 10% to 70%, notably surpassing the 48% benchmark set by a specialized, gold-medal-caliber IMO system. Furthermore, we demonstrate LEAP's research-level utility by autonomously formalizing complex proofs for open combinatorial challenges, including a verified proof for a key subproblem in Knuth's Hamiltonian decomposition of even-order Cayley graphs.

AI.tldr: LEAP is an agentic framework that lets general-purpose LLMs produce Lean proofs at state-of-the-art levels, using decomposition, interaction with the Lean compiler, and self-refinement, and is evaluated on a new Lean-IMO benchmark and real competitions.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs are good at informal mathematical reasoning but weak at generating fully formal, mechanically checkable proofs in systems like Lean. Benchmarks are becoming saturated and often do not reflect the difficulty and non-routine nature of advanced competition and research-level problems. The authors want a method and evaluation setting that better tests and improves genuine formal theorem-proving ability on hard problems.
AI.method: They design LEAP, an agentic framework that wraps a general-purpose foundation model. LEAP repeatedly interacts with the Lean proof assistant, decomposes complex theorems into smaller subgoals, constructs informal reasoning blueprints, and iteratively refines proof attempts based on compiler feedback. The framework exploits instruction-following and self-refinement capabilities of LLMs and tightly couples them with Lean’s formal environment. They also create Lean-IMO-Bench, a new benchmark of IMO-style problems formalized in Lean, to evaluate performance on non-routine, multi-step proofs at varying difficulty levels.
AI.result: On the 2025 Putnam competition problems, LEAP solves all 12 problems, matching frontier systems specialized for formal mathematics. On Lean-IMO-Bench, LEAP raises the one-shot formal solve rate of general-purpose LLMs from under 10% to 70%, beating a prior specialized IMO system that achieved 48%. LEAP is also able to autonomously formalize sophisticated proofs for open combinatorial challenges, including a checked proof of a subproblem in Knuth’s Hamiltonian decomposition conjecture for even-order Cayley graphs.
AI.conclusion: An agentic, decomposition-based framework tightly integrated with a proof assistant can transform general-purpose LLMs into highly capable formal theorem provers, rivaling specialized systems on competition-level problems and contributing to research-level mathematics. The new Lean-IMO-Bench benchmark provides a more rigorous and challenging testbed for future work in automated formal mathematics.

title: Benchmarking Speech-to-Speech Translation Models
summary: Speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) has advanced rapidly, but offline evaluation lacks a unified protocol: studies report non-overlapping metric subsets, preventing direct comparisons. We introduce COMPASS, a unified and reproducible benchmarking framework integrating 46 metrics across eight dimensions, and deploy it on 1,248 model-language configurations from FLEURS and CVSS, spanning cascaded and end-to-end architectures over ten language pairs. Architectures exhibit complementary strengths: best-vs-worst gaps exceed 30\% on naturalness and speaker preservation but remain within a few points on translation quality, so single-metric rankings systematically misrepresent system quality. Correlation filtering reduces 46 metrics to 10 per direction, with three axes requiring different metrics across X$\to$EN and EN$\to$X (e.g., TER/UTMOS vs. ChrF++/NISQA-MOS); these subsets preserve rankings (Spearman's $ρ>0.80$) while cutting evaluation time by $\approx 2.5\times$. Human validation across dubbing, podcasts, and medical domains shows standalone MOS predictors fail to predict listener preference, while top domain-specific metrics correlate with human judgment ($ρ\geq 0.90$). We release COMPASS as a foundation for domain-aware S2ST evaluation.

AI.tldr: They propose COMPASS, a unified benchmark with many metrics to fairly evaluate speech-to-speech translation systems and show it captures complementary strengths of different architectures.
AI.motivation: Current speech-to-speech translation research uses differing, non-overlapping evaluation metrics, making results non-comparable and hiding trade-offs between aspects like naturalness, speaker preservation, and translation quality.
AI.method: They build COMPASS, a benchmarking framework that integrates 46 metrics across eight evaluation dimensions, apply it to 1,248 model-language configurations from FLEURS and CVSS datasets over ten language pairs for both cascaded and end-to-end systems, analyze metric correlations to select compact metric subsets per translation direction, and validate metric effectiveness with human studies across multiple domains.
AI.result: They find large variability between systems in naturalness and speaker preservation but relatively small gaps in translation quality; single metrics misrepresent overall quality. Correlation analysis reduces 46 metrics to about 10 direction-specific metrics that still preserve system rankings (Spearman’s ρ>0.80) and cut evaluation time by about 2.5x. Human experiments show generic MOS predictors don’t align with listener preferences, while domain-specific metrics can correlate strongly (ρ≥0.90) with human judgments.
AI.conclusion: COMPASS offers a unified, domain-aware evaluation framework for speech-to-speech translation that captures complementary system strengths, enables efficient yet reliable metric subsets, and aligns better with human preferences than using standalone MOS predictors, providing a solid foundation for future S2ST evaluation and comparison.

title: The Reliability Gap in Benchmark Auditing: Distribution Shift and Scale as Failure Modes of Contamination Detection
summary: Benchmark contamination, where evaluation examples appear in a model's training data, threatens the validity of LLM assessment. Statistical tools for detecting training-data membership exist, but have been validated almost exclusively in controlled academic regimes: large, homogeneous pre-training corpora and transparent, single-stage training pipelines. Whether these methods remain reliable in realistic auditing scenarios remains unclear. We identify two under-studied failure modes: distribution shift, which arises when suspect and validation sets violate the IID assumption, and scale constraints, which arise because benchmarks are orders of magnitude smaller than pre-training corpora. We systematically evaluate three leading paradigms: LLM Dataset Inference, Post-Hoc Dataset Inference, and CoDeC across 27 models from multiple families (including Pythia, OLMo~2, and specialised cultural and medical LLMs) and scales (up to 27B). We then further extend our analysis to frontier industry models. Across 335 evaluations, only 199 yield correct outcomes. LLM Dataset Inference results in false positives under distribution shift, Post-Hoc Dataset Inference is underpowered at benchmark scale, and CoDeC provides only coarse provenance signals that are insufficient to verify individual benchmark splits. Our results reveal a systematic reliability gap between controlled validation and practical benchmark auditing, and show that statistical detection cannot yet replace transparent data provenance. We open-source our benchmark for further research.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how reliable current statistical methods are for detecting benchmark contamination in large language models under realistic conditions and finds they often fail, highlighting the need for better tools and transparent data provenance.
AI.motivation: Benchmark contamination undermines the credibility of LLM evaluations, but existing detection methods have mostly been tested only in idealized, controlled settings (large, clean, transparent training corpora). It is unknown whether these methods work in realistic, messy audit scenarios where data distributions shift and benchmarks are tiny relative to pretraining data. The paper aims to test and characterize the reliability of these methods in such practical conditions.
AI.method: The authors define and study two key failure modes for contamination detection tools: (1) distribution shift between suspect and validation sets, violating IID assumptions, and (2) scale constraints due to benchmarks being much smaller than pretraining data. They systematically benchmark three main paradigms—LLM Dataset Inference, Post-Hoc Dataset Inference, and CoDeC—across 27 open models from several families (Pythia, OLMo 2, specialized cultural and medical LLMs) and sizes up to 27B parameters, then extend evaluation to proprietary frontier models. They perform 335 separate evaluations to measure true/false positives and negatives under various settings.
AI.result: Out of 335 contamination detection evaluations, only 199 are correct, indicating significant unreliability. LLM Dataset Inference tends to produce false positives when there is distribution shift. Post-Hoc Dataset Inference tends to be statistically underpowered at realistic benchmark scales and often fails to confidently detect or rule out contamination. CoDeC can only provide very coarse-grained provenance information and cannot reliably verify whether specific benchmark splits were present in training data. These behaviors are observed across multiple model families and scales, and also appear in frontier closed models.
AI.conclusion: Current statistical tools for detecting benchmark contamination in LLMs, though promising in controlled experiments, are not reliably accurate in realistic auditing settings due to distribution shift and scale limitations. There is a substantial reliability gap between academic validation regimes and real-world benchmark auditing needs. As a result, statistical detection cannot yet be relied upon as a substitute for transparent data documentation and provenance; more robust methods and open benchmarks (which the authors release) are needed for trustworthy LLM evaluation.

title: When Does Complexity Conditioning Help a Frozen Sentence Embedding? A Controlled Study of Per-Sentence and Pair-Level Difficulty Adaptation
summary: A common intuition is that sentence embeddings should adapt to the difficulty of the input. We test this intuition in a controlled, multi-seed setting: a lightweight post-encoder adapter attaches to a frozen Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B encoder, accessing only its final pooled embedding, and is evaluated on four paraphrase and semantic-similarity tasks (PAWS, MRPC, QQP, STS-B).
