The naive form of the idea fails: surface-based per-sentence complexity is nearly uncorrelated with frozen-baseline error (Pearson approximately 0.05) and provides no advantage over constant or shuffled controls, while degrading a saturated baseline. Even when the target is aligned to a non-circular pair-difficulty signal, the per-sentence gate still cannot reliably capture difficulty because difficulty is primarily a property of the pair, not the individual sentence. In contrast, a small pair-level residual gated by a held-out cross-encoder difficulty signal yields consistent gains on the larger and graded tasks, including +0.022 Spearman on STS-B and +0.037 on QQP, while remaining anchored to the frozen baseline across all seeds. Because this useful form operates on sentence pairs rather than individual sentences, the resulting model is best understood as a lightweight re-ranker over cached frozen embeddings, not a replacement single-vector embedding; we make no state-of-the-art claim. Our contribution is a controlled account of when difficulty-aware adaptation helps and when it fails, together with a pre-training diagnostic that predicts the available headroom.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether making sentence embeddings adapt to per-sentence difficulty improves performance and finds that naive sentence-level difficulty-aware adaptation fails, whereas a pair-level, difficulty-gated residual works modestly well as a re-ranker over frozen embeddings.
AI.motivation: There is a common intuition that sentence embeddings should reflect how difficult the input is, potentially improving performance on semantic similarity and paraphrase tasks. However, it is unclear when and how such difficulty-aware adaptation is actually beneficial, especially in a controlled setting using strong frozen encoders.
AI.method: The authors attach a lightweight post-encoder adapter to a frozen Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B encoder, accessing only its final pooled embedding. They run controlled multi-seed experiments on PAWS, MRPC, QQP, and STS-B. They first try a per-sentence gate conditioned on surface-based complexity measures and compare this against constant and shuffled controls. They then align the target to a non-circular pair-difficulty signal and finally introduce a small pair-level residual module gated by a held-out cross-encoder difficulty signal, effectively re-ranking sentence pairs using cached frozen embeddings.
AI.result: Surface-based per-sentence complexity is nearly uncorrelated with the frozen baseline’s error (Pearson ≈ 0.05), yields no gains over simple baselines, and even harms a saturated frozen baseline. Even when the target is a better non-circular pair-difficulty signal, the per-sentence gate fails to capture difficulty because difficulty mainly resides at the sentence-pair level. In contrast, a small pair-level residual, gated by a held-out cross-encoder difficulty signal, produces consistent improvements on larger, graded tasks: about +0.022 Spearman on STS-B and +0.037 on QQP, while keeping performance close to the frozen baseline across random seeds.
AI.conclusion: Naively making sentence embeddings difficulty-aware at the individual sentence level does not help and can hurt, as difficulty is fundamentally a property of pairs rather than single sentences for paraphrase and similarity tasks. A more effective approach is to add a lightweight, pair-level, difficulty-gated residual that acts as a re-ranker on top of frozen embeddings, leading to modest, reliable gains. The paper offers a controlled characterization of the conditions under which difficulty-aware adaptation is useful and proposes a pre-training diagnostic to estimate how much improvement is possible (headroom).

title: The Violation Situation Pattern: A Knowledge-Graph Pattern for Compliance Violations
summary: Compliance pipelines detect violations as transient query results and do not keep the violation itself as a persistent graph object with review state, affected entities, or audit history. The Violation Situation Pattern (VSP) closes this gap. Building on the Situation pattern of Gangemi and Mika, VSP reifies each detected violation as a graph node with a rule identifier, a temporal validity interval, a lifecycle state, and evidence links to the entities involved. Lifecycle transitions are stored as immutable, PROV-O-aligned events, so audit history is a graph traversal. We instantiate VSP in a legal entity and contract lifecycle property graph and operationalize four deontic rules (V1 unauthorized signature, V2 expired mandate, V3 missing confidentiality clause, V4 missing breach-notification clause) through an FCL->Cypher->MERGE pipeline. We check V1 and V2 against BODACC corporate-officer publications, evaluate V4 on 73 GDPRhub enforcement decisions, and run a SHACL cross-formalism check on V3 and V4. The central finding is rule-body independence: extending V4 from clause-presence to deadline checking raises F1 from 0.312 to 0.602, while the pattern's identity, lifecycle, and evidence semantics stay the same. This separates a pattern contribution from a detector contribution, so detection logic can evolve without invalidating accumulated audit history.

AI.tldr: They propose a reusable graph pattern (Violation Situation Pattern) that turns each detected compliance violation into a first-class node with lifecycle and evidence, enabling persistent audit trails independent of the detection rules.
AI.motivation: Existing compliance systems treat violations as ephemeral query results and do not model them as persistent entities with states, links to affected objects, and auditable histories. This makes it hard to manage violations over time, track how they were handled, or evolve detection rules without breaking existing records.
AI.method: They extend Gangemi and Mika’s Situation pattern into a Violation Situation Pattern (VSP), where each violation is reified as a node annotated with rule ID, temporal validity, lifecycle state, and evidence links. Lifecycle changes are captured as immutable PROV-O-compliant events. They instantiate this in a property graph modeling legal entities and contracts, encode four deontic rules (unauthorized signature, expired mandate, missing confidentiality clause, missing breach-notification clause) via an FCL-to-Cypher-to-MERGE pipeline, and test the rules on corporate officer publications and GDPR enforcement decisions, also validating across formalisms with SHACL.
AI.result: The VSP can be instantiated in a property graph and populated via rule pipelines (FCL->Cypher->MERGE). The rules detect concrete violations over real datasets (BODACC and GDPRhub). When the body of one rule (V4) is strengthened from mere presence of a clause to checking its deadline, the detection performance improves substantially (F1 from 0.312 to 0.602), while the core pattern of how violations are represented and tracked remains unchanged.
AI.conclusion: The Violation Situation Pattern cleanly separates the representation of violations (identity, lifecycle, evidence, audit history) from the logic that detects them. This rule-body independence means that detection rules can evolve and improve without invalidating previously stored violations or their audit trails, making VSP a robust foundation for compliance and legal-knowledge graph systems.

title: Structures Facilitate Retrieve, Rerank, and Generate
summary: Document-grounded dialogue systems (DGDS) utilize knowledge from external documents to answer domain-specific user questions. Existing solutions typically divide documents into independent passages for retrieval and response generation. This approach, however, neither makes good use of structural information within documents nor provides enough (document) context for knowledge selection and responses. This paper proposes SF-Re2G to address such issues systematically. Firstly, we seek to improve a passage representation by contrasting it with others of the same section, thus improving the retrieval performance. Secondly, a structure-enhanced reranker is built, leveraging the fact that multiple grounding passages of one dialog turn tend to be in the same neighborhood. Specifically, candidates from the retrieval are grouped into subgraphs according to the document structure. The reranker will rescore the candidate integrating its group information. Finally, the chosen passages are used for responses, taking into account the subgraph context for better generation. Experimental results on two DGDS datasets validate our method for both Chinese and English.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SF-Re2G, a document-grounded dialogue system framework that better leverages document structure to improve passage retrieval, reranking, and response generation.
AI.motivation: Existing document-grounded dialogue systems treat document passages independently, failing to exploit structural relationships and broader context within documents, which limits retrieval quality and answer accuracy.
AI.method: The authors propose SF-Re2G with three key components: (1) structure-aware passage representation learning via contrastive learning among passages within the same section; (2) a structure-enhanced reranker that groups retrieved passages into subgraphs based on document structure and rescoring candidates using group information; and (3) a response generator that conditions on selected passages plus their subgraph context to produce better grounded answers.
AI.result: On two document-grounded dialogue datasets in both Chinese and English, SF-Re2G outperforms baseline methods in retrieval and response generation metrics, demonstrating improved use of document structure for grounding.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging document structure via section-level contrastive representations, subgraph-based reranking, and structure-aware generation can significantly enhance document-grounded dialogue systems across languages.

title: InfoMem: Training Long-Context Memory Agents with Answer-Conditioned Information Gain
summary: Long-context tasks require LLMs to identify and preserve answer-relevant information from large contexts. Chunk-wise memory agents address this issue by sequentially reading document chunks, updating a compact memory, and generating the final answer from the accumulated memory. However, existing RL-based chunk-wise agents either rely on sparse final-answer rewards or use lexical intermediate rewards for memory and retrieval actions. These signals supervise task success or local overlap, but do not directly evaluate whether the final memory supports the ground-truth answer. We propose InfoMem, a reward mechanism for training chunk-wise memory agents that evaluates final-memory utility using answer-conditioned information. InfoMem measures how much the final memory increases the model's per-token log-likelihood of the ground-truth answer. To stabilize RL optimization, InfoMem applies this signal only to successful trajectories and normalizes it before reward composition. Under the same GRPO framework and training budget, InfoMem improves long-context memory-agent performance over comparable memory-agent RL baselines. Analyses show that effective final-memory rewards should operate on successful trajectories, be normalized before reward composition, and be conditioned on the answer rather than the query. Our code is available at https://github.com/GenSouKa1/InfoMem.

AI.tldr: InfoMem is a reward mechanism that trains long-context chunk-wise memory agents by directly measuring how much their final memory helps predict the ground-truth answer.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based chunk-wise memory agents for long-context tasks struggle because their rewards are either sparse (only based on final answer correctness) or based on shallow lexical overlap with memory or retrieved chunks. These signals fail to directly assess whether the compact final memory actually supports generating the correct answer, limiting learning efficiency and performance.
AI.method: The authors introduce InfoMem, an answer-conditioned reward for reinforcement learning of chunk-wise memory agents. The agent reads document chunks sequentially, updating a compact memory. After processing, InfoMem evaluates the utility of the final memory by computing how much it increases the model’s per-token log-likelihood of the ground-truth answer compared to a baseline. This information-based reward is only applied to trajectories that already produce the correct final answer, and it is normalized before being combined with other reward components to stabilize optimization within a GRPO framework.
AI.result: Using InfoMem within the same GRPO training framework and budget as prior methods yields improved performance of long-context memory agents over RL baselines that use sparse final-answer rewards or lexical overlap–based intermediate rewards. Empirical analyses demonstrate that InfoMem leads to more useful final memories for answering long-context questions.
AI.conclusion: Effective rewards for training long-context memory agents should (1) be conditioned on the answer rather than just the query, (2) be applied primarily to successful trajectories, and (3) be normalized prior to reward composition. InfoMem satisfies these criteria and significantly improves the training and performance of chunk-wise memory agents on long-context tasks.

title: The Word and the Way: Strategies for Domain-Specific BERT Pre-Training in German Medical NLP
comment: Under revision at BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
summary: Digital healthcare generates vast amounts of clinical text that can support AI-assisted applications, yet German biomedical language models remain limited by older architectures or restricted training data. We present ChristBERT (Clinical- and Healthcare-Related Issues and Subjects Tuned BERT), a family of domain-specific German RoBERTa-based language models trained on a 13.5GB corpus of scientific publications, clinical texts, health-related web content, and translated clinical resources. To investigate the impact of domain adaptation strategies in German clinical NLP, we compare continued pre-training, training from scratch, and domain-specific vocabulary adaptation. The resulting models are evaluated on three medical named entity recognition tasks and two text classification tasks. ChristBERT consistently outperforms existing general-purpose and medical German language models on four of five benchmarks and establishes a new state of the art for German clinical language modeling. Our results show that the optimal adaptation strategy is task-dependent: in our evaluation, training from scratch is particularly effective for highly specialized clinical texts, whereas continued pre-training performs well on more commonly written medical texts. All models are publicly released to support future research and applications in German medical NLP.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ChristBERT, a family of German RoBERTa-based clinical language models trained on a large, diverse healthcare corpus, achieving state-of-the-art performance on most evaluated German medical NLP tasks and showing that the best domain adaptation strategy depends on the task type.
AI.motivation: Digital healthcare in German-speaking contexts produces large amounts of clinical text, but existing German biomedical language models are limited—either based on outdated architectures or trained on restricted datasets. There is a need for stronger, domain-specific German clinical language models and for a clearer understanding of how different domain adaptation strategies (continued pre-training, training from scratch, vocabulary adaptation) affect performance on various clinical NLP tasks.
AI.method: The authors build ChristBERT, a set of German RoBERTa-based language models trained on a 13.5GB corpus combining scientific publications, real clinical texts, health-related web content, and translated clinical resources. They systematically explore three domain adaptation strategies: (1) continued pre-training of existing models on clinical data, (2) training new models from scratch on the domain corpus, and (3) adapting the tokenizer/vocabulary to the medical domain. They then evaluate the resulting models on three medical named entity recognition (NER) benchmarks and two text classification tasks in German.
AI.result: ChristBERT models consistently outperform both general-purpose and existing medical German language models on four out of five benchmark datasets across NER and text classification. Performance differences across variants reveal that training from scratch yields the best results on highly specialized clinical texts, while continued pre-training is more effective for tasks involving broadly written medical language. Vocabulary adaptation provides additional but task-dependent benefits.
AI.conclusion: ChristBERT establishes a new state of the art for German clinical language modeling on most tested benchmarks, demonstrating the value of large-scale, domain-specific pre-training for German medical NLP. The study concludes that no single adaptation strategy is universally optimal; instead, the choice between training from scratch and continued pre-training should be guided by the specialization level and characteristics of the target texts. The publicly released models are intended to facilitate further research and practical applications in German digital healthcare and clinical NLP.

title: CP-Agent: Context-Aware Multimodal Reasoning for Cellular Morphological Profiling under Chemical Perturbations
summary: Cell Painting combines multiplexed fluorescent staining, high-content imaging, and quantitative analysis to generate high-dimensional phenotypic readouts to support diverse downstream tasks such as mechanism-of-action (MoA) inference, toxicity prediction, and construction of drug-disease atlases. However, existing workflows are slow, costly and difficult to interpret. Approaches for drug screening modeling predominantly focus on molecular representation learning, while neglecting actual experimental context (e.g., cell line, dosing schedule, etc.), limiting generalization and MoA resolution. We introduce CP-Agent, an agentic multimodal large language model (MLLM) capable of generating mechanism-relevant, human-interpretable rationales for cell morphological changes under drug perturbations. At its core, CP-Agent leverages a context-aware alignment module, CP-CLIP, that jointly embeds high-content images and experimental metadata to enable robust treatment and MoA discrimination (achieving a maximum F1-score of 0.896). By integrating CP-CLIP outputs with agentic tool usage and reasoning, CP-Agent compiles rationales into a structured report to guide experimental design and hypothesis refinement. These capabilities highlight CP-Agent's potential to accelerate drug discovery by enabling more interpretable, scalable, and context-aware phenotypic screening -- streamlining iterative cycles of hypothesis generation in drug discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CP-Agent, a multimodal LLM system that interprets Cell Painting experiments, linking drug-induced cell morphology changes to mechanisms of action with high accuracy and human-readable explanations.
AI.motivation: Cell Painting offers rich phenotypic information for tasks like MoA inference and toxicity prediction, but current workflows are slow, expensive, and hard to interpret. Most computational approaches focus on molecular representations and ignore critical experimental context such as cell line and dosing, which restricts generalization and limits MoA resolution. There is a need for models that can leverage both imaging and metadata to deliver accurate and interpretable outputs that better support drug discovery workflows.
AI.method: The authors build CP-Agent, an agentic multimodal large language model framework centered on CP-CLIP, a context-aware contrastive alignment module. CP-CLIP jointly embeds high-content Cell Painting images and experimental metadata (e.g., cell line, dosing schedule) into a shared representation space to distinguish treatments and mechanisms of action. CP-Agent uses these embeddings along with tool-augmented reasoning to generate structured, mechanism-relevant, natural language rationales that explain observed cell morphological changes and compile them into reports that support experimental design and hypothesis refinement.
AI.result: CP-CLIP achieves strong performance in treatment and MoA discrimination, with a reported maximum F1-score of 0.896, indicating robust separation of phenotypes and mechanisms across diverse conditions. CP-Agent successfully transforms these multimodal embeddings into coherent, mechanism-aware rationales that are human-interpretable and context-sensitive, suitable for guiding experimental decisions in drug screening campaigns.
AI.conclusion: CP-Agent demonstrates that combining context-aware multimodal representation learning (images + metadata) with an agentic LLM can yield accurate, interpretable explanations for drug-induced cellular phenotypes. This approach addresses limitations of prior Cell Painting workflows in speed, cost, and interpretability, and offers a scalable, context-aware framework to streamline hypothesis generation and refinement in drug discovery and phenotypic screening.

title: Beyond "To whom it may concern": Tailoring Machine Translation to Audience and Intent
summary: Translation quality depends on purpose: the same source text demands different translations depending on audience, tone, and communicative intent. Yet MT models and metrics treat translation as a fixed mapping from source to target. LLMs enable users to explicitly specify purpose alongside source text, yet this capability has not been evaluated at scale. We introduce a systematic evaluation of purpose-driven MT across 50 languages, 5 model sizes and 8 text domains. We find that (1) explicit instructions substantially improve translation adaptedness, with larger gains on informal domains (conversation, social media), for larger model sizes and for higher-resource languages; (2) instructions outperform semantically-matched few-shot examples and paragraph-level context; (3) traditional MT metrics fail to capture adaptation quality, often penalizing adapted translations; (4) when curated instructions are unavailable, models can self-generate them from surrounding document context, closing up to 80% of the adaptedness gap to curated instructions. Our results establish that purpose-adapted MT is a viable and measurable capability of LLMs, while highlighting the need for purpose-aware metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models can adapt machine translation to an explicit communicative purpose, showing that purpose-aware instructions significantly improve translation adaptedness and that current MT metrics miss this aspect.
AI.motivation: Conventional machine translation assumes a single correct translation per source text and ignores the fact that real-world translation must adapt to audience, tone, and communicative intent. With LLMs, users can now specify this purpose directly, but there has been no large-scale, systematic evaluation of this capability or supporting metrics.
AI.method: The authors design a large-scale evaluation of purpose-driven machine translation spanning 50 languages, five model sizes, and eight text domains, including informal ones like conversation and social media. They compare several prompting and conditioning strategies: explicit natural language instructions that describe the translation purpose, semantically-matched few-shot examples, and additional paragraph-level context. They also test a setting where curated purpose instructions are missing and the model instead infers or self-generates instructions from surrounding document context. They assess both translation adaptedness to purpose and traditional MT quality metrics to see whether they reflect adaptation quality.
AI.result: (1) Adding explicit instructions about the translation purpose substantially improves how well translations are adapted to that purpose, especially for informal domains, larger LLMs, and higher-resource languages. (2) Purpose instructions are more effective than providing semantically similar few-shot exemplars or extra paragraph-level context. (3) Standard MT metrics, which focus on lexical or semantic similarity to reference translations, often do not track adaptation quality and may even penalize purpose-adapted translations. (4) When manually curated instructions are not available, models can generate purpose descriptions from surrounding document context, recovering up to about 80% of the adaptedness gains achieved with curated instructions.
AI.conclusion: Purpose-adapted machine translation is a robust and scalable capability of LLMs if they are given clear instructions about the intended use of the translation. However, widely used MT evaluation metrics are misaligned with this goal and can undervalue high-quality, purpose-adapted outputs. The paper argues that future MT research and practice should incorporate purpose-aware prompting strategies and develop new metrics that explicitly account for adaptation to communicative intent.

title: What Makes Interaction Trajectories Effective for Training Terminal Agents?
summary: Stronger code agents are commonly assumed to be superior teachers for post-training, yet this assumption remains poorly disentangled from task difficulty, harness design, and student capacity. We investigate this pedagogical link using Terminal-Lego, a scalable pipeline that transforms multi-domain real-world issues into environment-verified agentic tasks. Surprisingly, standalone performance does not dictate teaching efficacy: while Claude Opus 4.6 achieves higher scores on Terminal-Bench 2.0, students fine-tuned on trajectories from DeepSeek-V3.2, a lower-scoring agent, exhibit significantly stronger generalization. We attribute this "pedagogical paradox" to Environment-Grounded Supervision (EGS): trajectories that explicitly expose inspect-act-verify behaviors through harness-visible interactions allow students to internalize robust problem-solving routines rather than fragile action sequences. Scaling analysis reveals exceptional data efficiency: with only 15.3k Terminal-Lego trajectories, for example, Qwen3-32B achieves a 24.3% score on Terminal-Bench 2.0, rivaling previous SOTA performance established with over 30x the data volume. Our results suggest that the frontier of agent post-training lies beyond mere outcome-matching, shifting the focus toward "Harness Engineering", where the systematic design of environment-grounded interaction structures serves as the primary catalyst for reproducible and generalizable agentic intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why better-performing code agents aren’t always better teachers for training new agents, introducing a pipeline (Terminal-Lego) that reveals how environment-grounded interaction design can lead to more efficient and generalizable post-training.
AI.motivation: In current practice, stronger code agents—those with higher standalone benchmark performance—are often assumed to produce better supervision data for post-training weaker models. However, this belief conflates teacher strength with factors like task difficulty, harness (environment) design, and student capacity. There is a need to disentangle these factors and understand what actually makes agent-generated trajectories effective for teaching, especially for building robust and generalizable agentic behaviors rather than brittle, outcome-matched policies.
AI.method: The authors introduce Terminal-Lego, a scalable pipeline that converts diverse, real-world multi-domain problems into environment-verified, agentic tasks within a terminal-based setup. They compare different code agents (e.g., Claude Opus 4.6 and DeepSeek-V3.2) as teachers by generating trajectories in this environment and fine-tuning student models on these trajectories. Crucially, they analyze the structure of supervision—whether trajectories expose inspect-act-verify behaviors via harness-visible interactions—under the concept of Environment-Grounded Supervision (EGS). They then conduct scaling analyses to measure how student performance on Terminal-Bench 2.0 improves with varying quantities and qualities of Terminal-Lego trajectories.
AI.result: They find a “pedagogical paradox”: the agent with higher standalone performance (Claude Opus 4.6) is not the better teacher. Students trained on data from the lower-scoring DeepSeek-V3.2 generalize better. This advantage is attributed to EGS, where the teacher’s trajectories explicitly reveal inspection, action, and verification steps in a way visible to the harness. Using only 15.3k Terminal-Lego trajectories, a Qwen3-32B student model attains a 24.3% score on Terminal-Bench 2.0, matching or rivaling prior state-of-the-art results that required more than 30 times as much data, demonstrating high data efficiency of the proposed supervision style and pipeline.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that post-training agent quality is not solely determined by the teacher’s raw performance but critically depends on how the environment and interaction harness structure the supervision signal. Environment-Grounded Supervision—where inspect-act-verify routines are made explicit—produces more robust and generalizable students, even when the teacher’s benchmark score is lower. This shifts the focus from simply outcome-matching with stronger teachers toward “Harness Engineering,” in which the deliberate design of environment-grounded interaction structures becomes central to building reproducible, scalable, and generalizable agentic intelligence.

title: SEA-NLI: Natural Language Inference as a Lens into Southeast Asian Cultural Understanding
summary: Frontier LLMs perform well in Western contexts, but remain poorly tested on underrepresented cultures such as those in Southeast Asia (SEA). Existing NLI benchmarks are largely Western-centric, translation-derived, or monolingual, limiting their ability to measure culturally grounded reasoning. We introduce SEA-NLI, a native, culturally grounded NLI benchmark covering eight SEA countries in English and native regional languages, verified by native speakers. Across 17 encoder and decoder models, we observe a low performance from all models, especially for knowledge-intensive categories such as Languages and Science and Technology. Our analysis shows that failure cases mainly stem from missing SEA cultural knowledge: SEA-adapted models and culture-aware prompting improve performance, while CoT prompting offers limited gains.

AI.tldr: SEA-NLI is a culturally grounded NLI benchmark for Southeast Asia showing that current LLMs lack SEA-specific knowledge.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of robust evaluation of large language models on underrepresented Southeast Asian cultures and languages, since existing NLI benchmarks are Western-centric, translation-based, or monolingual and thus cannot properly test culturally grounded reasoning in SEA contexts.
AI.method: The authors construct SEA-NLI, a native and culturally grounded natural language inference benchmark for eight Southeast Asian countries, with data in English and corresponding native regional languages, all verified by native speakers. They then evaluate 17 encoder and decoder language models on this benchmark and conduct category-wise and error analyses.
AI.result: All evaluated models perform poorly on SEA-NLI, with particularly low accuracy in knowledge-intensive categories such as Languages and Science and Technology. The analysis reveals that many errors are due to missing Southeast Asian cultural knowledge. Models that were adapted to SEA data and those using culture-aware prompting show improved performance, whereas chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting yields only marginal benefits.
AI.conclusion: Current frontier LLMs lack sufficient Southeast Asian cultural and domain knowledge, which leads to weak performance on culturally grounded NLI tasks in SEA languages and contexts. The SEA-NLI benchmark exposes these deficiencies and shows that SEA adaptation and culture-aware prompting can partially mitigate them, while generic CoT prompting is not very effective.

title: DMF: A Deterministic Memory Framework for Conversational AI Agents
summary: Conversational AI agents require memory systems that are both scalable and semantically coherent across long interaction horizons. Existing approaches rely predominantly on large language model (LLM)-based summarisation at write time, which introduces non-determinism, escalating token costs, and opacity in pruning decisions. We present the Deterministic Memory Framework (DMF), a CPU-first approach that replaces generative memory compression with a fully deterministic pipeline grounded in classical NLP analysis, vector geometry, and mathematical scoring. DMF assigns each conversational interaction a Survival Score $Ω$ computed from deterministic content signals, conversational cues, and structured provenance, combined through a logistic projection. An interaction-count decay law, denoted as $Ω_{\mathrm{eff}}(Δn)$, governs how relevance evolves as new turns arrive, where $Δn$ is the number of newer interactions rather than wall-clock time, preserving full determinism. We present the mathematical formulation of DMF, its structured recall pipeline, the pruning decision procedure, and the evaluation protocol. Experiments are conducted on a purpose-built benchmark using the LoCoMo and LongMemEval datasets. We compare DMF against Mem0, a popular memory layer for AI agents. DMF achieves comparable accuracy while using zero tokens to prepare the memory context and 5x to 242x fewer tokens over the entire conversation. These results show that it is possible to eliminate LLM calls from the memory-management loop, reducing token costs to nearly zero and enabling deterministic memory systems for conversational AI agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a fully deterministic, token-free memory management framework for conversational AI that matches the performance of LLM-based systems while being cheaper and more transparent.
AI.motivation: Current conversational AI memory systems rely heavily on LLM-based summarization to compress and manage long interaction histories. This leads to high and growing token costs, non-deterministic behavior due to stochastic generation, and opaque pruning decisions that are hard to analyze or reproduce. The authors want a memory mechanism that is scalable, deterministic, explainable, and nearly cost-free in terms of LLM tokens.
AI.method: They propose the Deterministic Memory Framework (DMF), a CPU-first pipeline that uses classical NLP features, vector-space geometry, and mathematical scoring instead of generative LLM calls. Each interaction is assigned a Survival Score Ω based on deterministic content signals, conversational cues, and provenance metadata, combined via a logistic projection. A turn-based decay law Ω_eff(Δn) updates relevance as new turns arrive, depending only on the count of newer interactions, not on time. DMF defines a structured recall pipeline and a deterministic pruning rule driven by these scores, and an evaluation protocol to compare against LLM-based memory layers.
AI.result: On a purpose-built benchmark using LoCoMo and LongMemEval datasets, DMF is compared against Mem0, a widely used LLM-based agent memory layer. DMF achieves similar accuracy to Mem0 while requiring zero tokens for preparing memory context and between 5x and 242x fewer tokens for the full conversation, drastically lowering LLM usage.
AI.conclusion: It is feasible to remove LLM calls entirely from the memory-management loop of conversational agents without sacrificing task performance. Deterministic, analytically defined memory systems like DMF can deliver comparable accuracy, near-zero token cost, and transparent, reproducible pruning and recall behavior, making them attractive for scalable, reliable conversational AI deployments.

title: Multilingual Unlearning in LLMs: Transfer, Dynamics, and Reversibility
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can memorize sensitive facts, motivating unlearning methods that remove targeted knowledge without costly retraining. However, unlearning research remains heavily English-centric. We study multilingual unlearning by extending the TOFU benchmark to five languages, and fine-tune, unlearn, and query our models with different permutations of languages. We find that unlearning transfer, the ability of an unlearned model to "forget" facts in languages other than the unlearning language, is highly variable: e.g., it is strongest between languages sharing scripts and families, and we show that the unlearning language predicts which query languages are most likely to yield the strongest transfer. Layer-wise analysis reveals that unlearning leaves the shared cross-lingual latent space largely intact in early layers, instead operating primarily in later decoding layers. This suggests that unlearning does not truly erase knowledge, but rather induces superficial suppression. Exploiting this structure, a single inference-time steering direction reverses much of this suppression across languages, recovering 50% (Qwen) and 90% (Gemma) of the unlearned knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well unlearning of specific facts in large language models transfers across multiple languages, finding that forgetting is often superficial and reversible.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM unlearning work is mostly in English, but real-world models are multilingual and may store sensitive facts in many languages. It is unclear whether unlearning in one language effectively removes the same facts in other languages, and how this relates to the model’s internal multilingual representations.
AI.method: They extend the TOFU unlearning benchmark to five languages, then fine-tune models with sensitive facts, apply unlearning procedures in specific languages, and systematically query the models in various source–target language combinations. They analyze transfer of unlearning, inspect layer-wise representation changes to see where unlearning acts in the network, and construct inference-time steering directions to counteract the unlearning-induced suppression.
AI.result: Unlearning transfer is highly variable across language pairs and is strongest between languages that share scripts and language families. The language used during unlearning predicts in which query languages the forgetting will be strongest. Representation analysis shows early layers’ cross-lingual latent space is mostly preserved, while later decoding layers are mainly affected. Using this structure, they find a single steering vector at inference can undo a large portion of the unlearning, restoring about 50% of the removed knowledge in Qwen and 90% in Gemma across languages.
AI.conclusion: Unlearning in multilingual LLMs tends to suppress access to facts rather than fully erasing underlying knowledge, and this suppression is uneven across languages. Because early multilingual representations remain intact and unlearning mostly alters later decoding layers, a simple steering direction at inference can substantially recover the supposedly unlearned knowledge. This raises concerns about the robustness of current unlearning techniques in multilingual settings and highlights the need for methods that modify deeper shared representations rather than surface-level decoding behavior.

title: StepFinder: A Temporal Semantic Framework for Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: LLM-based multi-agent systems exhibit remarkable collaborative capabilities in complex multi-step tasks. However, these systems are highly sensitive to single-step execution errors that can propagate through agent interactions and lead to cascading failures. To understand the causes of failure and improve system reliability, failure attribution has been introduced as a task that aims to automatically identify the root cause step responsible for a failure. Existing failure attribution methods mainly rely on LLMs to reason over original execution trajectories, which not only incur high inference costs and latency, but also suffer from interference caused by redundant and noisy execution logs, causing LLMs to struggle in accurately identifying the true root cause step. To address this, we propose StepFinder, a lightweight failure attribution framework. We use LLMs solely during the feature construction phase to encode execution logs into temporal semantic sequences. Subsequently, a parameter-efficient combination of temporal modeling and attention modules is applied to capture the sequential evolution and cross-step dependencies of the trajectories. Finally, the step-level error score is refined through multi-scale differences and position bias, enabling precise root cause identification. Experimental results on the Who&When benchmark demonstrate that StepFinder outperforms LLM-based methods in step-level failure attribution while achieving substantially higher inference efficiency, reducing inference time by 79% compared with the fastest LLM-based method, with no text generation overhead. Our code is available at https://github.com/taiyu-zhu/StepFinder.

AI.tldr: StepFinder is a lightweight framework to automatically find the root-cause step of failures in LLM-based multi-agent systems, outperforming prior LLM-only approaches while being much faster.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems can collaborate on complex multi-step tasks but are fragile: a small error at one step can propagate through agents and cause overall failure. To improve reliability, it is useful to automatically pinpoint which step was the root cause. Existing failure attribution approaches use LLMs directly over full execution logs, leading to high inference cost, latency, and confusion from redundant/noisy logs, which harms attribution accuracy. A more efficient and robust method is needed.
AI.method: StepFinder first uses an LLM only once in an offline or feature-construction stage to encode raw execution logs into temporal semantic sequences. After this, it relies on a parameter-efficient neural architecture that combines temporal modeling (to track how the trajectory evolves over time) and attention modules (to model cross-step dependencies). It then computes and refines an error score for each step using multi-scale differences (capturing changes across different temporal ranges) and position bias, enabling precise identification of the most likely root-cause step without further text generation.
AI.result: On the Who&When benchmark for step-level failure attribution, StepFinder surpasses LLM-based baselines in correctly identifying the root cause step. It also dramatically improves efficiency: inference time is reduced by 79% relative to the fastest LLM-based method, and because the model operates on pre-encoded features, it avoids any additional text generation cost.
AI.conclusion: StepFinder demonstrates that decoupling feature construction from inference and using a compact temporal-attention model over semantic step representations yields more accurate and far more efficient failure attribution for LLM-based multi-agent systems. This suggests a promising direction for building reliable diagnostic tools for complex LLM agent workflows, where post-hoc analysis can be performed quickly without incurring heavy LLM reasoning costs over raw logs.

title: SagaQA: A Multi-hop Reasoning Benchmark for Long-form Narrative Understanding in TV Series
summary: We introduce SagaQA, a long-form video benchmark for multi-hop reasoning over full-length TV series. Existing video reasoning benchmarks often emphasize local understanding of adjacent frames or clips. SagaQA addresses this gap by requiring high-level comprehension of extended multimodal narratives in entire TV shows. A distinguishing feature of SagaQA is the granularity of its reasoning steps. Our dataset necessitates long-range reasoning hops to connect information across completely different episodes. This requires models to reason over entire events and actions, demanding a deep understanding of the show's narration and progression at a multimodal level. Motivated by recent progress in agentic methods, we further study how different planning strategies handle such complex reasoning. We categorize these approaches into three classes-Parallel, Sequential, and Hybrid planners-and evaluate their ability to generate coherent and complete reasoning plans. Our results on SagaQA suggest that hybrid planners consistently produce higher-quality plans and exhibit stronger capabilities for complex, high-level narrative understanding in TV shows.

AI.tldr: SagaQA is a new benchmark that tests whether models can perform multi-hop reasoning over entire TV series rather than short clips, and it finds that hybrid planning agents reason best over these long, multimodal narratives.
AI.motivation: Most existing video QA and reasoning benchmarks focus on local understanding of short clips or adjacent frames, which does not reflect the kind of long-range, multi-episode reasoning humans use to understand full TV series. There is a need for a benchmark that evaluates high-level narrative comprehension across extended video content and tests modern agentic planning methods in this challenging setting.
AI.method: The authors construct SagaQA, a long-form video QA benchmark based on full-length TV series, where questions require multi-hop reasoning that spans distant segments and even different episodes. They design questions so models must connect events, actions, and narrative elements across the entire show, leveraging both visual and linguistic cues. They then study how agentic planning strategies—categorized into Parallel, Sequential, and Hybrid planners—handle this reasoning. Each planner type is tasked with generating reasoning plans to answer questions, and their outputs are evaluated for coherence, completeness, and reasoning quality.
AI.result: On SagaQA, Hybrid planning approaches outperform both purely Parallel and purely Sequential planners. Hybrid planners generate more coherent and complete reasoning plans and handle long-range, multi-hop narrative reasoning better. This empirical result suggests that combining elements of parallel and sequential planning is advantageous for complex long-form video understanding.
AI.conclusion: SagaQA exposes the limitations of current video reasoning systems that focus on short-range understanding and demonstrates that true narrative comprehension over long-form TV content requires models capable of extended, multi-hop reasoning. The benchmark shows that hybrid planning architectures are particularly effective in this setting, highlighting a promising direction for future research on agentic, multimodal, long-context video understanding.

title: A formal definition and meta-model for a machine theory of mind
summary: This paper proposes, for the first time, a rigorous formal definition of the concept of Machine Theory of Mind, based on principles supported by evidence from cognitive psychology, neuroscience and artificial intelligence, and uses the above as a lens to examine state-of-the-art and current efforts in the field, driving a potential agenda for further research there able to "crack" the problem. It also advances a general holistic meta-model for Machine Theory of Mind, and examines the state of the art when it comes to empirically benchmarking such models.

AI.tldr: Defines Machine Theory of Mind (Machine ToM), proposes a holistic meta-model, and reviews and benchmarks current approaches to guide future research.
AI.motivation: Theory of Mind (ToM) is central to human social intelligence, but machines currently lack a clear, rigorous and unified notion of ToM. Existing work uses the term loosely and inconsistently, which hinders progress, evaluation, and comparison across systems. The paper aims to provide a principled, formal definition grounded in cognitive science and AI, and to organize the field under a coherent conceptual framework.
AI.method: 1) Synthesize evidence and insights from cognitive psychology, neuroscience, and AI to extract core principles of Theory of Mind relevant for machines. 2) Use these principles to construct a formal definition of Machine ToM. 3) Propose a general holistic meta-model that organizes the components and processes required for Machine ToM. 4) Survey and analyze state-of-the-art Machine ToM approaches through the lens of this definition and meta-model. 5) Review and critically assess existing empirical benchmarks for Machine ToM, identifying gaps and limitations. 6) Outline a research agenda guided by the proposed framework and benchmarking analysis.
AI.result: Provides: (a) a formal, principled definition of Machine Theory of Mind; (b) a holistic meta-model describing its key components and interactions; (c) a structured analysis of current approaches and how they map onto (or fall short of) this meta-model; and (d) a critical review of current benchmarks, clarifying what aspects of Machine ToM are or are not being adequately tested.
AI.conclusion: A rigorous, cognitively grounded definition and meta-model of Machine Theory of Mind can unify fragmented work in the area, expose conceptual and empirical gaps, and guide the design of better models and benchmarks. This structured framework creates a clearer path for future research aiming to build machines with robust, testable Theory of Mind-like abilities.

title: From Script to Semantics: Prompting Strategies for African NLI
comment: Accepted at the RAIL Workshop, LREC 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly evaluated in multilingual settings, yet their inference behavior in low-resource African languages remains underexplored especially under pure prompting without fine-tuning. We present a systematic study of prompting strategies for Natural Language Inference (NLI) in Swahili, Yoruba, and Hausa using the AfriXNLI benchmark. We evaluate five prompting strategies Baseline (zero-shot), Script-Aware, Language Specific, Contrastive, and Native-Label Self-Translation (NL-STP) across two mid-sized open weight models (Llama3.2-3B and Gemma3-4B). To isolate the effect of prompt design, the effect of few-shot examples and Chain-of-Thought reasoning is eliminated in our study. We find a significant difference in performance of class wise across strategies with highly neutral class collapse and high prediction skew in some configurations. Contrastive prompting proves to be the most reliable and steadily improving strategy over language and model and has better balance of class behavior and balance of overall accuracy gains. Notably, well-constructed prompts are sufficient to beat more powerful baselines that are provided with few-shot prompts and Chain-of-Thought prompts. We have found that prompt formulation is essential to multilingual NLI with low-resource languages and that language aware decision structuring can be used to meaningfully enhance robustness in resource challenged settings.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how different prompt designs affect Natural Language Inference performance of mid-sized LLMs in low-resource African languages, showing that careful contrastive, language-aware prompting can outperform larger models using few-shot and Chain-of-Thought methods.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are increasingly tested in multilingual contexts, their behavior in low-resource African languages under pure prompting (without fine-tuning) is poorly understood. Existing work often relies on few-shot examples or Chain-of-Thought, making it hard to isolate the specific effects of prompt design on NLI performance. There is a need to understand which prompting strategies work best for NLI in African languages like Swahili, Yoruba, and Hausa, and whether good prompt design alone can close the performance gap with more powerful models.
AI.method: The authors use the AfriXNLI benchmark to run a systematic comparison of five prompting strategies for NLI in Swahili, Yoruba, and Hausa: (1) Baseline zero-shot prompting, (2) Script-Aware prompting (sensitive to writing systems), (3) Language-Specific prompting (tailored wording per language), (4) Contrastive prompting (explicit comparison of hypothesis vs premise), and (5) Native-Label Self-Translation prompting (NL-STP), where labels are translated between native and another language. They evaluate these prompts on two mid-sized open-weight LLMs, Llama3.2-3B and Gemma3-4B. To isolate the effect of prompt design, they deliberately exclude few-shot examples and Chain-of-Thought reasoning from the experiments, then analyze class-wise behavior (entailment, contradiction, neutral), prediction skew, and overall accuracy across languages and models.
AI.result: They observe large class-wise performance differences across prompting strategies, including strong collapse toward the neutral class and skewed predictions in some settings. Among all methods, contrastive prompting emerges as the most reliable and consistently improving strategy across the three languages and both models. It offers a better balance of class predictions and yields the most stable accuracy gains. Furthermore, thoughtfully engineered prompts (without few-shot or Chain-of-Thought) can outperform stronger baseline systems that do rely on those additional techniques.
AI.conclusion: Prompt formulation is a key factor in multilingual NLI for low-resource African languages. Language-aware and contrastive prompt designs can substantially improve robustness and class balance, sometimes even surpassing more powerful models augmented with few-shot examples and Chain-of-Thought reasoning. Therefore, in resource-constrained multilingual settings, carefully structured, language-sensitive prompts are an effective and practical way to enhance NLI performance without additional data or fine-tuning.

title: ThoughtFold: Folding Reasoning Chains via Introspective Preference Learning
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have achieved remarkable progress thanks to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) on Chain-of-Thoughts (CoTs). However, since long CoTs naturally contain trial and errors and mainstream RLVR approaches choose outcome-correct CoT trajectories for memorization, the redundant explorations in long CoTs are inevitably reinforced, which results in the over-thinking issues of LRMs. Previous attempts to resolve this issue mainly give more advantage to shorter trajectories, yet their learning signals are still outcome-based and cannot reduce the memorization of redundant explorations in long CoTs. Therefore, we propose ThoughtFold, a framework that leverages fine-grained preference learning to mitigate redundant explorations for efficient reasoning. ThoughtFold employs an introspective strategy to identify redundancy within each correct trajectory, which yields a spectrum of candidate sub-trajectories. Leveraging this spectrum, we introduce a masked preference optimization objective that explicitly penalizes redundant explorations and encourages the model to directly bridge essential reasoning segments, effectively folding its reasoning chains into a more concise path. Extensive experiments show that ThoughtFold significantly enhances efficiency. It reduces the token usage of DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B by approximately 56% while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy.

AI.tldr: ThoughtFold is a fine-grained preference learning framework that trims redundant steps from correct chain-of-thought trajectories, making large reasoning models much more efficient without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models trained with RLVR tend to over-think: they learn to reproduce long chains-of-thought including trial-and-error segments because the learning signal only cares about final correctness. Prior attempts that favor shorter trajectories still rely on outcome-based rewards and thus do not directly suppress redundant exploration within long, correct traces. A method is needed that can explicitly identify and penalize unnecessary reasoning steps inside successful chains, so models can reason more concisely while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: ThoughtFold introduces fine-grained preference learning over segments of chain-of-thought trajectories. For each correct trajectory, it uses an introspective strategy to detect redundant portions and constructs a spectrum of sub-trajectories that vary in how much redundancy they contain. Based on these, it defines a masked preference optimization objective that contrasts concise, essential sub-trajectories against more redundant ones. The model is trained to prefer trajectories that bridge key reasoning segments directly, while being penalized for redundant exploration within otherwise correct chains.
AI.result: Experiments on LRMs, particularly DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, show that ThoughtFold substantially reduces over-thinking. It cuts token usage by about 56% while keeping state-of-the-art accuracy on tested reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating that it can greatly improve reasoning efficiency without sacrificing performance.
AI.conclusion: ThoughtFold effectively mitigates redundant exploration in large reasoning models by operating at a finer granularity than outcome-based RLVR. By learning preferences over sub-trajectories within correct chains-of-thought and penalizing redundant steps, it folds long reasoning into more concise paths. This leads to large reductions in token usage and preserves high accuracy, indicating a promising direction for making LRMs more efficient and practical.

title: Beyond Ideal Instruction: A Comprehensive Framework for Evaluating LLMs in Realistic Interactions
summary: Despite great advances in tool-use capabilities of large language models (LLMs), existing evaluation benchmarks struggle to fully align with real-world scenarios. Such benchmarks mostly rely on simulated idealized user assumptions and lacks experience-oriented evaluation. These limitations fail to account for the ambiguity, uncooperative behaviors, and shifting intentions characteristic of real-world users. To fill this gap, we propose RUT-Bench, a dedicated benchmark designed to assess LLMs under diverse Real-world User Tool calling scenarios. RUT-Bench supports high-fidelity simulations covering both ideal rational patterns and heterogeneous non-ideal behaviors across single-turn and multi-turn dialogues. We conduct comprehensive evaluations on 19 widely adopted open-source and proprietary LLMs using our benchmark. Experimental results reveal that no tested LLMs achieve an overall success rate above 40%, and nearly all of them experience noticeable performance drops when facing more complicated non-ideal user inputs. Our code and data is available at https://github.com/TorresYangX/RUT-Bench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RUT-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate LLM tool-use under realistic, messy user behaviors, showing current models perform poorly, especially with non-ideal users.
AI.motivation: Existing tool-use benchmarks assume ideal, cooperative, and unambiguous users, which does not reflect how real users behave in practice. This gap makes it hard to know how robust LLMs really are when interacting with tools in real applications. The authors want an evaluation setting that captures ambiguity, uncooperativeness, shifting goals, and other realistic challenges.
AI.method: The authors design RUT-Bench, a benchmark that simulates real-world user behaviors for tool calling in both single-turn and multi-turn dialogues. It includes scenarios with both ideal rational user patterns and various non-ideal behaviors (ambiguous instructions, inconsistent goals, etc.). They then run systematic evaluations of 19 popular open-source and proprietary LLMs on these scenarios, measuring success rates and robustness under different user types and dialogue complexities.
AI.result: Across the 19 evaluated LLMs, none achieves an overall success rate above 40% on RUT-Bench. Almost all models show clear performance degradation when moving from idealized rational user inputs to more complex, non-ideal user behaviors and multi-turn interactions.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are far from robust in realistic tool-use scenarios, particularly when confronted with ambiguous, shifting, or uncooperative users. RUT-Bench exposes this gap and provides a more faithful, experience-oriented benchmark for future model and system improvements; the authors release code and data to support further research.

title: Overlaying Governance: A Compositional Authorization Framework for Delegation and Scope in Agentic AI
summary: As AI systems evolve from passive models into autonomous active agents capable of initiating actions, collaborating, and delegating tasks, the traditional boundaries of software systems blur. Traditional authorization and delegation frameworks, built around fixed principals, explicit requests, and static scopes, are insufficient to govern agentic systems. Agentic AI demands richer authorization semantics: agents must inherit and delegate permissions, act under time-limited authority, and coordinate through shared protocols. Existing Identity and Access Management (IAM) systems fail to fully capture this notion of agency, lacking mechanisms for recursive delegation, contextual boundaries, and dynamic scoping as executable governance primitives. Unlike access delegation standards such as OAuth 2.0, we treat delegation as a contractual term rather than merely a static token-based consent credential. This paper proposes a compositional governance framework that introduces primitives indispensable for agentic AI. We define types of delegation and their permissions and accountability implications, and we introduce a notion of resource scope attenuation to bound agentic access envelopes. These concepts are expressed as general relational definitions that can be composed into existing authorization domains (e.g., financial systems). To operationalize this composition, we define a compositional operator that overlays new agentic semantics, such as recursive delegation chains, onto existing relational policies without rewriting them. We substantiate this framework through formal proofs and empirical evaluation, showing that it provides a formal yet practical foundation for accountable authorization in agentic AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new, compositional governance framework for authorizing and constraining autonomous AI agents, adding richer delegation and scope semantics on top of existing IAM systems.
AI.motivation: As AI shifts from passive models to autonomous agents that can act, collaborate, and delegate, existing authorization and delegation systems (like OAuth or traditional IAM) are too rigid. They assume fixed identities, explicit one-step requests, and static scopes, and they lack features such as recursive delegation, time-bounded authority, contextual constraints, and dynamic permission scoping—capabilities that are crucial when agents act on behalf of users and each other. There is a need for a formal model that captures these agentic behaviors while remaining compatible with current enterprise authorization infrastructures.
AI.method: The authors design a compositional governance framework centered on new authorization and delegation primitives tailored to agentic AI. They formally define different types of delegation, their permission semantics, and accountability implications, and introduce the notion of resource scope attenuation to safely bound what agents can access and do. These concepts are expressed as general relational definitions that can overlay existing authorization domains (e.g., financial systems). A key technical construct is a compositional operator that lets practitioners add agentic semantics—like recursive delegation chains—on top of pre-existing relational access policies without rewriting those policies. The framework is supported by formal proofs of its properties and by empirical evaluation showing it can be practically integrated.
AI.result: The framework yields a formal, compositional way to express rich agentic behaviors—recursive delegation, inheritance and attenuation of permissions, contextual and time-limited authority—while remaining compatible with legacy IAM systems. The authors demonstrate that their operator can overlay new agentic semantics onto existing relational policies, preserving correctness and enabling accountable delegation. Formal proofs validate key security and accountability guarantees, and empirical evaluation indicates that the approach is practically implementable in real-world domains such as financial systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current IAM and delegation standards are inadequate for governing autonomous agentic AI. By treating delegation as a contractual governance primitive and providing a compositional relational framework with resource scope attenuation and recursive delegation, the authors offer a principled and practical foundation for authorization in agentic AI systems. Their approach enables accountable, constrained, and interoperable agent behavior without redesigning existing authorization infrastructures, paving the way for safer deployment of AI agents in complex domains.

title: Evaluating LLMs' Effectiveness on Real-World Consumer Device Repair Questions
summary: Consumer device repair is an important but underexplored testbed for large language models (LLMs). Repair tasks require reasoning over incomplete problem descriptions, hardware-specific diagnostics, actionable troubleshooting, and safety-critical decisions, where incorrect advice can cause device damage, battery hazards, or permanent data loss. We introduce a benchmark of 991 real-world repair questions from Reddit spanning phone repair, computer repair, and data recovery, each paired with technician-written reference solutions, and provide Bangla translations to evaluate cross-lingual performance. We evaluate six state-of-the-art LLMs in English and Bangla using four repair-specific criteria: correctness, completeness, practicality, and safety. Our results show that while LLMs can provide useful repair assistance, they remain unreliable for high-risk real-world repair tasks without rigorous evaluation and explicit safety safeguards. Phone repair is the most difficult and safety-sensitive domain, and all models make substantial errors in board-level diagnosis, repair prioritization, and safe recovery procedures. Across domains and models, Bangla responses consistently perform worse than English responses. Among the evaluated models, GPT-5.4 performs best overall.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark of 991 real-world consumer device repair questions to evaluate large language models on correctness, completeness, practicality, and safety, showing that current LLMs are helpful but unreliable, especially for phone repair and in Bangla.
AI.motivation: Consumer device repair is a challenging, safety-critical, and underexplored domain for evaluating LLMs. Repair tasks involve incomplete problem descriptions, hardware-specific diagnostics, and high-stakes decisions where incorrect guidance can cause physical damage, hazards, or data loss. Existing benchmarks do not adequately capture these real-world, risk-sensitive aspects, motivating the need for a dedicated repair benchmark and evaluation framework.
AI.method: The authors compile a benchmark of 991 real-world repair questions from Reddit across three domains: phone repair, computer repair, and data recovery. Each question is paired with a technician-written reference solution. They also provide Bangla translations of the dataset to assess cross-lingual performance. Six state-of-the-art LLMs are evaluated in both English and Bangla using four domain-specific criteria: correctness, completeness, practicality, and safety of the repair advice.
AI.result: LLMs can offer useful assistance for consumer device repair but show significant unreliability on high-risk tasks. Phone repair emerges as the most difficult and safety-sensitive domain, where all evaluated models exhibit substantial errors in board-level diagnosis, repair prioritization, and safe recovery procedures. Across all domains and models, responses in Bangla consistently underperform compared to English. Among the six evaluated models, GPT-5.4 achieves the best overall performance on the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs, while promising for assisting with consumer device repair, are not yet reliable enough for high-risk, real-world repair scenarios without strong evaluation protocols and explicit safety safeguards. Phone repair, in particular, poses serious challenges in terms of safety and diagnostic complexity. The introduced benchmark and bilingual setup highlight persistent weaknesses, especially in non-English settings, and provide a framework for more rigorous, safety-aware development and evaluation of future LLMs in practical repair contexts.

title: SAGE: A Quantitative Evaluation of Socialized Evolution in Agent Ecosystems
summary: Self-improving language agents are typically evaluated in isolation: an agent attempts a task, receives feedback, and iteratively refines its own behavior. Yet agents increasingly operate alongside peers whose strategies and outcomes are publicly visible. This raises an under-studied question: when does shared experience produce improvements that self-improvement alone cannot achieve? We introduce SAGE (Social Agent Group Evolution),an evaluation framework that compares two compute-matched conditions: SocialEvo, where agents from five distinct model families co-evolve with access to all peers' histories; and SelfEvo, where each agent receives the same number of task attempts but sees only its own past, which is conventional in self-improving agent studies. We instantiate SAGE in three arenas: open-ended ML research, long-horizon economic planning, and strategic multiplayer play, evaluated across multiple evolutionary rounds. We find that group history is not a universal amplifier: the strongest agent does not exceed its self-evolution ceiling. However, agents that plateau under self-improvement can achieve significant breakthroughs when peer experience is available. In competitive settings, counterfactual controls reveal that agents improve generally rather than developing opponent-specific strategies. Across different forms of shared history, filtered peer traces and reflective summaries often outperform raw logs, indicating that social gains depend on abstraction rather than exposure volume. These findings reveal that peer-history gains are agent-specific, arena-dependent, and contingent on the capacity to abstract transferable knowledge from public traces.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SAGE, a framework to test whether language agents learn more effectively from shared social histories than from solo self-improvement, finding that peer histories help some but not all agents and depend on abstraction quality.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of self-improving language agents focus on agents learning only from their own past interactions. However, in realistic settings agents can observe the successes and failures of many other agents in the same environment. It is unclear when and how such shared experience yields benefits beyond what self-improvement alone can provide, and whether access to group history universally amplifies performance or only under certain conditions.
AI.method: The authors introduce SAGE (Social Agent Group Evolution), an evaluation framework that contrasts two compute-matched regimes: SocialEvo, where multiple agents from five distinct model families co-evolve with full access to each other’s interaction histories; and SelfEvo, where each agent has the same number of task attempts but only observes its own logs. They instantiate this framework in three different arenas—open-ended ML research tasks, long-horizon economic planning, and strategic multiplayer games—running multiple evolutionary rounds and varying the form of shared history (raw logs, filtered traces, reflective summaries) to assess how different abstractions of peer experience affect learning.
AI.result: Across arenas, access to peer histories does not universally boost performance: the strongest agent’s performance ceiling under SocialEvo matches its self-improvement ceiling under SelfEvo. However, agents that stall when learning only from their own experience can achieve substantial gains in SocialEvo, surpassing their SelfEvo plateaus. In competitive settings, counterfactual analyses show that improvements generalize rather than being tailored to specific opponents. Furthermore, not all forms of shared history are equally useful—processed peer information such as filtered traces and reflective summaries often yield larger benefits than unprocessed raw logs, indicating that how experience is abstracted matters critically.
AI.conclusion: Social learning through shared histories can unlock new capabilities for some language agents, but its benefits are neither universal nor automatic. Performance gains depend on the agent’s underlying learning dynamics, the task arena, and the availability of appropriately abstracted, transferable knowledge distilled from public traces. The findings suggest that designing effective social training regimes for language agents requires careful control over what and how peer experience is exposed, rather than simply increasing the volume of shared data.

title: Lingo_Research_Group at SemEval-2026 Task 9: Evaluating Prompt Variants for Polarization Detection
comment: Accepted at the SemEval Workshop, ACL 2026
summary: Our submission presented in this paper is for SemEval-2026 Task 9: Multilingual Text Classification Challenge - Polarization Detection and it covers all three subtasks: (1) binary polarization detection, (2) polarization type classification and (3) polarization manifestation identification. We adopt a systematic approach of research on short designed prompts by considering twelve designed prompts that are different in terminology clarity, detail of the definition, guidance of reasoning and in-context examples use. The experiments are conducted using aya-101 and Gemma3-27B, with the latter chosen for the submission at the end of the development through performance considerations. Our system has an average macro level F1-score of 0.762 on Subtask 1, 0.587 on Subtask 2 and 0.444 on Subtask 3 with the average accuracy of 0.819, 0.678 and 0.498, respectively, on the official test set averaged among 22 languages, respectively. With cross-task and cross-lingual analysis, we demonstrate that prompt-based approaches can be used effectively to detect coarse grained polarization but encounter more and more difficulties as far as fine-grained and multi-label sociolinguistic classification is concerned.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a prompt-based multilingual system for SemEval-2026 Task 9 on polarization detection, showing strong performance on binary detection but decreasing effectiveness for finer-grained and multi-label subtasks.
AI.motivation: To explore how far carefully designed prompts with large language models can go for multilingual polarization detection across 22 languages, and to understand their strengths and limitations for increasingly fine-grained sociolinguistic classification.
AI.method: The authors design twelve short prompts that vary in terminology clarity, definitional detail, reasoning guidance, and use of in-context examples. They evaluate these prompts using two LLMs, aya-101 and Gemma3-27B, on three subtasks: binary polarization detection, polarization type classification, and polarization manifestation identification, and then select the best-performing configuration for submission. Cross-task and cross-lingual analyses are performed to understand behavior across languages and task granularities.
AI.result: Using Gemma3-27B with the best-designed prompts, the system achieves average macro F1-scores of 0.762 (Subtask 1), 0.587 (Subtask 2), and 0.444 (Subtask 3), with corresponding accuracies of 0.819, 0.678, and 0.498 on the official test set averaged over 22 languages. Performance is highest for binary detection and degrades as tasks become more fine-grained and multi-label.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-based LLM approaches are effective for coarse-grained, binary polarization detection in a multilingual setting, but they struggle increasingly with fine-grained, multi-label sociolinguistic classification tasks, indicating the need for more sophisticated methods or additional supervision for such subtasks.

title: From Prompt to Service: An SLM-Based Agent Orchestration Gateway for AI-Driven Virtual Worlds
summary: As generative AI capabilities expand, AI-driven virtual worlds face a growing architectural challenge. Users interact through in-world interfaces in multimodal ways, yet their requests demand fundamentally different AI backend models and computational resources. Embedding these capabilities directly into virtual world systems reduces extensibility, complicates maintenance, and limits the ability to coordinate services distributed across edge and cloud infrastructure. This paper presents an SLM-based Agent Orchestration Gateway, a lightweight runtime coordination mechanism that decouples a virtual world client from heterogeneous AI backends through intent-driven service routing. An edge-deployed SLM classifies the semantic intent of each user prompt, a configurable service registry validates and resolves the routing decision, and the selected backend is invoked transparently, enabling new AI capabilities to be introduced in the virtual world without modifying the client application. The gateway is implemented and evaluated within the InterwovenXR virtual museum testbed. The evaluation shows that compact SLMs can serve as reliable intent routers on edge hardware, and that task-specific fine-tuning can transform sub-billion-parameter models into practical, low-latency routers. A layered configuration pairing a fine-tuned sub billion-parameter model as router with a larger SLM for conversational response generation is shown to be deployable on mid-range edge hardware and more efficient than delegating both responsibilities to a single model. The findings show that SLMs can support practical AI service orchestration in virtual worlds and the work contributes an evaluated architecture for scalable, extensible, and edge-supported AI interaction, enabling virtual agents become access points to distributed generative AI services.

AI.tldr: They propose and evaluate a lightweight, SLM-based gateway that routes user intents in virtual worlds to the right AI backends, enabling scalable, low-latency, edge-supported orchestration of heterogeneous generative AI services.
AI.motivation: Generative AI is increasingly used in virtual worlds where users interact via multimodal, in-world interfaces. However, different user requests (e.g., chat, image generation, analytics) require different backend AI models and compute resources. Hardwiring these capabilities into the virtual world platform reduces extensibility, complicates maintenance, and makes it difficult to leverage heterogeneous, distributed (edge + cloud) infrastructure.
