A more flexible, decoupled orchestration mechanism is needed so that new AI services can be added or swapped without changing the client application, while still meeting latency and resource constraints at the edge.
AI.method: They design an Agent Orchestration Gateway centered on a small language model (SLM) deployed at the edge. The SLM classifies the semantic intent of each user prompt. Based on this intent, a configurable service registry validates and resolves which backend AI service to call (e.g., different LMs, generative models, or tools). The gateway then transparently invokes the selected backend, decoupling the virtual world client from specific AI models. They implement this architecture in the InterwovenXR virtual museum testbed, and experimentally evaluate different SLM sizes, fine-tuning strategies, and deployment layouts (including a layered setup combining a small router SLM with a larger conversational SLM) on mid-range edge hardware.
AI.result: Experiments show that compact, sub‑billion‑parameter SLMs can act as accurate and reliable intent routers when deployed on edge hardware. Task-specific fine-tuning significantly improves routing performance, making these small models practical low-latency orchestrators. A layered configuration in which a fine-tuned small SLM handles routing and a larger SLM handles conversational response generation is feasible on mid-range edge devices and yields better efficiency than using a single large model for both routing and conversation.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that small language models are well-suited for intent-based orchestration of heterogeneous AI services in virtual worlds, especially under edge computing constraints. The proposed Agent Orchestration Gateway offers a scalable, extensible, and maintainable architecture that decouples virtual world clients from backend AI models, enabling new generative AI capabilities to be introduced without client changes. This supports practical AI service orchestration in XR environments and positions virtual agents as flexible access points to distributed generative AI services across edge and cloud.

title: The Unsampled Truth: Psychometrics in SLMs Measure Prompt Artifacts, Not Psychological Constructs
summary: When prompting SLMs for psychometric assessments, researchers assume the outputs reflect semantic reasoning. We evaluate this premise across 13 open-weights models (0.6B to 14B parameters) using a prompt variation framework that separates semantic signals from prompt artifacts. By systematically varying personas, instructions, items, and option symbols, we find that artifactual variance frequently overpowers the semantic signal. In these cases, models predominantly reflect prompt compliance rather than simulated psychological traits. While these findings limit SLM utility in psychometrics, our framework provides a diagnostic tool to identify destructive artifacts and isolate semantic understanding for future frontier-model research.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that small language models (SLMs) used for psychometric assessments often follow superficial prompt cues more than they demonstrate true semantic or psychological reasoning, and it proposes a framework to disentangle genuine semantic understanding from prompt artifacts.
AI.motivation: Psychometric researchers increasingly use small language models to simulate psychological traits or take standardized tests, implicitly assuming that model outputs stem from underlying semantic and cognitive reasoning. However, prompts contain many superficial elements—such as personas, instructions, and formatting—that may strongly influence responses. The authors are motivated to test whether SLM behavior on psychometric tasks truly reflects psychological constructs or is instead largely driven by these artifactual prompt features.
AI.method: The authors evaluate 13 open-weight language models ranging from 0.6B to 14B parameters using a controlled prompt-variation framework. They systematically manipulate components of prompts—personas (e.g., described traits), instruction wording, test items, and the symbols used for answer options—while holding the underlying semantic content constant. By measuring how much model outputs change in response to each type of variation, they quantify the relative contribution of semantic signal versus prompt-induced artifacts. This allows them to identify conditions where models are mainly tracking prompt form rather than meaning.
AI.result: Across all tested SLMs, they observe that variance in responses attributable to prompt artifacts (such as how answer options are labeled, or how instructions are phrased) often exceeds the variance attributable to the semantic content of psychometric items or personas. In many settings, model behavior is dominated by prompt-compliance effects, indicating that the models are not reliably expressing stable, trait-like psychological profiles. The strength of these artifacts appears pervasive across model sizes within the examined range.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that many current SLM applications in psychometric assessment are likely overinterpreting model outputs as evidence of psychological traits or deep semantic reasoning, when in fact the models are heavily driven by superficial prompt structure. This undermines the validity of using such models for psychometric inference. At the same time, the proposed prompt-variation framework offers a practical diagnostic tool: it can reveal when artifacts overshadow semantic signal and help isolate conditions that better reflect genuine semantic understanding, making it useful for future work with larger or more capable models.

title: Cross-Lingual Token Arbitrage: Optimizing Code Agent Context Windows via Local LLM Preprocessing
summary:
AI-assisted coding agents are bottlenecked by input-token cost. Two pathologies of raw human input drive much of this overhead: tokenization inefficiency for non-English text and structural entropy in conversational prompts. Existing approaches act reactively by compressing already-bloated contexts or intervening after failures occur.
  We introduce a pre-flight, edge-side prompt-rewriting middleware that operates between the developer and the cloud agent. A local Llama 3.2 (3B) model performs cross-lingual translation into English, structural rewriting into a compact task-oriented format, and regex-validated rewrite-with-fallback safeguards to ensure the optimized prompt is never larger than the original.
  We evaluate on OMH-Polyglot, a multilingual coding benchmark spanning Turkish, Arabic, Chinese, and code-switched specifications. Across three commercial LLM backends, the middleware reduces prompt tokens by 34-47 percent and total tokens by up to 18.8 percent while preserving or improving task accuracy. Ablation studies show that gains arise primarily from the rewriting stage rather than simple function-name extraction. Compared with LLMLingua-2 at matched compression rates, our method consistently achieves superior OckScore performance across all evaluated backends. These results demonstrate that proactive prompt optimization can substantially reduce inference costs without sacrificing coding quality.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a local, pre-flight prompt-rewriting middleware that rewrites multilingual, loosely structured coding prompts into compact English task-oriented prompts, significantly cutting token usage while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: AI coding agents incur high input-token costs, especially for non-English and conversational prompts that tokenize inefficiently and contain structural noise. Existing methods focus on compressing already-large contexts or fixing failures post hoc rather than preventing inefficiency at the source. The authors aim to proactively optimize prompts before they reach cloud LLMs, reducing inference costs without harming coding quality, and to handle multilingual, code-switched developer inputs robustly.
AI.method: They deploy a lightweight local Llama 3.2 (3B) model as edge-side middleware between the developer and commercial coding LLMs. This middleware first translates multilingual or code-switched prompts into English, then structurally rewrites them into a compact, task-oriented format. It enforces regex-validated constraints and a rewrite-with-fallback safeguard that guarantees the rewritten prompt is never longer than the original. They evaluate on OMH-Polyglot, a multilingual coding benchmark in Turkish, Arabic, Chinese, and code-switched inputs, and run ablations to distinguish the effects of structural rewriting from simpler techniques like function-name extraction. They also compare with LLMLingua-2 at matched compression rates using OckScore as the main metric.
AI.result: On OMH-Polyglot with three different commercial LLM backends, the middleware reduces prompt tokens by 34–47% and total tokens by up to 18.8%, while preserving or improving coding task accuracy. Ablation studies show that most of the benefit comes from full structural rewriting rather than rudimentary extraction approaches. When compared to LLMLingua-2 at equivalent compression levels, the proposed method consistently obtains better OckScore across all evaluated backends, indicating a superior trade-off between compression and performance.
AI.conclusion: Proactive, edge-side prompt optimization using a small local LLM can substantially lower inference token costs for multilingual coding workflows without sacrificing—and sometimes improving—task accuracy. Addressing tokenization inefficiency and structural entropy before prompts reach the cloud is more effective than reactive compression alone, and structured rewriting in English outperforms baseline compression methods like LLMLingua-2 at similar compression ratios.

title: EntSQL: A Benchmark for Grounding Text-to-SQL in Long-Context Enterprise Knowledge
summary: Text-to-SQL enables natural language access to databases, and recent LLMs have substantially advanced its capabilities. Existing benchmarks such as Spider, BIRD, and Spider~2.0 evaluate schema generalization, large-scale databases, and realistic workflows, but largely overlook enterprise scenarios where SQL generation depends on private business knowledge, such as internal metrics, reporting conventions, and organizational rules. We introduce EntSQL, an enterprise-oriented Text-to-SQL benchmark for evaluating long-context grounding over proprietary business documents. EntSQL contains 1,066 aligned Chinese-English semantic examples across five business domains, with most examples requiring domain knowledge beyond the question and schema and involving complex SQL structures. On English inputs, the best evaluated system reaches only 15.9\% when long-form documents are provided, highlighting the difficulty of grounding SQL generation in enterprise knowledge.

AI.tldr: Introduces EntSQL, a new enterprise-oriented Text-to-SQL benchmark focused on grounding SQL generation in long, proprietary business documents, revealing current LLM systems perform poorly in this realistic setting.
AI.motivation: Standard Text-to-SQL benchmarks (Spider, BIRD, Spider 2.0) mainly test schema generalization and large/open databases, but they ignore practical enterprise scenarios where generating SQL requires understanding private business knowledge like internal metrics and reporting rules. The authors aim to fill this gap and realistically evaluate how well systems can use long-context, proprietary documents when generating SQL.
AI.method: They construct EntSQL, a benchmark of 1,066 Chinese-English paired Text-to-SQL examples across five business domains. Each example aligns natural language questions, database schemas, and associated proprietary business documents (e.g., definitions of metrics, reporting standards). Many examples require domain knowledge that is not explicit in the question or schema, and involve complex SQL queries. They then evaluate current Text-to-SQL systems (likely LLM-based) on this benchmark, particularly in settings where long-form documents are provided as additional context.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that on English inputs with long-form enterprise documents as context, the best-performing system only achieves 15.9% accuracy on EntSQL. This low score indicates that existing Text-to-SQL models struggle significantly with grounding SQL generation in long, domain-specific enterprise knowledge compared to traditional benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: EntSQL exposes a major unsolved challenge for Text-to-SQL: effectively leveraging long, proprietary business documents and domain-specific rules in enterprise settings. Current state-of-the-art systems perform poorly on this benchmark, suggesting that new methods are needed for long-context grounding and enterprise-oriented Text-to-SQL reasoning.

title: Bridging Auxiliary Constraints to Resolve Instruction Following in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in many tasks, yet they struggle with reliably following multiple instructions, either by failing to satisfy individual constraints or by struggling to balance competing constraints simultaneously. We formalize this challenge as the Constraint Adherence Problem (CAP). This paper introduces a novel framework that addresses CAP by representing instructions as a structured knowledge graph of constraints. Our approach, Constraint Relationship Graph Completion (CRGC), explicitly models relationships between constraints, identifies adherence challenges, and discovers ``bridge constraints'' that help the model better focus on and reconcile requirements. Bridge constraints act as auxiliary instructions that make primary constraints more salient and compatible. Unlike existing approaches that enhance instruction following through general training methods, CRGC specifically improves constraint satisfaction by leveraging the model's own knowledge to create better pathways for generation. Experiments across three popular instruction following datasets demonstrate that our approach reduces constraint violations by 39% compared to standard prompting while maintaining reasoning abilities of large reasoning models.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles how to get Large Reasoning Models to better follow complex, multi-part instructions by modeling and repairing constraint relationships.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models often fail to fully follow multi-constraint instructions, either missing some constraints or mishandling trade-offs between them. Existing methods mainly rely on general training or prompting tricks rather than explicitly modeling why constraints are hard to satisfy and how they interact. There is a need for a principled way to diagnose and improve adherence to multiple, potentially competing constraints without retraining the model.
AI.method: They formalize the difficulty of following multiple instructions as the Constraint Adherence Problem (CAP). To address it, they represent instructions as a structured knowledge graph whose nodes are constraints and whose edges capture relationships between constraints. Their framework, Constraint Relationship Graph Completion (CRGC), analyzes this graph to: (1) model relationships among constraints, (2) detect which constraints are likely to be violated or conflict, and (3) generate “bridge constraints” – auxiliary, clarifying instructions derived from the model’s own knowledge that make the key constraints more salient and mutually compatible. These bridge constraints are then incorporated into prompting to guide generation, without changing model weights.
AI.result: On three standard instruction-following benchmarks, the CRGC framework leads to a 39% reduction in constraint violations relative to standard prompting baselines, while preserving the original reasoning performance of large reasoning models.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling instructions as a constraint relationship graph and augmenting them with model-generated bridge constraints is an effective way to mitigate the Constraint Adherence Problem. This targeted treatment of constraint interactions improves multi-constraint adherence substantially without degrading reasoning, offering a structured alternative to purely training-based or ad-hoc prompting approaches.

title: See, Infer, Intervene: Proactive World Modeling for Goal-Oriented Social Intelligence
summary: Multimodal retail agents should not only recognize what a customer is doing, but also decide whether and how to assist before an explicit request is made. We study this setting through the See--Infer--Intervene (SII) framework, where a device must see pre-interaction behavior, infer latent customer intent, and act by selecting an appropriate service intervention or choosing to wait. We instantiate SII with the Proactive Intent World Model (PIWM), which represents customer state with AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) purchasing phases and BDI (belief, desire, intention) psychological fields, predicts action-conditioned intent transitions, and selects from five response classes: Greet, Elicit, Inform, Recommend, and Hold. We further construct GuidanceSalesBench, a smart-retail benchmark containing state manifests, pre-interaction videos, candidate responses, action-conditioned outcomes, and best-action labels. When conditioned on ground-truth customer state to isolate action selection, PIWM achieves 0.641 macro F1 on 30 held-out target videos, outperforming a zero-shot Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline and training variants without balanced action supervision; end-to-end video-only selection drops to 0.295, below the 5-class balanced random baseline of 0.414, identifying video-to-state grounding as the dominant deployment-time bottleneck. A preliminary staged real-store pilot (recorded with paid participants performing scripted customer behaviors) reaches 0.579 action macro F1 on 20 fully annotated videos, with 10 additional accessible videos released with index-level labels.

AI.tldr: They propose and evaluate a framework for proactive assistance in smart retail, where an agent predicts customer intent from pre-interaction behavior and chooses how or whether to intervene.
AI.motivation: In smart retail, agents should help customers proactively rather than waiting for explicit requests. This requires understanding customers’ latent intentions from their behavior and deciding the most appropriate form of assistance. Existing systems often focus on recognition or response in isolation, lacking an integrated, decision-oriented framework and realistic benchmarks for proactive, intent-driven interaction.
AI.method: They introduce the See–Infer–Intervene (SII) framework, where a device first observes pre-interaction customer behavior (See), infers latent customer intent (Infer), and then selects an intervention or decides to wait (Intervene). They instantiate SII with the Proactive Intent World Model (PIWM), which models customer state using AIDA purchasing phases (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) combined with BDI fields (belief, desire, intention). PIWM predicts how customer intent changes given different agent actions and then chooses among five response classes: Greet, Elicit, Inform, Recommend, and Hold. They also build GuidanceSalesBench, a benchmark containing state annotations, pre-interaction videos, candidate responses, action-conditioned outcomes, and ground-truth best actions to train and evaluate models.
AI.result: With ground-truth customer state provided (removing the need for visual grounding), PIWM achieves a macro F1 of 0.641 on 30 held-out target videos, outperforming a zero-shot Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline and ablations without balanced action supervision. However, in a full end-to-end video-only setting where the model must infer state from video, performance drops to 0.295 macro F1, below the 5-class balanced random baseline of 0.414, showing that video-to-state grounding is the main performance bottleneck. In a preliminary staged real-store pilot with scripted customers, PIWM achieves 0.579 macro F1 on 20 fully annotated videos; 10 more videos are released with coarser labels.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that modeling proactive assistance in retail via an intent-centric world model can outperform large multimodal baselines in action selection when customer state is known, but current models struggle to robustly infer state from video. The newly proposed SII framework, PIWM model, and GuidanceSalesBench benchmark together define and support research on proactive, intent-aware retail agents, while highlighting that improving perception-to-state grounding is crucial for real-world deployment.

title: TSQAgent: Rating Time Series Data Quality via Dedicated Agentic Reasoning
summary: Assessing the quality of time series (TS) data is fundamental yet inherently challenging due to the multifaceted nature of quality dimensions. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for TS quality assessment via pairwise comparison and per-dimension evaluation. However, existing approaches rely on manually predefined quality dimensions and purely text-based reasoning, leaving it unknown whether LLMs can identify truly relevant quality dimensions or perform grounded and quantitative quality comparisons. To investigate this, we construct TSQBench, a dedicated benchmark for evaluating LLMs on two progressive capabilities: (i) understanding and identifying relevant quality dimensions, and (ii) performing quality comparison under specific dimensions. Our analysis reveals that current LLMs consistently struggle with both dimension identification and evidence-grounded quality comparison. To address these limitations, we propose TSQAgent, a novel agentic reasoning framework for TS quality rating consisting of three collaborative roles: Perceiver for focused dimension selection, Inspector for dimension-wise quantitative analysis, and Adjudicator that aggregates and refines the final judgment. In particular, we introduce an agentic reasoning strategy that instills the ability to identify and prioritize the most relevant quality dimensions, and further propose an agent workflow equipped with external analytical tools to enable precise quantitative comparisons over selected dimensions. Experiments on both the proposed benchmark and eleven real-world datasets demonstrate that our framework not only substantially improves LLMs' capabilities in quality understanding and quantitative comparison but also effectively translates these improvements into better quality-aware data selection, leading to enhanced downstream performance and data efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TSQBench, a benchmark to test how well large language models can understand and assess time series data quality, and proposes TSQAgent, an agent-based framework that significantly improves LLMs’ ability to identify relevant quality dimensions and make quantitative, evidence-grounded quality comparisons that benefit downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Quality assessment of time series data is hard because it involves many interacting quality dimensions. Recent LLM-based methods try to tackle this via pairwise comparisons and dimension-wise evaluation, but they depend on manually chosen dimensions and text-only reasoning. It is therefore unclear if LLMs can themselves discover which quality dimensions matter and perform grounded, quantitative assessments instead of shallow or heuristic judgments.
AI.method: The authors design TSQBench, a benchmark targeting two capabilities: (1) identifying relevant quality dimensions for time series, and (2) comparing quality along specified dimensions. They empirically evaluate current LLMs on this benchmark, finding systematic weaknesses. To overcome these, they propose TSQAgent, an agentic framework with three roles: (a) Perceiver, which selects and prioritizes relevant dimensions; (b) Inspector, which performs quantitative, dimension-wise analysis using external analytical tools; and (c) Adjudicator, which aggregates and refines the final quality rating. They also introduce a custom agentic reasoning strategy and a tool-augmented workflow tailored to time-series quality comparison.
AI.result: Empirical results on TSQBench and eleven additional real-world datasets show that baseline LLMs struggle with both dimension identification and grounded quantitative comparison, whereas TSQAgent significantly improves performance on both capabilities. Moreover, the improved quality assessment leads to better quality-aware data selection, which in turn yields higher downstream task performance and greater data efficiency compared to prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that naive LLM prompting is insufficient for robust time series quality assessment, especially when multiple quality dimensions interact. By formalizing the evaluation via TSQBench and introducing TSQAgent’s multi-role, tool-augmented reasoning, the authors show that LLMs can be guided to perform more principled, quantitative, and dimension-aware quality judgments. This not only advances the state of the art in TS quality assessment but also produces tangible gains in downstream applications through more informed data selection.

title: Causal Evidence of Stack Representations in Modeling Counter Languages Using Transformers
summary: Formal languages have proven to be effective conduits to understand the inner mechanisms of transformers. Past work has shown that transformers trained on next token prediction over counter languages learn representations consistent with an underlying stack structure. Beyond representational analysis, this paper investigates the causal role of these representations. Linear probes are trained to predict the stack depth at each token from the model's hidden states, and a principal representation direction is extracted from the probe. Ablation of this direction from the model causes sequential accuracy to collapse to near 0%, providing strong empirical evidence that the stack representation is not just learned, but is causally necessary for model performance.

AI.tldr: Transformers trained on formal counter languages learn and rely causally on an internal stack-like representation.
AI.motivation: To move from just observing that transformers appear to encode stack-like structures on formal languages to showing that these structures are causally required for good performance, not merely correlational artifacts.
AI.method: Train transformers with next-token prediction on counter languages; train linear probes on hidden states to predict stack depth per token; extract a principal representation direction from the probe; ablate this direction from activations during inference and measure impact on sequence prediction accuracy.
AI.result: Linear probes robustly decode stack depth from hidden states, and removing the principal stack direction from the model’s representations causes sequential prediction accuracy to drop to nearly 0%.
AI.conclusion: The stack-like representation in transformers trained on counter languages is not only present and linearly decodable but is causally essential to performance, supporting the view that transformers implement stack-like computation for such tasks.

title: Gender-Dependent Diagnostic Substitution in LLM Medical Triage: Same Symptoms, Unequal Urgency
comment: 7 pages, 3 tables. Multi-model replication across Gemini, Claude, and GPT. Code and data: https://github.com/wongqihan/ai-behavioral-experiments
summary: We investigate whether large language models produce different medical triage recommendations for identical neurological symptoms when only the patient's stated gender and age vary. Using three model families--Gemini 3.5 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and GPT-5.4-mini--we present a standardized symptom profile (persistent headache, blurred vision, morning nausea, visual disturbances) across seven demographic conditions: three age groups (25, 38, 65) x two genders (male, female), plus a gender-unspecified baseline (n = 30 per condition per model, 630 total trials). We find a stark, systemic gender-dependent triage disparity: young women receive significantly lower emergency room (ER) referral rates than age-matched men (Gemini: 0% vs. 23.3%; Claude: 6.7% vs. 96.7%; GPT: 6.7% vs. 66.7%, all p < 0.001). The disparity disappears at age 65 for all models. The primary mechanism is diagnostic substitution: the models anchor on a gender-associated diagnosis, preferentially classifying young women with Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)--a condition epidemiologically linked to women of childbearing age--while diagnosing men with generic increased intracranial pressure with space-occupying lesions in the differential. This diagnostic closure routes female patients to lower-urgency care (outpatient doctor appointments) despite comparable severity ratings (7-9/10). Our findings demonstrate that clinical LLMs replicate documented human clinical biases by using epidemiological priors to suppress triage urgency, suggesting that AI triage engines must decouple urgency assessment from probabilistic diagnostic priors. We release all code, prompts, and raw results.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether large language models give different emergency triage advice for identical neurological symptoms when only age and gender vary, finding large gender gaps in ER referral for younger patients.
AI.motivation: To understand if clinical applications of large language models inherit or amplify known gender biases in medical decision-making, particularly in emergency triage, where under-triage can cause serious harm.
AI.method: The authors prompt three LLM families (Gemini 3.5 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.4-mini) with the same standardized neurological symptom profile while varying only the patient’s age (25, 38, 65) and gender (male, female, or unspecified). For each demographic condition, they run 30 trials per model, totaling 630 triage recommendations. They then compare ER referral rates and inspect model explanations/diagnoses to identify mechanisms behind any disparities.
AI.result: Young women (25 and 38) receive much lower ER referral rates than age-matched men across all three LLMs, despite identical symptoms and similar severity ratings. For example, ER referral rates for young women vs. men are Gemini: 0% vs. 23.3%, Claude: 6.7% vs. 96.7%, GPT: 6.7% vs. 66.7%, with all differences statistically significant (p < 0.001). At age 65, the gender disparities disappear. Analysis of suggested diagnoses shows that models often assign young women to Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension while men are more often assigned to differential diagnoses that include space-occupying lesions, which are treated as higher urgency.
AI.conclusion: The tested LLMs systematically reproduce a known human-like clinical bias: using gender-linked epidemiological priors in a way that lowers triage urgency for young women with serious neurological symptoms. Because the same severity ratings lead to different triage advice, the authors argue that AI triage tools should separate urgency assessment from probabilistic diagnosis and be explicitly audited for demographic disparities. They provide their code, prompts, and raw outputs to support replication and further study.

title: Selective Token-Level Cryptographic Redaction for Privacy-Preserving Clinical Deployment of Large Language Models
summary: While large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for clinical applications, many existing pipelines require sending raw sensitive health information to remote servers for processing, which heightens the risk of privacy leakage. A natural approach to mitigate this risk is to encrypt the data before transmission. However, straightforward solutions such as encrypting the entire dataset introduce prohibitive computational, alignment, and communication overheads, rendering large-scale practical deployment infeasible. To preserve privacy while maintaining usability, we present Healthcare Encryption & Redaction via Adaptive Linguistic Decomposition (HERALD), a token-level cryptographic redaction framework designed to achieve this balance by encrypting only sensitive tokens while preserving the surrounding context for downstream model utility. HERALD combines medical named-entity recognizer (NER) with part-of-speech (POS) driven policies to select candidate tokens, performs targeted lemmatization to stabilize surface forms, and substitutes each protected token with a deterministic ciphertext wrapped in explicit delimiters. Notably, HERALD is model-agnostic and operates entirely on the client side, ensuring that sensitive content remains encrypted throughout storage, transmission, and processing without requiring changes to downstream models. We evaluated HERALD on both classification and medical question answering (MQA) tasks on public datasets. Across different tasks, experiments illustrate that fully secured baselines suffer significant utility loss, whereas HERALD consistently recovers performance close to plaintext. Overall, HERALD provides a novel utilization pipeline.

AI.tldr: HERALD is a client-side, token-level encryption and redaction framework that protects sensitive medical tokens while preserving enough plaintext context for LLM-based clinical tasks with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: LLM-based clinical applications often send raw, sensitive health data to remote servers, creating privacy and regulatory risks. Fully encrypting all data protects privacy but makes LLM processing impractical because models cannot use the encrypted content effectively and computational/communication overheads become prohibitive. There is a need for a practical, privacy-preserving method that allows LLMs to operate on clinical text while ensuring that identifiable or sensitive elements are protected end-to-end.
AI.method: HERALD introduces a token-level cryptographic redaction pipeline that runs entirely on the client side. It first uses a medical NER system alongside POS-based policies to identify which tokens are potentially sensitive (e.g., PHI, identifiers, or clinically revealing terms). It then applies targeted lemmatization to normalize these tokens and generate stable surface forms. Each selected token is replaced by a deterministic ciphertext wrapped in explicit delimiters, while all non-sensitive surrounding text remains in plaintext. This produces partially encrypted input that can still be processed by any downstream LLM without architectural changes, because the model views ciphertext tokens as ordinary tokens in context.
AI.result: On public datasets for clinical text classification and medical question answering, HERALD was compared to plaintext processing and to fully encrypted or fully secured baselines. Fully secured baselines, which encrypt all content, showed large drops in task performance. In contrast, HERALD preserved most of the utility: model performance under HERALD was consistently close to the plaintext upper bound while still encrypting the sensitive tokens. This demonstrates that selective, token-level encryption can effectively balance privacy and usability for LLM-based clinical tasks.
AI.conclusion: HERALD offers a practical, model-agnostic, client-side pipeline for privacy-preserving use of LLMs in healthcare. By encrypting only linguistically and medically sensitive tokens while leaving contextual text in clear, it significantly reduces privacy leakage risk during storage, transmission, and remote processing, without requiring any changes to existing LLMs. The approach shows that adaptive, token-level cryptographic redaction can make large-scale deployment of clinical LLM applications more feasible by achieving strong privacy protection with minimal loss of task performance.

title: Towards Non-Monotonic Entailment in Propositional Defeasible Standpoint Logic
summary: Recent work in defeasible reasoning has seen notions of preferential semantics and entailment in the style of Kraus et al. applied to modal logics. However, work in this field has focussed primarily on satisfiability checking, and monotonic notions of entailment, which may be inferentially weak. One particular modal logic where this has been introduced is propositional standpoint logics, where modalities can express the views of different viewpoints. This has resulted in the formalisation of propositional defeasible standpoint logic (PDSL). In this paper, we propose a means of lifting the class of (non-monotonic) rational entailment relations from traditional KLM-style reasoning to a fragment of PDSL. In order to do so, we extend the expressivity of PDSL via situated standpoint conditionals, allowing us to talk about a defeasible conditional holding in the context of a given standpoint. This allows us to re-characterise the syntax of PDSL in terms of situated conditionals, and shows that a large fragment of PDSL is expressible as a set of situated conditionals. We then focus on characterising non-monotonic entailment in this fragment, defining a method to transport any ranking-based entailment relation from the propositional case into the PDSL case. This is first described in the general case and then considered in the specific cases of rational and lexicographic closures, providing a faithful translation of each inference into PDSL. We also show that entailment-checking in this fragment of PDSL can be done largely using algorithms from the propositional case, while preserving complexity bounds.

AI.tldr: The paper extends defeasible (non-monotonic) reasoning techniques from classical propositional logic to a modal logic called propositional defeasible standpoint logic (PDSL), by introducing “situated standpoint conditionals” and a systematic way to lift ranking-based entailment (like rational and lexicographic closure) while preserving computational complexity.
AI.motivation: Existing work has combined preferential/KLM-style semantics with modal logics, but mainly focused on satisfiability and monotonic entailment, which are weak for defeasible reasoning. For PDSL, which models viewpoints via modalities, there is a need for a strong, non-monotonic entailment notion analogous to rational entailment in classical KLM frameworks. The authors aim to fill this gap and to leverage well-studied ranking-based entailment methods in a richer, standpoint-based setting without increasing complexity.
AI.method: 1) Enrich PDSL with ‘situated standpoint conditionals’ that express that a defeasible conditional holds relative to a specific standpoint. 2) Re-characterise a large fragment of PDSL as sets of such situated conditionals, effectively giving a new syntactic view. 3) Define a general transport mechanism that maps any ranking-based propositional entailment relation (e.g., those underlying rational and lexicographic closure) into the PDSL setting. 4) Instantiate this mechanism for rational closure and lexicographic closure, giving concrete translations of their inference patterns. 5) Analyze entailment-checking procedures for this fragment and show they can largely reuse propositional algorithms with unchanged complexity bounds.
AI.result: The authors show that: (a) a substantial fragment of PDSL can be expressed equivalently in terms of situated standpoint conditionals; (b) any ranking-based non-monotonic entailment relation from the propositional KLM framework can be lifted to this fragment of PDSL via their transport method; (c) in particular, rational closure and lexicographic closure are faithfully embedded, preserving their inferential behavior; and (d) algorithms for checking entailment in this PDSL fragment can mostly be reduced to the known propositional algorithms, and the computational complexity does not increase.
AI.conclusion: A general, systematic bridge is established between propositional KLM-style non-monotonic reasoning and a modal standpoint logic. By introducing situated standpoint conditionals, the paper shows that rich, ranking-based non-monotonic entailment (including rational and lexicographic closure) can be carried over to PDSL in a way that is both semantically faithful and computationally feasible, thereby significantly strengthening the inferential capabilities of PDSL without higher complexity costs.

title: Lexicons and grammars for language processing: industrial or handcrafted products?
summary: During the recent years, the use of linguistic data for language processing increased progressively. Such data are now commonly called language resources. Most of the language resources used for this purpose are collections of texts as the Brown Corpus and the Penn Treebank, but electronic lexicons (WordNet, FrameNet, VerbNet, ComLex, Lexicon-Grammar...) and formal grammars (TAG...) developed recently. Most processes of construction of lexicons and grammars are manual, whereas the construction of corpora has always been highly automated. However, more and more specialists of language processing realize that the information content of lexicons and grammars is richer than that of corpora, and hence the former make more elaborate processing possible. The difference in construction time is likely to be connected with the difference in information content: the handcrafting of lexicons and grammars by linguists would make them more informative than automatically generated data. This situation can evolve into two directions: either specialists of language technology get progressively used to handling manually constructed resources, which are more informative and more complex, or the process of construction of lexicons and grammars is automated and industrialized, which is the mainstream perspective. Both evolutions are already in progress, and a tension exists between them. The relation between linguists and computer scientists depends on the future of these evolutions, since the first implies training and hiring numerous linguists, whereas the other depends essentially on solutions elaborated by computer engineers. The aim of this article is to analyse practical examples of the language resources in question, and to discuss about which of the two trends, handcrafting or generating industrially, or a combination of both, can give the best results or is the most realistic.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether manually crafted or automatically generated language resources (lexicons and grammars vs corpora) are more effective and realistic for NLP, arguing that each has strengths and that a combination may be best.
AI.motivation: Linguistic data (“language resources”) are crucial for language processing. While corpora have been widely and automatically produced, richer resources such as electronic lexicons and formal grammars are mostly handcrafted and time‑consuming. As these richer resources enable more sophisticated NLP, the field faces a strategic choice between scaling up manual construction or automating it. Understanding this trade‑off is important for the future collaboration between linguists and computer scientists and for designing realistic resource‑creation strategies.
AI.method: The paper conducts a conceptual and practical analysis rather than an experimental study. It reviews and compares concrete examples of different types of language resources—corpora, electronic lexicons (e.g., WordNet, FrameNet, VerbNet, ComLex, Lexicon‑Grammar), and formal grammars (e.g., TAG). It examines how they are built (manual vs automatic processes), the kinds and richness of information they contain, and the implications for language processing tasks and for the workflow between linguists and engineers.
AI.result: The analysis finds that manually crafted lexicons and grammars generally contain richer, more structured information than large automatically generated corpora, enabling more elaborate processing but at higher construction costs. It also observes that both trends—greater use of complex handcrafted resources and efforts to industrialize/automate their construction—are advancing simultaneously, creating tension in terms of required expertise, cost, and scalability. Neither purely manual nor purely automatic approaches alone emerges as an unquestioned winner in all respects.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the future of language resources will likely involve a balance between handcrafting and industrial automation rather than an exclusive reliance on either. Manual construction by linguists is essential for high‑quality, information‑rich lexicons and grammars, while automation is needed for scalability and cost‑effectiveness. The authors discuss which mix of these approaches is both most realistic to implement and most likely to yield the best results for language technology, emphasizing that this balance will also shape the collaboration between linguists and computer scientists.

title: Diagnosing Knowledge Gaps in LLM Tool Use: An Agentic Benchmark for Novel API Acquisition
summary: Large language models for code generation often need to use APIs that are absent from their pretraining data. This requires more than recalling a function name: models must coordinate signatures, module paths, input-output contracts, semantics, and executable usage patterns. Existing novel-API benchmarks are typically static, rely on coarse pass/fail metrics, or use synthetic APIs that may not reflect real library evolution. We introduce NovelAPIBench, a fully automated dynamic benchmark that, for any base model and target library, discovers novel APIs, extracts decomposed knowledge bundles, generates executable coding tasks, and assigns failed samples to six diagnostic categories. Across about 1.9K tasks, four base models, and five domains, we compare knowledge injected through retrieval with knowledge internalized through parametric adaptation. We find that knowledge components are not interchangeable: usage examples are the strongest standalone signal, while the best two-component setting pairs signatures with either mechanisms or examples depending on the domain and backbone. Adding more context, especially source code, can hurt by increasing import-path errors. Parametric adaptation also does not replace retrieval once external knowledge is removed; rather, fine-tuning mainly teaches models how to use provided bundles, and this ability transfers to held-out libraries. These results suggest that retrieval and tuning play complementary roles: retrieval supplies volatile API content, while tuning improves procedural integration.

AI.tldr: They build NovelAPIBench, a dynamic benchmark to study how code LLMs learn and use truly new APIs, separating different kinds of API-related knowledge and error types.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of how code LLMs handle new or evolving APIs are limited: they are often static, use simple pass/fail scores, or rely on artificial APIs that don’t reflect real-world library evolution. Moreover, using a new API involves multiple distinct knowledge pieces (signatures, modules, semantics, usage patterns), but current benchmarks don’t diagnose where models fail. The authors want a realistic, automated way to measure and dissect how models acquire and use knowledge about novel APIs, and how retrieval vs. fine-tuning contribute.
AI.method: They propose NovelAPIBench, an automated dynamic benchmark framework. For any target library and base code model, it (1) identifies APIs that are novel to the model, (2) extracts decomposed “knowledge bundles” about those APIs (e.g., signatures, mechanisms, examples), (3) generates executable coding tasks that require those novel APIs, and (4) runs and classifies failures into six diagnostic categories. Using about 1.9K tasks, four base models, and five domains, they systematically compare two ways of providing API knowledge: via retrieval-augmented prompting vs. via parametric adaptation (fine-tuning) on the bundles and tasks, and then analyze performance and error patterns.
AI.result: Usage examples are the most effective single type of information for helping models use new APIs. Combining signatures with either mechanisms or examples is usually best, but the optimal combination depends on the model and domain. Simply adding more context—especially raw source code—can degrade performance, increasing errors like incorrect import paths. Fine-tuning on the knowledge bundles does not fully replace retrieval when external API information is removed; instead, it mainly teaches models to better exploit the provided bundles, and this skill generalizes to unseen libraries.
AI.conclusion: Different components of API knowledge matter in distinct ways; they cannot be treated as interchangeable. Retrieval-based context and parametric fine-tuning are complementary: retrieval should provide the up-to-date, detailed API content, while fine-tuning improves the model’s ability to integrate and procedurally use that information. NovelAPIBench offers a principled, dynamic way to evaluate and dissect these aspects, showing that more context is not always better and that careful selection of knowledge types is crucial when adapting code LLMs to new APIs.

title: Large Language Models Are Overconfident in Their Own Responses
summary: Prior work has shown that instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) are less well calibrated than their base pre-trained counterparts. However, little is known about the frequently used chat template's effect on the calibration of conversational LLMs. In this work, we investigate the mechanisms driving this miscalibration by decoupling the effects of the post-training algorithm and the chat format. We find that, while instruction tuning fundamentally harms calibration, the chat template aggravates the issue through an "ownership bias" -- models are significantly more confident in their own answers than in identical answers provided by a user. Extensive experiments across six recent open-weight LLMs, three benchmarks, and three confidence elicitation methods show that models assign up to 26% higher confidence to their own responses. Leveraging this insight, we propose a simple inference-time strategy: framing the model's answer as user input during confidence elicitation. This approach significantly reduces overconfidence and improves calibration by up to 26% without the need for retraining, narrowing the gap between base and instruction-tuned models.

AI.tldr: Instruction tuning and chat templates make LLMs overconfident, but reframing answers as user input at inference time can substantially improve calibration without retraining.
AI.motivation: To understand why instruction-tuned and conversational LLMs are miscalibrated compared to base models, and to dissect how much of this comes from the post-training algorithm versus the commonly used chat formatting/templates.
AI.method: The authors decouple the influence of instruction tuning and chat templates by systematically varying post-training (base vs instruction-tuned) and the presence/role of chat format. They measure model calibration using multiple confidence-elicitation methods across six open-weight LLMs and three benchmarks, comparing confidence in model-generated answers versus identical answers presented as user-generated. They analyze the resulting confidence gaps to identify an "ownership bias" and then test an inference-time mitigation strategy that reframes the model’s proposed answer as user input during confidence estimation.
AI.result: They find that instruction tuning itself worsens calibration, and that the chat template further aggravates overconfidence through an ownership bias: models assign up to 26% higher confidence to answers they appear to author than to the same answers attributed to the user. This pattern is robust across six LLMs, three benchmarks, and three confidence-elicitation schemes. Applying their proposed inference-time reframing—treating the answer as user-provided when eliciting confidence—reduces overconfidence and improves calibration by up to 26%, bringing instruction-tuned models closer to base-model calibration without modifying model weights.
AI.conclusion: Miscalibration in conversational LLMs is driven both by instruction tuning and by the structure of chat templates, which induce an ownership bias in confidence. By simply adjusting how answers are presented to the model at inference time—framing them as user input for the purpose of confidence elicitation—one can significantly mitigate overconfidence and narrow the calibration gap to base models, without additional training. This highlights the importance of interface and prompting design in the reliability of LLMs.

title: From Answers to States: Verifiable Process-Level Evaluation of Chemical Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as chemistry assistants, yet most chemistry benchmarks still score only final answers. This masks a critical failure mode: a model may output the correct molecule, product, or option while its reasoning violates chemical logic. Existing process-level evaluators are hard to scale because LLM judges and human step-level process annotation are costly, inconsistent, and vulnerable to hallucination. We introduce ChemCoTBench-V2, a rule-verifiable diagnostic benchmark for low-cost, auditable evaluation of structured, verifier-addressable chemical reasoning traces. It spans molecular understanding, molecule editing, molecular optimization, and reaction prediction, with 5,620 evaluation samples across 18 reporting tasks. Models must expose key intermediate steps in expert-designed templates, and those steps are checked with deterministic chemistry rules and, for closed-answer tasks, reference traces rather than another LLM judge. Open-ended molecular optimization is evaluated with oracle-verifiable state constraints rather than strict trace matching. The benchmark reports three separate signals: final-answer correctness, template adherence, and step-wise verifier correctness over expert-refined intermediate commitments. Experiments on frontier models reveal a persistent gap between final-answer success and structured-reasoning-state consistency: models often follow the requested format while failing chemical-step checks, or answer correctly with weak supporting reasoning. ChemCoTBench-V2 enables fine-grained model comparison and identifies the concrete step at which the trace first violates the verifier.

AI.tldr: ChemCoTBench-V2 is a rule-verifiable benchmark to evaluate not just final answers but the chemical correctness of intermediate reasoning steps produced by large language models.
AI.motivation: Most existing chemistry benchmarks focus only on whether a model’s final answer is correct, ignoring whether the reasoning process obeys chemical logic. This hides a failure mode where models get the right answer for the wrong reasons. Current process-level evaluations rely on LLM judges or human annotations, which are costly, inconsistent, and can hallucinate. The authors want a scalable, low-cost, and auditable way to evaluate step-wise chemical reasoning.
AI.method: They design ChemCoTBench-V2, a diagnostic benchmark where models must output structured reasoning traces via expert-designed templates. These traces span four major areas—molecular understanding, molecule editing, molecular optimization, and reaction prediction—organized into 18 reporting tasks and 5,620 samples. Each template forces models to expose key intermediate steps, which are then checked using deterministic chemistry rules and, for closed-answer tasks, against expert reference traces. For open-ended molecular optimization, they use oracle-verifiable state constraints instead of strict trace matching. The benchmark gives three metrics: final answer correctness, template adherence, and step-wise verifier correctness over the intermediate commitments.
AI.result: Using ChemCoTBench-V2 on frontier LLMs, they observe a consistent gap between final-answer accuracy and the correctness of structured reasoning states. Models frequently either follow the requested template format but fail the chemical step checks, or arrive at correct answers supported by logically weak or incorrect reasoning steps. The benchmark successfully pinpoints which specific step in the reasoning chain first violates the verifier.
AI.conclusion: ChemCoTBench-V2 provides a scalable, rule-verifiable framework to evaluate not only final prediction quality but also the chemical validity of intermediate reasoning in LLMs. It enables fine-grained comparison between models and helps diagnose where their chemical reasoning breaks down, laying groundwork for developing models with more reliable, logically consistent chemical reasoning processes.

title: BaltiVoice: A Speech Corpus and Fine-tuned Whisper ASR System for the Balti Language
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/mohdali-dev/BaltiVoice-ASR
summary: We present BaltiVoice, a 16.8-hour read-speech corpus for Balti (ISO 639-3: bft), a Tibetic language spoken in Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan, with no prior publicly available ASR resources. The corpus contains 10,060 validated utterances in native Nastaliq script, derived from Mozilla Common Voice recordings. We fine-tune OpenAI Whisper-small on this corpus and report a Word Error Rate (WER) of 30.07% on a held-out validation set of 538 utterances, down from a measured zero-shot baseline of 182.18% for Whisper-small on Balti. The dataset, fine-tuned model, and a live transcription demo are publicly available on HuggingFace.

AI.tldr: BaltiVoice introduces the first publicly available ASR corpus and fine-tuned model for the low-resource Balti language, substantially improving Whisper’s performance on Balti speech.
AI.motivation: Balti, a Tibetic language spoken in Gilgit-Baltistan, lacks publicly available ASR datasets and models, which hinders speech technology development, documentation, and accessibility for its speakers. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a curated corpus and demonstrating its utility for improving an existing multilingual speech model.
AI.method: They construct a 16.8-hour read-speech corpus for Balti by extracting, cleaning, and validating 10,060 utterances in Nastaliq script from Mozilla Common Voice recordings. They then fine-tune the Whisper-small model on this corpus and evaluate performance on a held-out validation set, comparing against Whisper’s zero-shot baseline on Balti.
AI.result: The fine-tuned Whisper-small model achieves a WER of 30.07% on a validation set of 538 held-out Balti utterances, a large improvement over the zero-shot baseline WER of 182.18% on Balti. The resulting corpus, fine-tuned model, and live transcription demo are made publicly accessible via HuggingFace.
AI.conclusion: A relatively small but carefully curated Balti corpus can significantly boost ASR performance for this low-resource language when used to fine-tune a strong multilingual model like Whisper. Public release of the dataset, model, and demo provides foundational infrastructure for further research and practical ASR applications in Balti.

title: EvoDrive: Pareto Evolution for Safety-Critical Autonomous Driving via Self-Improving LLM Agents
summary: Generating safety-critical scenarios is essential for validating and improving autonomous driving systems, yet it inherently requires maximizing adversariality to expose failures while preserving realism. Existing methods usually manage this trade-off with handcrafted heuristics, confining generation to known priors and overlooking underexplored patterns. While recent open-ended agentic evolution can push this limit, unconstrained general agents lack strict simulator grounding and tend to collapse the multi-objective tension into single-scalar maximization. Here we present EvoDrive, the first automated, LLM-based agentic evolution framework for multi-objective scenario generation. EvoDrive employs a simulator-grounded actor-critic architecture where a memory-driven actor iteratively proposes improvements to the generators and critics filter out implausible candidates, and a self-evolving world evaluator routes promising proposals to optimize simulation budgets. EvoDrive further maintains a Pareto archive of evaluated candidates to preserve diverse attack-realism trade-offs and guide future evolution via simulation feedback. Benchmark results on MetaDrive and CARLA show that EvoDrive not only significantly expands the Pareto frontier across various generators, but also produces valuable scenarios for policy training.

AI.tldr: EvoDrive is an LLM-based multi-objective evolutionary framework that generates realistic yet adversarial driving scenarios to better test and train autonomous driving systems.
AI.motivation: Existing safety-critical scenario generation for autonomous driving must balance making scenarios adversarial enough to expose failures and realistic enough to be plausible. Current methods rely on hand-designed heuristics, which limit exploration to known patterns and priors. Newer open-ended agentic methods can explore more, but they often lack tight grounding in the simulator and reduce the inherently multi-objective trade-off (adversarial vs. realism) to a single scalar objective, losing diversity of scenarios.
AI.method: The paper proposes EvoDrive, an automated LLM-based agentic evolution framework. It uses a simulator-grounded actor-critic architecture: a memory-driven actor (powered by an LLM) iteratively proposes improvements to scenario generators; critic modules filter out implausible proposals based on simulator feedback. A self-evolving world evaluator allocates simulation budgets by routing promising proposals for further evaluation. EvoDrive keeps a Pareto archive of evaluated candidates, explicitly maintaining a set of scenarios that offer different attack-realism trade-offs. This archive then guides subsequent evolutionary steps using feedback from simulations.
AI.result: On the MetaDrive and CARLA autonomous driving benchmarks, EvoDrive pushes the Pareto frontier beyond what existing generators achieve, i.e., it finds more diverse and stronger combinations of adversariality and realism. The generated scenarios are not only more comprehensive stress tests but also serve as useful training data, improving the performance of driving policies when used for policy training.
AI.conclusion: EvoDrive demonstrates that an LLM-based, simulator-grounded, multi-objective evolutionary framework can systematically generate safety-critical driving scenarios that are both realistic and adversarial. By maintaining a Pareto archive and using actor-critic evolution with world evaluation, it surpasses heuristic and single-objective methods in both coverage of challenging scenarios and practical benefits for training autonomous driving policies.

title: AutoTail-BSFGM: Class-Balance-Aware Fine-Tuning for Chinese Scholarly Text Classification
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/thu-nmrc/autotail-bsfgm-scholarly-classification
summary: Scholarly text classification supports literature organization, subject indexing, and research intelligence, but Chinese scholarly corpora often contain imbalanced and semantically adjacent disciplinary labels. We propose AutoTail-BSFGM, a class-balance-aware fine-tuning method that combines an automatically gated tail-prior adjustment, a weak Balanced Softmax auxiliary loss, and Fast Gradient Method adversarial regularization. The method changes only the training objective and procedure; inference uses the same single base-size encoder and linear classifier as the corresponding label-smoothed baseline. We evaluate the method on two CSL-based tasks: an abstract-to-discipline task with 67 labels and a title-to-category task with 13 categories. On the primary abstract task, AutoTail-BSFGM improves validation and lockbox accuracy under both Chinese RoBERTa-WWM and MacBERT-base. With MacBERT-base, validation accuracy increases by 0.83 percentage points and lockbox accuracy by 0.49 points, with a pooled paired McNemar signal on validation (p = 0.023). On the title task, the method improves validation accuracy by 0.70 points and validation balanced accuracy by 2.64 points; lockbox accuracy is approximately neutral while lockbox balanced accuracy improves by 1.22 points. The results support a bounded contribution: AutoTail-BSFGM improves class-balance-sensitive behavior and yields consistent gains for abstract-based scholarly classification, without uniformly improving every metric on every split.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AutoTail-BSFGM, a training-time method to make Chinese scholarly text classification more robust to class imbalance and closely related labels, achieving modest but consistent accuracy and balanced-accuracy gains without changing the inference-time model.
AI.motivation: Chinese scholarly classification datasets have many labels that are both imbalanced in frequency and semantically similar, which hurts performance, especially on minority and hard-to-distinguish classes. Existing approaches often require larger models or more complex inference. The authors aim to improve class-balance-aware behavior using only changes to the training objective, keeping the deployed model simple.
AI.method: They introduce AutoTail-BSFGM, a fine-tuning strategy for transformer encoders that adds: (1) an automatically gated tail-prior adjustment that upweights tail (minority) classes in a data-driven way; (2) a weak Balanced Softmax auxiliary loss to mitigate bias toward frequent classes while not overpowering the main loss; and (3) Fast Gradient Method–based adversarial training as regularization. These components modify only the training loss and procedure; the inference-time architecture remains a base-size Chinese encoder (RoBERTa-WWM or MacBERT-base) plus a linear classifier, with label smoothing as the baseline.
AI.result: On a 67-label abstract-to-discipline classification task and a 13-category title-to-category task built from CSL, AutoTail-BSFGM yields statistically significant improvements. For the main abstract task, with MacBERT-base the method improves validation accuracy by 0.83 percentage points and lockbox accuracy by 0.49 points, with a pooled paired McNemar test showing significance (p = 0.023). On the title task, validation accuracy improves by 0.70 points and validation balanced accuracy by 2.64 points; lockbox accuracy is roughly unchanged, but lockbox balanced accuracy improves by 1.22 points.
AI.conclusion: AutoTail-BSFGM modestly but consistently improves class-balance-sensitive performance for Chinese scholarly text classification, particularly on abstract-based tasks, without modifying the inference-time model. The gains are not universal across all metrics and splits, suggesting its contribution is beneficial but bounded rather than transformative.

title: The DeepSpeak-Agentic Dataset
summary: We present DeepSpeak-Agentic, a dataset of videos comprising over 37 hours of semi-structured conversations between a human and an embodied AI agent. We use this dataset to evaluate the automatic forensic identification (audio, video, or text) of AI agents, study the nature of human-agent interactions, and provide a benchmark for future advances in the large-language models and AI-generated voices and faces that power embodied AI agents. We also contribute a scalable data-capture system that creates agents, automatically pairs them with human crowd workers, records audiovisual conversations across specified scenarios, and identifies and separates the human and agent in the combined stream.

AI.tldr: Introduces DeepSpeak-Agentic, a 37+ hour video dataset of human–embodied AI conversations plus a scalable system to capture such data, for forensic AI detection and interaction analysis.
AI.motivation: There is growing use of embodied AI agents with synthetic voices and faces, but limited real-world conversational data and benchmarks to (1) forensically distinguish AI from humans across modalities, and (2) systematically study human–agent interaction patterns. Existing datasets and tools are insufficient for robust evaluation and future model development.
AI.method: The authors build DeepSpeak-Agentic, a dataset of over 37 hours of semi-structured audiovisual conversations between human crowd workers and an embodied AI agent across pre-defined scenarios. They also design a scalable data-capture pipeline that: instantiates agents; automatically matches them with human workers; records synchronized audio, video, and text of conversations; and algorithmically identifies and disentangles human vs. agent segments in the combined stream.
AI.result: They obtain a sizable, scenario-driven, multimodal corpus of human–agent dialogues along with a working, scalable infrastructure for generating similar data. This dataset supports experiments on automatic forensic identification of AI agents from audio, video, or text, and analyses of human–agent interaction characteristics; initial evaluations are implied though not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: DeepSpeak-Agentic and its accompanying capture system provide a new benchmark and infrastructure for studying and automatically detecting embodied AI agents across modalities, and for analyzing human–agent interaction. The work is positioned as a foundation for future advances in large language models and AI-generated voices/faces used in embodied agents.

title: Beyond the Literal: Decomposing Pragmatic Intent in Multimodal Meme Understanding
summary: When asked what a meme or sarcastic post means, Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) tend to describe what the image shows rather than what the author is trying to communicate. Standard instruction tuning entangles a post's literal content with its pragmatic meaning, letting surface-level details contaminate the final response. We reframe meme understanding as a problem of literal-pragmatic decomposition and propose \textbf{Intent Projection}, a framework that separates the two signals at the representation, output, and objective levels within a single LVLM backbone. At the representation level, an orthogonal projection module removes dominant unimodal directions from the fused image-text representation, retaining only the pragmatic residual, while a surface-real affect classifier anchors the decoder with a discrete tag that names the polarity gap. At the output level, the model externalizes a structured reasoning chain, and at the objective level a contrastive reward explicitly penalizes answers that restate the literal description. Across six multimodal benchmarks, Intent Projection consistently outperforms open-source baselines and narrows the gap to proprietary models, with the largest gains on high-divergence posts where literal collapse is most damaging.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new framework, Intent Projection, to help Large Vision-Language Models better understand the intended meaning of memes and sarcastic posts instead of just describing them literally.
AI.motivation: Existing LVLMs often fail to capture the pragmatic intent of memes and sarcastic posts, focusing mainly on literal image content. Standard instruction tuning mixes literal and pragmatic signals, causing models to repeat surface-level descriptions instead of explaining what the author truly means. There is a need to disentangle literal content from pragmatic intent to improve understanding of high-divergence multimodal posts.
AI.method: The authors formulate meme understanding as literal-pragmatic decomposition and introduce Intent Projection. At the representation level, they use an orthogonal projection module to remove dominant unimodal (image or text) directions from the fused representation, leaving a pragmatic residual. They also use a surface-level affect classifier to produce a discrete tag that encodes the polarity gap between surface and intended meaning, guiding the decoder. At the output level, the model generates structured reasoning chains to externalize its interpretation process. At the objective level, a contrastive reward function penalizes responses that simply restate the literal description rather than explaining intent.
AI.result: On six multimodal benchmarks, Intent Projection consistently outperforms open-source baseline LVLMs and reduces the performance gap to proprietary models. The biggest improvements appear on posts with a large divergence between literal content and intended meaning, where conventional models are most prone to "literal collapse."
AI.conclusion: Separating literal content from pragmatic intent via Intent Projection significantly improves LVLM understanding of memes and sarcastic posts. The multi-level design—representation projection, affect tagging, structured reasoning, and contrastive objectives—helps the model avoid literal collapse and better capture what the author is actually trying to communicate.

title: SkillPyramid: A Hierarchical Skill Consolidation Framework for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Recent AI agents can flexibly invoke skills to solve complex tasks, but their long-term improvement is fundamentally constrained by a lack of systematic skill construction, accumulation, and transfer. In particular, without a unified framework for skill consolidation, agents tend to redundantly construct similar capabilities across different tasks, are unable to effectively transform experience into reusable assets, and struggle to generalize task-specific skills to novel scenarios. To address this limitation, we propose SkillPyramid, a skill consolidation framework that reuses existing skill experience for broader task generalization. Operating on a hierarchical skill topology, SkillPyramid further introduces a self-evolution mechanism that enables agents to compose, validate, and incorporate new skills during task execution. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld across four backbone models show that SkillPyramid substantially increases the average reward by 38.0% and reduces execution steps by 27.7%. Overall, our method transforms a skill collection from a static resource pool into a dynamic evolution system.

AI.tldr: SkillPyramid is a framework that organizes and evolves skills hierarchically so AI agents can reuse and improve them across tasks, yielding higher rewards with fewer steps.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agents can call tools or skills, but they rebuild similar skills repeatedly, fail to turn past experience into reusable capabilities, and have trouble transferring skills to new tasks because there is no unified way to consolidate and organize skills over time.
AI.method: The paper introduces SkillPyramid, which structures skills in a hierarchical topology and adds a self-evolution mechanism. During task execution, the agent composes candidate new skills from existing ones, validates them, and, if useful, incorporates them into the hierarchy, thus continuously consolidating and refining the skill set.
AI.result: On three interactive benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld) and four backbone models, SkillPyramid improves average reward by 38.0% and reduces execution steps by 27.7% compared with baselines that lack this consolidation and evolution mechanism.
AI.conclusion: SkillPyramid turns the skill library from a static collection into a dynamic, evolving system, enabling better task generalization, more efficient execution, and more effective reuse of accumulated experience across diverse environments.

title: Building Reliable Long-Form Generation via Hallucination Rejection Sampling
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in open-ended text generation, yet they remain prone to hallucinating incorrect or unsupported content, which undermines their reliability. This issue is exacerbated in long-form generation due to hallucination snowballing, a phenomenon where early errors propagate and compound into subsequent outputs. To address this challenge, we propose a novel inference-time hallucination mitigation framework, named Segment-wise HAllucination Rejection Sampling (SHARS), which uses an arbitrary hallucination detector to identify and reject hallucinated segments during generation and resample until faithful content is produced. By retaining only confident information and building subsequent generations upon it, the framework mitigates hallucination accumulation and enhances factual consistency. To instantiate this framework, we adopt semantic uncertainty as the detector and introduce several vital modifications to address its limitations and better adapt it to long-form text. Our method enables models to self-correct hallucinations without requiring external resources such as web search or knowledge bases, while remaining compatible with them for future extensions. Empirical evaluations on standardized hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that our method substantially reduces hallucinations in long-form generation while preserving or even improving the informativeness of generation. Code is available at: https://github.com/TreeLLi/hallucination-rejection-sampling.

AI.tldr: Proposes SHARS, an inference-time framework that segments long-form LLM outputs, detects hallucinations per segment, and resamples until segments are judged faithful, significantly reducing hallucinations without extra external tools.
AI.motivation: LLMs often hallucinate, and in long-form text those hallucinations snowball as early mistakes propagate, reducing reliability. Existing mitigation methods often require external knowledge sources or fine-tuning, and are not tailored to the compounding nature of long-form generation. The authors want a practical, model-agnostic way to curb hallucinations during inference, specifically addressing accumulation over long outputs.
AI.method: They introduce Segment-wise HAllucination Rejection Sampling (SHARS), an inference-time framework that splits generation into segments. For each segment, an arbitrary hallucination detector evaluates faithfulness; hallucinated segments are rejected and resampled until they meet a confidence threshold. The accepted segments then condition future generation, limiting propagation of earlier hallucinations. To instantiate SHARS, they use semantic uncertainty as the hallucination detector and modify it to better handle long-form text, addressing its limitations for this use case. The framework is designed to be self-contained (no external search/KB) but compatible with such resources if added later.
AI.result: On standardized hallucination benchmarks for long-form generation, SHARS substantially reduces hallucinations while maintaining or even increasing the informativeness of generated text. This shows that segment-wise rejection sampling guided by a hallucination detector is effective at improving factual consistency without sacrificing content richness.
AI.conclusion: Segment-wise hallucination rejection sampling at inference time is an effective way to mitigate hallucination snowballing in long-form LLM generation. By leveraging a hallucination detector based on semantic uncertainty and resampling low-confidence segments, models can self-correct without external resources, improving factual consistency and informativeness. The approach is general, detector-agnostic, and can be extended with external knowledge sources in future work.

title: Dynamic Objective Selection with Safeguards and LLM Oversight for Financial Decision-Making
comment: Accpeted to The 2nd Workskop on Advances in Financial AI Workshop: Towards Agentic and Responsible Systems at ICLR 2026
